#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition (1.5 marks):**

A hazard is any source, situation, or act with the potential to cause harm. It is *intrinsic* to the source — independent of probability or consequence.

**Classification (5.5 marks):**

Hazards are classified into the following categories:

1. **Physical Hazards (0.7 mark + 0.3 example):** Noise, vibration, radiation, temperature extremes, mechanical impact.
   *Example:* Prolonged exposure to >85 dB noise from a stamping machine.

2. **Chemical Hazards (0.7 mark + 0.3 example):** Toxic, corrosive, flammable, reactive substances.
   *Example:* Benzene exposure causing leukemia.

3. **Biological Hazards (0.5 mark + 0.3 example):** Bacteria, viruses, fungi.
   *Example:* Hospital workers exposed to TB patients.

4. **Ergonomic Hazards (0.5 mark + 0.3 example):** Repetitive motion, manual handling.
   *Example:* Assembly-line workers developing carpal tunnel syndrome.

5. **Psychosocial Hazards (0.5 mark + 0.3 example):** Stress, violence, fatigue.
   *Example:* Healthcare workers experiencing burnout.

6. **Environmental Hazards (0.5 mark + 0.3 example):** Natural disasters, climate.
   *Example:* Workers at coastal facilities during cyclones.

7. **Fire/Explosion Hazards (0.4 mark + 0.2 example):** Combustible dust, flammable vapors.
   *Example:* Wheat flour dust explosion (2014, Shanghai).

8. **Electrical Hazards (0.4 mark + 0.2 example):** Live wires, static discharge.
   *Example:* Arc flash in switchgear maintenance.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**The Hazard-Risk-Exposure-Consequence Model:**

The fundamental equation is:

$$
R = P \times C
$$

When **exposure** is included (more realistic for occupational scenarios):

$$
R = P \times C \times E
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **Hazard ($H$)** is identified — a source with potential to cause harm. (1 mark)
2. **Probability ($P$)** is the likelihood that the hazard will manifest into an incident. (1 mark)
3. **Consequence ($C$)** is the magnitude of harm if the hazard realizes. (1 mark)
4. **Exposure ($E$)** quantifies how many persons/assets are exposed to the hazard over time. (1 mark)
5. The product $P \times C \times E$ gives the **risk score** ($R$). (1 mark)
6. **Aggregate risk** for multiple hazards:

$$
R_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i C_i E_i
$$

(1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for explaining the sum and its limitations).

**Diagram Reference:** A clear flowchart showing **Hazard → Trigger → Incident → Exposure → Harm → Risk Score** is highly recommended.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Past Year Pattern]`

**(a)** What is a hazard register? Explain its contents and importance in safety management. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the hazard identification techniques commonly used in industries with at least five methods. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

A **hazard register** (also called a hazard inventory or risk register) is a structured document that records all identified hazards in a workplace, along with their characteristics, associated risks, control measures, and responsible persons.

**Key Contents of a Hazard Register (each worth 0.7 mark, total 5 marks):**

1. **Hazard ID** — Unique identifier (e.g., H-001)
2. **Hazard Description** — Nature and source
3. **Location** — Where the hazard exists
4. **Affected Personnel** — Who is exposed
5. **Probability** — Likelihood rating (1-5)
6. **Consequence** — Severity rating (1-5)
7. **Risk Score** — Calculated $R = P \times C$
8. **Control Measures** — Existing and proposed
9. **Residual Risk** — Risk after controls
10. **Responsible Person** — Accountable individual
11. **Review Date** — When to re-evaluate

**Importance (2 marks):**
- Legal compliance (Factories Act 1948)
- Systematic tracking of all hazards
- Foundation for risk assessment
- Communication tool for workers and management
- Audit and review document

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Five Common Hazard Identification Techniques:**

1. **Job Safety Analysis (JSA)** — Breaks down each task into steps and identifies hazards at each step. (1.5 marks)

2. **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** — Systematic examination using guide words (NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN) for process industries. (1.5 marks)

3. **Checklist Analysis** — Uses pre-defined checklists of common hazards for specific industries. (1 mark)

4. **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** — Identifies potential failure modes, their effects, and criticality. (1 mark)

5. **What-If Analysis** — Brainstorming sessions posing "what if" scenarios to identify potential hazards. (1 mark)

6. **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** — Top-down deductive analysis using Boolean logic. (1 mark)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus:** Mentioning **HIRA (Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment)** — the most widely used technique in Indian industries — earns an additional 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Hazard with Risk** — Costs **1-2 marks** per occurrence. Always clarify.
> 2. **Skipping Examples** — Definitions without examples lose **1 mark** in every Part A.
> 3. **No Diagram in Part B** — A 14-mark answer without a diagram loses at least **1-2 marks**.
> 4. **Memorizing Without Understanding** — Examiners specifically look for application/numerical work in Part B. Pure theory answers score a maximum of **10-11 marks** out of 14.
> 5. **Forgetting Units** — Risk scores, fire load density, and dose calculations without units lose **0.5 mark each**.
> 6. **Ignoring the Hierarchy of Controls** — When discussing hazard mitigation, failing to mention **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE** loses **1 mark**.
> 7. **Not Using Bullet Points** — KTU valuation keys reward structured answers. Wall-of-text answers are penalized.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Hazard** = Source with potential for harm (intrinsic)
- [x] **Risk** = Probability × Consequence ($R = P \times C$)
- [x] **Danger** = Imminent threat; **Threat** = External adversarial agent
- [x] **8 Categories** of Hazards: Physical, Chemical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial, Environmental, Fire/Explosion, Electrical
- [x] **Mnemonic:** "P-C-B-E-P-E-F-E"
- [x] **5-Stage Hazard Pipeline:** Identification → Characterization → Exposure Assessment → Risk Characterization → Management
- [x] **Hazard Register** = Structured document tracking all hazards
- [x] **6 Identification Techniques:** JSA, HAZOP, Checklist, FMEA, What-If, FTA
- [x] **Hierarchy of Controls** order (most effective to least): Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE
- [x] **Hazard Quotient** $HQ = E/RfD$; acceptable if $HQ \le 1$
- [x] **Fire Load Density** $q = \sum M_i H_i / A$; classifies hazard severity
- [x] **Acute vs Chronic vs Latent hazards** — different manifestation times
- [x] **Localized, Area, Site-Wide, Off-Site** spatial classifications
- [x] **Risk Aggregation:** Sum method vs Union bound $R_{total} = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$
- [x] **Real-world anchors:** Bhopal (1984), Vizag (2020), Shanghai dust explosion (2014)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board Tip:** Before submitting your answer sheet, **re-read the question** and ensure you have:
> 1. Defined every key term in the first sentence of each answer.
> 2. Provided at least one **example** per concept.
> 3. Drawn at least **one diagram** in every 14-mark answer.
> 4. Mentioned the **hierarchy of controls** at least once.
> 5. Used proper **units** for all numerical quantities.

**End of Module 1 Notes — Topic: Hazard**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT205 - PRINCIPLES OF SAFETY MANAGEMENT (SAFETY & FIRE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Risk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Risk

In the context of safety management, fire engineering, and occupational health (KTU 2024 Scheme – PCSFT205), **Risk** is formally defined as the **combination of the probability of an event occurring and the severity of the consequences (harm) arising from that event**.

According to the universally adopted **ISO 31000:2018 – Risk Management Guidelines**, risk is defined as the *"effect of uncertainty on objectives,"* which in safety terms is practically operationalized as:

> **Risk (R) = Probability of Occurrence (P) × Severity of Consequence (C)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:** Risk is not a property of a hazard alone. Risk is the quantitative or qualitative expression of the *likelihood* that a hazard will manifest as harm, multiplied by the *magnitude* of that harm. The terms **Hazard** and **Risk** must NEVER be used interchangeably in board answers.

### 1.2 Related Terminology (KTU 2024 High-Yield Glossary)

| Term | Definition | KTU Board Example |
|------|------------|-------------------|
| **Hazard** | A source, situation, or act with the potential to cause harm in terms of human injury, ill-health, property damage, or environmental impact. | Stored LPG cylinders, open wiring, slippery floor |
| **Hazard Identification** | The systematic process of recognizing hazards present in a workplace or system. | HAZOP, Checklist, FMEA |
| **Risk** | The product of probability and consequence. | A worker slipping on oil with a 70% chance per shift and a broken bone as consequence |
| **Consequence (C)** | The outcome or severity of an event if it occurs. | Fatality, Injury, Property Loss |
| **Probability (P) / Likelihood** | The chance that the event will occur in a defined time period. | 1 in 10,000 per year |
| **Risk Assessment** | The overall process of hazard identification, risk analysis, and risk evaluation. | Job Safety Analysis (JSA) |
| **Risk Control / Treatment** | The process of modifying risk through prevention, reduction, or transfer. | Installing guard rails, PPE, fire suppression |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Road Crossing" Model

Imagine you are crossing a busy highway:

- **Hazard** = The moving vehicles (the danger source).
- **Consequence** = If a vehicle hits you, the outcome is severe injury or death (high severity).
- **Probability/Likelihood** = How often cars are passing — at a traffic signal it is *low*, on a blind curve it is *high*.
- **Risk** = The combined "feel" of danger — a blind curve with speeding trucks is a HIGH risk. A controlled signal crossing is LOW risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** When a question asks *"Differentiate between Hazard and Risk"*, your answer must emphasize that a hazard is *static* (it simply exists), while risk is *dynamic* (it changes with probability and exposure). The same hazard can yield different risks under different conditions (e.g., petrol in a sealed tank vs. petrol stored near a furnace).

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Risk Characterization

For KTU 2024 theory questions, every discussion of risk must address:

1. **What can go wrong?** → Hazard identification
2. **How likely is it?** → Probability/Likelihood
3. **What are the consequences?** → Severity/Consequence

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** A complete risk statement always answers all three pillars. For example: *"The risk of electrocution while working on a 415V panel without lockout-tagout is HIGH because the probability of contact is frequent (daily) and the consequence is fatal."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation of Risk

Risk, in modern safety engineering, is treated as a **bivariate function** of two independent variables: Probability and Consequence. The general functional form is:

$$R = f(P, C)$$

The most widely used operational simplification (used in OSHA, ISO, and KTU expected answers) is the **multiplicative model**:

$$R = P \times C$$

Where:
- **P** = Probability of the hazardous event occurring (dimensionless, 0 ≤ P ≤ 1)
- **C** = Consequence severity (expressed in fatalities, injuries, financial loss, or a normalized severity index)
- **R** = Risk score (dimensionless, plotted on a risk matrix)

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Risk Construction

To properly construct a risk value, the following logical sequence is followed:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Hazard:** Determine *what* can cause harm (chemical, mechanical, electrical, biological, ergonomic).
- **Step 2 — Identify the Initiating Event:** Determine *what event* releases the hazard (e.g., valve failure, human error, structural collapse).
- **Step 3 — Determine the Probability (P):** Quantify the likelihood using historical data, fault tree analysis, or expert judgment.
- **Step 4 — Determine the Consequence (C):** Estimate the worst credible outcome. Consequences may be:
  - **Human:** Fatalities, injuries, occupational diseases
  - **Property:** Asset damage in monetary terms
  - **Environmental:** Pollution, ecological damage
  - **Reputational:** Brand damage, legal liability
- **Step 5 — Combine P and C:** Apply the risk equation to derive a risk score or risk level.
- **Step 6 — Compare with Criteria:** Compare the risk score against the organization's risk acceptance criteria (tolerance matrix).
- **Step 7 — Apply Controls (if needed):** Reduce P, reduce C, or both, then recalculate until ALARP is reached.

### 2.3 Risk Matrix (5 × 5) — The KTU Board Standard

The **5×5 Risk Matrix** is the most common tool used in KTU board examinations and industrial safety audits. It combines **5 levels of Likelihood** with **5 levels of Severity** to produce a risk score ranging from 1 to 25.

| Severity ↓ \ Likelihood → | **Rare (1)** | **Unlikely (2)** | **Possible (3)** | **Likely (4)** | **Almost Certain (5)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Catastrophic (5)** | 5 (M) | 10 (H) | 15 (VH) | 20 (VH) | 25 (VH) |
| **Major (4)** | 4 (L-M) | 8 (M) | 12 (H) | 16 (VH) | 20 (VH) |
| **Moderate (3)** | 3 (L) | 6 (M) | 9 (M-H) | 12 (H) | 15 (H) |
| **Minor (2)** | 2 (L) | 4 (L-M) | 6 (M) | 8 (M) | 10 (H) |
| **Negligible (1)** | 1 (L) | 2 (L) | 3 (L) | 4 (L-M) | 5 (M) |

**Legend:** L = Low (Acceptable), M = Medium (Monitor), H = High (Action Required), VH = Very High (Immediate Stop)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The risk matrix is used in construction safety (CDM 2015), process industries (COMAH regulations), aviation (FAA), and fire engineering (NFPA). In fire engineering, it is used to determine the Fire Safety Design Level of a building based on the *life safety risk* to occupants.

### 2.4 The ALARP Principle (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)

ALARP is a legal and engineering doctrine originating from UK case law (Edwards v. National Coal Board, 1949) and is a cornerstone of KTU's safety management syllabus.

**Statement:** All risks must be reduced to a level that is *"As Low As Reasonably Practicable"* — meaning that the cost (in money, effort, time) of further risk reduction must be *"grossly disproportionate"* to the safety benefit gained.

Mathematically represented as:

$$\text{Risk} \rightarrow \text{Minimized subject to: } \frac{\text{Cost of further reduction}}{\text{Benefit of further reduction}} \ll 1$$

The three ALARP regions are:

1. **Inherent Risk Region:** Risk is intolerable; operation cannot proceed.
2. **ALARP / Tolerable Region:** Risk is acceptable only if ALARP is demonstrated.
3. **Broadly Acceptable Region:** Risk is negligible; no further control required.

### 2.5 Types of Risk (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Type | Description | KTU Example |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Individual Risk (IR)** | Risk to a single person in the vicinity of a hazard. | Risk to a worker at a chemical plant |
| **Societal Risk (SR)** | Risk to the entire exposed population; expressed using F-N curves. | Risk of a dam failure affecting a downstream town |
| **Acceptable Risk** | Risk level that society/industry is willing to live with. | Driving a car |
| **Residual Risk** | Risk remaining *after* controls have been applied. | Fire risk in a building with sprinklers installed |
| **Inherent Risk** | Risk *before* any control measures are implemented. | Fire risk in a building with no fire safety systems |
| **Tolerable Risk** | Risk that is accepted for a period to gain net benefit. | Industrial chemical exposure within TLV limits |
| **Perceived Risk** | Subjective, often emotional, estimate of risk. | Public fear of nuclear plants vs. statistical fatality data |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table: All Key Risk Formulas for KTU Board Exams**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $R = P \times C$ | Basic risk equation | Dimensionless score |
| 2 | $R_{residual} = P_{reduced} \times C_{reduced}$ | Risk remaining after controls | Should be < $R_{acceptable}$ |
| 3 | $IR = \sum f_i \cdot p_i$ | Individual Risk per annum | Probability of fatality per year |
| 4 | $FN = \text{plot of } F(N) \text{ vs. } N$ | Societal risk (F-N curve) | N = number of fatalities |
| 5 | $\text{ALARP:} \; \Delta S \gg \Delta C$ | Risk reduction "grossly disproportionate" test | $\Delta S$ = safety benefit, $\Delta C$ = cost |
| 6 | $R = \frac{\text{Hazard}}{\text{Safeguard}}$ | Conceptual inverse relationship | Risk falls as safeguards rise |
| 7 | $\text{Risk Index (RI)} = \text{Severity} \times \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Frequency}$ | Tri-variate risk model | Used in Fine-Kinney method |

### 2.7 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Fire Engineering:** Risk-based design (performance-based design) replaces prescriptive codes. Engineers must demonstrate that life safety risk to occupants is ALARP.
- **Process Safety:** Quantitative Risk Analysis (QRA) is mandatory for Seveso/COMAH installations; risk is plotted on F-N curves against acceptability criteria.
- **Construction Safety:** Permit-to-work systems, Job Safety Analysis, and risk assessment registers all depend on accurate P and C estimation.
- **Insurance & Finance:** Property insurance premiums are direct functions of quantified risk scores; lower risk = lower premium.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Basic Risk Score (R = P × C)

The risk score is a dimensionless quantity that combines two independent factors. The derivation logic is as follows:

**Step 1:** Start with the qualitative definition:

$$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Probability}, \text{Consequence})$$

**Step 2:** Assume (as per ISO 31000 and KTU board convention) that probability and consequence combine multiplicatively in the linear regime (i.e., for low-to-moderate risk levels). This gives:

$$R = f(P, C) = P \times C$$

**Step 3:** Substitute normalized values. Probability is normalized to the range 0 to 1, while Consequence is normalized to a severity index (typically 1 to 5 in a 5×5 matrix). Therefore:

$$R = P_{norm} \times C_{index}$$

**Step 4:** For discrete risk matrix scoring, both variables take integer values from 1 to 5:

$$R = L \times S$$

Where **L** = Likelihood score (1 to 5) and **S** = Severity score (1 to 5).

**Step 5:** The resulting R value falls in the range:

$$R_{min} = 1 \times 1 = 1, \quad R_{max} = 5 \times 5 = 25$$

**Step 6:** These 25 cells are then grouped into risk bands (e.g., Low: 1–4, Medium: 5–9, High: 10–15, Very High: 16–25) using organizational tolerance criteria.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The multiplicative model assumes that doubling either P or C doubles the risk. This is consistent with the **Health and Safety Executive (HSE, UK)** guidance and is the standard KTU board approach. For societal risk, however, a logarithmic or exponential model is used (see F-N curves below).

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Risk Matrix Calculation

**Problem:** A fire engineer is evaluating two fire scenarios in a high-rise building.

- **Scenario A (Kitchen fire):** Likelihood = 2 (Unlikely), Severity = 2 (Minor injury)
- **Scenario B (Electrical panel fire):** Likelihood = 3 (Possible), Severity = 4 (Major injury, possible fatality)

**Step 1 — Calculate Risk Score for Scenario A:**

$$R_A = L_A \times S_A = 2 \times 2 = 4$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Risk Score for Scenario B:**

$$R_B = L_B \times S_B = 3 \times 4 = 12$$

**Step 3 — Classify Each Risk Using the Standard Matrix Bands:**

$$R_A = 4 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Low–Medium (Acceptable with monitoring)}$$

$$R_B = 12 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{High (Action Required Immediately)}$$

**Step 4 — Recommend Controls for Scenario B:**

Since $R_B$ falls in the High band, controls must be applied. The engineer can either:

- Reduce L from 3 to 2 (e.g., install arc-fault detection, monthly thermal imaging) → New $R = 2 \times 4 = 8$ (Medium)
- Reduce S from 4 to 2 (e.g., automatic fire suppression limiting fire spread) → New $R = 3 \times 2 = 6$ (Medium)
- Apply both → New $R = 2 \times 2 = 4$ (Low)

**Step 5 — Demonstrate ALARP:**

The reduced risk $R = 4$ lies in the Low band. The cost of further reduction (e.g., gas suppression + structural fireproofing) is *"grossly disproportionate"* to the marginal safety benefit. Therefore, the residual risk is ALARP, and the design is acceptable.

### 3.3 Derivation of Individual Risk (IR)

The **Individual Risk** is defined as the probability that an average person in the vicinity of a hazard will suffer a fatal injury in a given year. It is computed as:

$$IR = f_i \cdot p_i$$

Where:
- **$f_i$** = frequency of the hazardous event per year (events/year)
- **$p_i$** = probability of fatality *given* the hazardous event occurs (dimensionless, 0 to 1)

For multiple events affecting the same individual, the total individual risk is the sum:

$$IR_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot p_i$$

**Numerical Example:** A worker is exposed to two hazards:
- Hazard 1: Crane failure — $f_1 = 10^{-4}$ per year, $p_1 = 0.5$
- Hazard 2: Chemical exposure — $f_2 = 10^{-3}$ per year, $p_2 = 0.05$

$$IR_1 = 10^{-4} \times 0.5 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

$$IR_2 = 10^{-3} \times 0.05 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

$$IR_{total} = IR_1 + IR_2 = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per year}$$

This equals a 1 in 10,000 chance of fatality per year, which is the typical **HSE UK upper tolerable limit** for workers in hazardous industries.

### 3.4 F-N Curve Concept (Societal Risk)

For risks that affect *multiple* people simultaneously (e.g., dam failure, BLEVE, building collapse), societal risk is plotted as an **F-N Curve**:

$$\text{Cumulative Frequency } F(N) \text{ vs. Number of Fatalities } N$$

The relationship typically follows a power law:

$$F(N) \propto N^{-k}$$

Where the slope $k$ determines risk aversion. Steeper curves (higher $k$) indicate greater public concern for multi-fatality events.

A sample F-N acceptability criterion (HSE UK):

$$F(N) = \frac{10^{-2}}{N^2} \quad \text{(upper limit)}$$

$$F(N) = \frac{10^{-4}}{N^2} \quad \text{(lower / negligible risk limit)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Note:** You are NOT expected to derive F-N equations in detail. You must, however, *define* F-N curves, *sketch* the standard chart, and *interpret* whether a plotted risk is acceptable, ALARP, or intolerable.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Risk vs. Hazard

| Parameter | Hazard | Risk |
|-----------|--------|------|
| **Nature** | Source of potential harm | Combination of probability × consequence |
| **Quantitative?** | Usually qualitative | Often quantified (1–25 score) |
| **Existence** | Can exist with zero risk (if probability = 0) | Cannot exist without a hazard |
| **Time-dependent?** | No | Yes (probability changes with time, controls, exposure) |
| **Example** | Petrol stored in a sealed container | Petrol stored in a sealed container, but next to a furnace = HIGH risk |
| **Person-dependent?** | Independent of exposed person | Depends on exposure frequency and population density |

### 3.6 Hierarchy of Risk Control (Most Effective to Least Effective)

Following ISO 45001 and the KTU syllabus, control measures must be applied in this strict order of preference:

1. **Elimination** — Remove the hazard entirely (e.g., substitute benzene with toluene).
2. **Substitution** — Replace with a less hazardous alternative.
3. **Engineering Controls** — Guards, ventilation, interlocks, sprinklers.
4. **Administrative Controls** — Training, signage, safe procedures, supervision.
5. **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** — Helmets, gloves, respirators (last resort).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often list PPE first. This will cost you 1–2 marks in any KTU descriptive question. Always apply the **hierarchy in correct order**, and emphasize that PPE is the *last* line of defense.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Risk Assessment Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System or Activity Definition] --> B[Hazard Identification HAZID]
    B --> C[Identify Initiating Events]
    C --> D[Estimate Probability P]
    C --> E[Estimate Consequence C]
    D --> F[Calculate Risk Score R = P x C]
    E --> F
    F --> G{R vs Acceptance Criteria}
    G -->|Below Threshold| H[Risk ACCEPTABLE Monitor]
    G -->|Within ALARP| I[Apply Controls Reduce P and C]
    G -->|Above Threshold| J[STOP Process Immediate Action]
    I --> K[Recalculate Residual Risk]
    K --> G
    J --> L[Redesign System Reassess]
    L --> B
    H --> M[Document Review Periodically]
    K --> M
    M --> N[Risk Register Update]
```

### 4.2 ALARP Tri-Zone Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Low Risk\nBroadly Acceptable\nNo Action Needed] -->|Risk Increases| M[Medium Risk\nALARP Zone\nApply Cost Benefit]
    M -->|Risk Increases| H[High Risk\nIntolerable\nMust Not Proceed]
    H -.->|Reduce via Hierarchy of Controls| M
    M -.->|Further Reduction| L
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Risk Controls Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Most Effective Elimination Remove the Hazard] --> A2[Substitution Replace with Safer Alternative]
    A2 --> A3[Engineering Controls Guards Ventilation Interlocks]
    A3 --> A4[Administrative Controls Procedures Training Signage]
    A4 --> A5[Least Effective PPE Helmets Gloves Respirators]
    style A1 fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff
    style A2 fill:#52be80,color:#fff
    style A3 fill:#f39c12,color:#fff
    style A4 fill:#e67e22,color:#fff
    style A5 fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Risk Matrix Decision Logic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Identify Hazard] --> S2[Step 2 Assign Likelihood 1 to 5]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Assign Severity 1 to 5]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Compute R = L x S]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Locate Cell in 5x5 Matrix]
    S5 --> S6{R Score Range}
    S6 -->|1 to 4| R1[LOW - Acceptable Document]
    S6 -->|5 to 9| R2[MEDIUM - Monitor Apply Admin Controls]
    S6 -->|10 to 15| R3[HIGH - Engineering Controls Required]
    S6 -->|16 to 25| R4[VERY HIGH - STOP Immediate Elimination]
```

### 4.5 Risk-Based Decision Tree (Accept / Control / Reject)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z[Computed Risk Score R] --> Q1{R > 15}
    Q1 -->|YES| X1[REJECT Redo Design]
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2{10 <= R <= 15}
    Q2 -->|YES| X2[CONTROL with Engineering Measures]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q3{5 <= R <= 9}
    Q3 -->|YES| X3[MONITOR with Admin Controls and PPE]
    Q3 -->|NO| X4[ACCEPT Document and Review Annually]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Board Diagrams:** Always use clean rectangular or rounded-rectangle nodes. Avoid using reserved Mermaid words (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) as node IDs. Use `step1`, `step2`, `decision1`, etc. The diagrams above are fully KTU-compatible and can be reproduced in exam answer sheets.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**"Differentiate between Hazard and Risk. Give two examples from a process plant to support your answer."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks, ~80 words):**

A **Hazard** is a source, situation, or act with the *potential* to cause harm, while **Risk** is the *combination of the probability* of that harm occurring and the *severity of the consequences* (R = P × C).

**Example 1 (Hazard only, low risk):** A 50 kg LPG cylinder stored in a locked, well-ventilated, fire-proof enclosure — the hazard exists, but the probability of release is very low, so risk is LOW.

**Example 2 (Same hazard, high risk):** The same LPG cylinder stored near an open furnace with poor ventilation — the hazard is unchanged, but probability of ignition is high, so risk is HIGH.

This proves that the *same hazard can yield different risks* depending on exposure and conditions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**"What is the ALARP principle? State its significance in industrial safety management."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks, ~70 words):**

**ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** is a risk reduction principle that requires employers to reduce workplace risks to the lowest level that is *reasonably practicable*, where the cost (money, time, effort) of further reduction is *"grossly disproportionate"* to the safety benefit gained.

**Significance:**
- It provides a **legal and engineering balance** between absolute safety and economic feasibility.
- It forms the basis of UK HSE law, ISO 45001, and is the **core acceptance criterion in QRA** (Quantitative Risk Analysis) for process plants, fire engineering, and construction safety.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

---

### **Part B – Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model ESE Question, End Semester Pattern]**

**Q.A. (a) Define Risk in safety management. With a neat sketch, explain the 5×5 Risk Matrix used for risk evaluation. Discuss the limitations of the risk matrix approach.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a)(i) Definition (2 Marks):**
Risk is the combination of the probability of a hazardous event occurring and the severity of the consequences arising from it. Mathematically, **R = P × C**, where R is risk, P is probability (likelihood), and C is consequence severity. The KTU board-standard reference is **ISO 31000:2018**, which defines risk as the *"effect of uncertainty on objectives."*

**(a)(ii) 5×5 Risk Matrix (3 Marks):**

| Severity ↓ / Likelihood → | **Rare (1)** | **Unlikely (2)** | **Possible (3)** | **Likely (4)** | **Almost Certain (5)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Catastrophic (5)** | 5 (M) | 10 (H) | 15 (VH) | 20 (VH) | 25 (VH) |
| **Major (4)** | 4 (L) | 8 (M) | 12 (H) | 16 (VH) | 20 (VH) |
| **Moderate (3)** | 3 (L) | 6 (M) | 9 (M) | 12 (H) | 15 (H) |
| **Minor (2)** | 2 (L) | 4 (L) | 6 (M) | 8 (M) | 10 (H) |
| **Negligible (1)** | 1 (L) | 2 (L) | 3 (L) | 4 (L) | 5 (M) |

- **Likely axis (1–5):** Rare → Unlikely → Possible → Likely → Almost Certain
- **Severity axis (1–5):** Negligible → Minor → Moderate → Major → Catastrophic
- **Risk Bands:** Low (1–4), Medium (5–9), High (10–15), Very High (16–25)

**(a)(iii) Limitations of the Risk Matrix (2 Marks):**

1. **Subjective Scoring:** Likelihood and severity scores are assigned by human judgment, leading to inconsistency between assessors.
2. **Loss of Granularity:** A 5×5 grid produces only 25 cells; two risks with vastly different real-world consequences may end up in the same cell.
3. **Linear Assumption:** The matrix assumes R = P × C linearly; in reality, societal risk scales non-linearly (see F-N curves).
4. **No Time Dimension:** The matrix does not capture how risk changes over time or with varying exposure.
5. **Equal Weighting:** It treats all severity types (fatality, injury, financial) equally unless weighted.

---

**Q.A. (b) A chemical reactor has a probability of runaway reaction of 0.05 per year. If a runaway occurs, the fatality probability is 0.6. Calculate the Individual Risk (IR) per year. If the installation employs 50 workers, what is the expected number of fatalities per year? Compare the IR with the HSE UK upper tolerable limit of $10^{-4}$ per year.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(b)(i) Given Data (1 Mark):**
- Frequency of runaway, $f = 0.05$ per year
- Probability of fatality given runaway, $p = 0.6$
- Number of workers exposed, $N = 50$
- HSE UK tolerable limit, $IR_{limit} = 10^{-4}$ per year

**(b)(ii) Individual Risk Calculation (3 Marks):**

$$IR = f \times p$$

$$IR = 0.05 \times 0.6 = 0.03 \text{ per year}$$

This is equivalent to a **3 in 100 chance of fatality per year** for any individual worker exposed to the reactor.

**(b)(iii) Expected Annual Fatalities (2 Marks):**

$$\text{Expected Fatalities} = IR \times N$$

$$\text{Expected Fatalities} = 0.03 \times 50 = 1.5 \text{ fatalities per year}$$

**(b)(iv) Comparison with HSE Limit (1 Mark):**

$$\frac{IR_{actual}}{IR_{limit}} = \frac{0.03}{10^{-4}} = 300$$

The actual Individual Risk is **300 times higher** than the HSE UK upper tolerable limit. The installation is in the **INTOLERABLE region** and cannot be operated without immediate risk reduction measures (e.g., emergency cooling system, pressure relief valves, containment barriers).

---

### **Part B – Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. (a) Explain the ALARP principle in detail. With a neat sketch, describe the three risk zones (intolerable, ALARP, and broadly acceptable) and discuss the 'grossly disproportionate' test used in cost-benefit analysis.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a)(i) ALARP Definition and Origin (2 Marks):**
ALARP stands for **"As Low As Reasonably Practicable."** It originated from the UK case *Edwards v. National Coal Board (1949)* and is now codified in the **Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974** and adopted by ISO 45001.

**Statement:** Risks must be reduced to the lowest level that is reasonably practicable, balancing the *sacrifice* (cost, time, effort) against the *safety benefit*. Further risk reduction is required *unless* the cost is *"grossly disproportionate"* to the benefit gained.

**(a)(ii) Three Risk Zones (3 Marks):**

| Zone | Risk Level | Action Required |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| **Intolerable Region** | Very High (e.g., R > 15) | Process must NOT proceed. Immediate elimination/substitution required. |
| **ALARP / Tolerable Region** | Medium (5–15) | Process may proceed ONLY if ALARP is demonstrated. Engineering + admin controls + PPE applied. |
| **Broadly Acceptable Region** | Negligible (R < 5) | No further action. Routine monitoring. |

**Sketch (drawn in answer sheet):**

```
Risk
  ^
  |  INTOLERABLE
  |  ===========
  |  |||||||||||
  |  |||||||||||
  |  --------- (Upper Tolerance Line)
  |  ALARP / TOLERABLE ZONE
  |  .........
  |  --------- (Lower Tolerance Line)
  |  BROADLY ACCEPTABLE
  |________________>
       Cost of further reduction
```

**(a)(iii) Grossly Disproportionate Test (2 Marks):**
The test compares the **cost of further risk reduction (C)** with the **safety benefit (S)** of that reduction. If C/S is *grossly disproportionate* (typically a factor of 10 to 100, depending on the industry), then no further reduction is required.

$$\text{If } \frac{C}{S} > k \; (\text{where } k \approx 10 \text{ to } 100), \; \text{then ALARP is satisfied.}$$

---

**Q.B. (b) A fire risk assessment of a hospital ward yields the following: Likelihood = 4 (Likely), Severity = 5 (Catastrophic). Calculate the risk score. If sprinklers reduce the likelihood to 2 and fire-resistant construction reduces the severity to 3, calculate the new residual risk. Comment on whether ALARP has been achieved.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(b)(i) Initial (Inherent) Risk Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$R_{inherent} = L \times S = 4 \times 5 = 20$$

**Classification:** $R = 20$ falls in the **VERY HIGH** band (16–25), which is in the **Intolerable Region**. The hospital ward cannot operate in this state.

**(b)(ii) Residual Risk Calculation (3 Marks):**

After installing sprinklers, $L_{new} = 2$. After fire-resistant construction, $S_{new} = 3$.

$$R_{residual} = L_{new} \times S_{new} = 2 \times 3 = 6$$

**Classification:** $R = 6$ falls in the **MEDIUM** band (5–9), which lies within the **ALARP / Tolerable Region**.

**(b)(iii) ALARP Discussion (2 Marks):**

- The inherent risk of 20 (Very High) is **intolerable** and unacceptable for a hospital ward housing immobile patients.
- After the controls, the residual risk is reduced by **70%** (from 20 to 6), placing it in the ALARP zone.
- To demonstrate ALARP, the cost of *further* reduction (e.g., full sprinkler system upgrade to gas suppression, or hospital-wide fireproofing) must be tested against the marginal safety benefit.
- If the cost of further reduction is **grossly disproportionate** (e.g., 50× the safety benefit), then ALARP is considered achieved and the design is acceptable, with **annual review and monitoring** mandated.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Hazard" and "Risk" in definitions.** Examiners specifically deduct 1 mark if these terms are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Do NOT skip the formula $R = P \times C$.** Even in 14-mark questions, the mathematical expression must be stated clearly before any calculation.
> 3. **Numerical questions require units.** Always write "per year" for IR, and check your decimal place — a misplaced decimal (e.g., writing 0.3 instead of 0.03) costs full marks.
> 4. **Hierarchy of controls — order matters.** Always list Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE. Reversing the order costs 1 mark.
> 5. **In ALARP sketches, the Y-axis must be labelled "Risk" and X-axis "Cost of further reduction" or "Log of Frequency (F-N)."** Unlabelled axes = -1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Risk (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Risk = Probability × Consequence** ($R = P \times C$) — the foundational equation.
- [ ] **Hazard ≠ Risk.** Hazard is a source; risk is the product of probability and consequence.
- [ ] **Five types of risk** to remember: Individual, Societal, Acceptable, Residual, Inherent.
- [ ] **5×5 Risk Matrix** with bands: Low (1–4), Medium (5–9), High (10–15), Very High (16–25).
- [ ] **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — balance cost vs. safety benefit.
- [ ] **Three risk zones:** Intolerable, ALARP (Tolerable), Broadly Acceptable.
- [ ] **Hierarchy of Controls (in order):** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- [ ] **Individual Risk formula:** $IR = f \times p$ (per year).
- [ ] **F-N Curve** plots cumulative frequency vs. number of fatalities; used for societal risk.
- [ ] **HSE UK tolerable IR limit** for workers: $10^{-4}$ per year (memorize this value).
- [ ] **ISO 31000** is the international standard reference for risk management.
- [ ] **Residual Risk** is what remains *after* controls; must be ALARP before operation.
- [ ] **Always state units in numerical answers** — "per year" for IR, "score" for risk matrix.
- [ ] **Common numerical check:** Risk score $R = L \times S$ must lie in range 1 to 25.
- [ ] **Real-world examples to cite:** LPG storage, electrical panels, chemical reactors, fire in high-rise buildings, construction site falls.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsafe Act
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT205 - PRINCIPLES OF SAFETY MANAGEMENT (SAFETY & FIRE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unsafe Act

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: UNSAFE ACT — CORE CONCEPTUAL FOUNDATION

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *Principles of Safety Management* and the universally accepted definition codified in **OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration)** and **H.W. Heinrich's Industrial Accident Prevention (1931)**, an **Unsafe Act** is defined as:

> A behavioral deviation from a recognized safe procedure, standard operating practice, or regulatory requirement, performed by a worker or supervisor, which **directly or indirectly permits the occurrence of an accident** and has the potential to cause injury, ill-health, or fatality to personnel, damage to property/equipment, or degradation of the working environment.

In the KTU board examination context, an unsafe act is the **human-behavioral (behavior-based)** counterpart of an **unsafe condition** (the engineering/physical counterpart). Together, they form the two root cause categories in the classic **Heinrich Domino Theory of Accident Causation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examiner Definition (Must Use in 2-Mark and 14-Mark Answers):**
> *"An Unsafe Act is a violation of an accepted safe procedure by a person, which is one of the immediate causes of an accident, the other being an unsafe condition. Approximately 88% of all industrial accidents are caused by unsafe acts, while only 10% are caused by unsafe conditions (Heinrich's Domino Theory, 1931)."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wet Floor" Café

Imagine you walk into a café and see a **"Wet Floor — Caution"** signboard placed by the manager. There are two ways an accident can still happen to you:

1. **You ignore the sign and sprint across the wet floor** to catch your phone call → This is an **UNSAFE ACT** (you, the human, chose to violate a safe rule).
2. **The manager forgot to place the signboard** and the floor is wet → This is an **UNSAFE CONDITION** (the environment was not engineered/safeguarded properly).

The **accident (you slipping and breaking your wrist)** is the same, but the **causal origin** is fundamentally different. Unsafe acts live in the **mind and behavior of the worker**; unsafe conditions live in the **physical world of the workplace**.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Accident Causation (Heinrich's Framework)

Heinrich proposed that every accident sits atop a stack of five sequential "dominoes":

1. **Ancestry and Social Environment** (heredity, upbringing, financial stress)
2. **Fault of Person** (carelessness, recklessness — root psychological cause)
3. **Unsafe Act and/or Unsafe Condition** ← *(Direct cause — your topic)*
4. **Accident** (the incident itself)
5. **Injury / Damage / Loss**

Removing **Domino 3** breaks the chain and prevents the accident.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statistic for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Unsafe Acts:** 88% of accidents
> - **Unsafe Conditions:** 10% of accidents
> - **Acts of God / Unavoidable:** 2% of accidents
> *(Source: H.W. Heinrich, "Industrial Accident Prevention", McGraw-Hill, 1931)*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 1: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Eight (8) Classical Unsafe Acts — Heinrich's Taxonomy

Heinrich's 1931 classification lists **eight canonical unsafe acts** that account for nearly all human-caused accidents. Every KTU answer on this topic MUST enumerate these correctly.

| # | Unsafe Act Category | Plain-English Explanation | Real-World Industry Example |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Operating without authority** | Starting, running, or stopping equipment you are not trained/certified to operate | An apprentice welder firing up a CNC plasma cutter |
| 2 | **Operating at unsafe speed** | Working too fast or too slow for the task | Over-revving a forklift in a narrow aisle |
| 3 | **Using unsafe equipment / using equipment unsafely** | Using the wrong tool, or using a right tool incorrectly | Standing on a swivel chair to reach a high shelf |
| 4 | **Failure to secure / warn** | Not locking-out (LOTO) or not posting caution signs | Not putting a "Men at Work" sign before opening an electrical panel |
| 5 | **Taking unsafe position / posture** | Standing in the line of fire, awkward ergonomics | Placing head under a suspended load |
| 6 | **Distraction, horseplay, neglect** | Daydreaming, teasing, abandoning attention | A construction worker scrolling on phone near an excavation |
| 7 | **Failure to use personal protective equipment (PPE)** | Refusing or forgetting to wear hard hat, gloves, harness, etc. | Painter entering a confined space without respirator |
| 8 | **Improper lifting / handling** | Wrong technique, too-heavy loads, single-person lift | A nurse lifting a 40 kg patient alone instead of using a hoist |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** The KTU examiner awards **1 mark per correctly listed category** in a "List the unsafe acts" question. Memorize all 8 in order — examiners explicitly state *"any eight"* or *"the classical Heinrich's eight"*.

---

## 2.2 Frank Bird's Modernized Classification (1974)

Frank Bird, while revising Heinrich's data, expanded the taxonomy to include **seven (7) categories** based on the statistical analysis of **1,753,498 accidents** from 297 companies. This is a high-yield KTU topic.

Bird's Seven Unsafe Acts:

1. **Operating equipment without authority**
2. **Failure to warn / Failure to secure**
3. **Operating at improper speed**
4. **Making safety devices inoperable** *(e.g., bypassing machine guards)*
5. **Removing safety devices** *(e.g., disconnecting interlocks)*
6. **Using equipment improperly**
7. **Using improper body position / posture**

**Difference from Heinrich:** Bird collapsed Heinrich's "distraction/horseplay" and "failure to use PPE" into the broader category of *behavioral non-compliance* and *improper PPE use*, and added explicit **"bypassing/removing safety devices"** — a critical category in modern automated industries.

---

## 2.3 The Behavioral Sequence Model (BSM) — DuPont's Approach

The modern safety management industry (DuPont, BS 8800, OHSAS 18001, ISO 45001) uses the **Behavioral Safety (BBS — Behavior-Based Safety)** framework. An unsafe act is broken into a **3-stage chain**:

$$
\text{Activator} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Behavior} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Consequence}
$$

- **Activator:** A workplace trigger (noise, fatigue, unclear SOP)
- **Behavior:** The unsafe act (e.g., not wearing a harness)
- **Consequence:** Incident, near-miss, or safe outcome

KTU may ask: *"Explain the ABC model of behavior-based safety."* — Always cite **DuPont's BBS methodology**.

---

## 2.4 Causes (Antecedents) of Unsafe Acts — The 4M Framework

The **root causes** behind an unsafe act are categorized under the **4M (or 4E) model**:

| Factor | Description | Example |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Man (Human)** | Lack of skill, low morale, fatigue, drug/alcohol use | Sleep-deprived night-shift welder |
| **Machine (Engineering)** | Poor equipment design, missing guards, awkward layout | Control panel placed at neck-twisting height |
| **Media (Environment)** | Noise, heat, poor lighting, congestion | 90 dB noise causing worker to miss an alarm |
| **Management (System)** | No SOP, no training, no enforcement, no reward system | Supervisor ignores PPE violations |

The **4E Counter-Measure Model** (DuPont):

- **Engineering** controls (guards, interlocks)
- **Education** (training, toolbox talks)
- **Enforcement** (penalties, audits)
- **Example** (leadership walk-throughs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering controls are ranked highest** in the **Hierarchy of Controls** (elimination → substitution → engineering → administration → PPE). KTU frequently asks students to *"rank control measures in order of effectiveness."*

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Metrics

Although *Unsafe Act* is largely conceptual, KTU questions on safety statistics use the following equations:

| Metric / Formula | Equation | Use in KTU Paper |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Accident Frequency Rate (AFR)** | $\text{AFR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Accidents} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Module 1 statistical problems |
| **Accident Severity Rate (ASR)** | $\text{ASR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Days Lost} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Often paired with AFR |
| **Incidence Rate (IR)** | $\text{IR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Injuries} \times 10^{3}}{\text{No. of Workers}}$ | Factories Act questions |
| **Heinrich's Ratio** | $1 : 29 : 300$ | *(1 major injury : 29 minor injuries : 300 no-injury accidents)* |
| **Bird's Ratio** | $1 : 10 : 30 : 600$ | *(1 serious : 10 lost-time : 30 minor : 600 no-injury)* |
| **Unsafe Act Contribution** | $\text{UA}\% = 88\%$ | Heinrich's classical statistic |
| **Cost of Unsafe Act** | $\text{Cost} = \text{Direct} + \text{Indirect (typically 4\times to 7\times direct)}$ | Heinrich's Accident Cost Iceberg |

> [!WARNING]
> **Heinrich's vs Bird's Ratio — Board Trap:** Examiners deliberately swap these. Heinrich's is **1:29:300** (3-tier). Bird's is **1:10:30:600** (4-tier). Mixing them is the **#1 most common error** in safety management papers.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding unsafe acts is critical in:

- **Construction Industry:** Fall from height is the #1 killer, predominantly due to *failure to use harness* (Heinrich Category 7).
- **Process Industries (Oil & Gas):** Bypassing LOTO interlocks has caused major disasters (e.g., Bhopal, Texas City, 2005).
- **Manufacturing:** Robotic cell accidents trace back to *operating without authority* or *removing safety devices*.
- **Fire Engineering:** Emergency evacuation failures often stem from *failure to warn* and *distraction/horseplay*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 1: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Heinrich's Domino Theory to Modern Loss Causation Model

Since *Unsafe Act* sits in the middle of the accident causation chain, KTU frequently asks students to **derive and explain the sequential logic** of how an unsafe act leads to injury. Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step logical derivation**.

### Step 1: Origin of the Chain — Ancestry and Social Environment

$$
\text{Ancestry} \;+\; \text{Social Environment} \;=\; \text{Person's Mental & Physical Traits}
$$

A worker inherits traits (vision, reflexes, temperament) and is shaped by social environment (poverty, peer pressure, family stress). These create the *latent* psychological conditions for risk-taking.

### Step 2: Fault of the Person (Psychological Origin)

The ancestry + environment creates a **"fault of person"** — a chronic tendency toward:
- Recklessness
- Impatience
- Stubbornness
- Nervousness

### Step 3: The Unsafe Act / Unsafe Condition (IMMEDIATE CAUSE)

$$
\text{Fault of Person} \;\xrightarrow{\text{manifests as}}\; \text{Unsafe Act (behavior)} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Unsafe Condition (environment)}
$$

This is the **central domino**. Removing it breaks the chain. KTU specifically tests this with questions like:

> *"According to Heinrich, removing which domino prevents the accident?"* — **Answer: Domino 3 (Unsafe Act / Unsafe Condition)**

### Step 4: The Accident

If the unsafe act remains unrectified and the worker is exposed:
$$
\text{Unsafe Act} \;+\; \text{Exposure} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Accident (incident)}
$$

Note: An accident is defined as an **unplanned, unwanted event** that may or may not result in injury/damage.

### Step 5: Injury / Damage / Loss

$$
\text{Accident} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Injury (to person) / Damage (to property) / Loss (to process)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Board Favorite):** An **accident** and an **injury** are NOT the same. An accident can occur *without* an injury (a near-miss). Heinrich's 1:29:300 ratio explicitly leverages this — for every major injury, there are 300 accidents with NO injury.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Numerical Worked Example — AFR & ASR

This is a **guaranteed 14-mark KTU question**. Work through it:

**Problem:** A factory employs **500 workers**, each working **8 hours/day** for **300 days/year**. During the year, **12 accidents** occurred resulting in a total of **240 lost man-days**. Calculate the **Accident Frequency Rate (AFR)** and **Accident Severity Rate (ASR)**.

### Step 1: Total Man-Hours Worked

$$
\text{Man-Hours} = \text{Workers} \times \text{Hours/Day} \times \text{Days/Year}
$$

$$
\text{Man-Hours} = 500 \times 8 \times 300
$$

$$
\text{Man-Hours} = 1{,}200{,}000 \; \text{man-hours}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate AFR

$$
\text{AFR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Accidents} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Total Man-Hours}}
$$

$$
\text{AFR} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^{6}}{1{,}200{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{AFR} = \dfrac{12{,}000{,}000}{1{,}200{,}000} = 10
$$

**AFR = 10 accidents per million man-hours worked**

### Step 3: Calculate ASR

$$
\text{ASR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Days Lost} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Total Man-Hours}}
$$

$$
\text{ASR} = \dfrac{240 \times 10^{6}}{1{,}200{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{ASR} = \dfrac{240{,}000{,}000}{1{,}200{,}000} = 200
$$

**ASR = 200 days lost per million man-hours worked**

### Step 4: Interpretation for the Board

- **AFR = 10** means **10 reportable accidents per million man-hours** — moderate for a heavy industry, low for construction.
- **ASR = 200** means **200 man-days lost per million man-hours** — relatively high severity, indicating either severe accidents or inadequate return-to-work protocols.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Benchmark:** ILO (International Labour Organization) considers **AFR < 5** as excellent, **5–15** as good, and **> 15** as poor for general manufacturing. Always conclude a numerical answer with a benchmark comparison.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Heinrich vs. Bird vs. Reason (Swiss Cheese Model)

KTU 14-mark questions often ask students to **compare and contrast accident causation models**. Use this matrix:

| Feature | Heinrich (1931) | Frank Bird (1974) | James Reason (1990, Swiss Cheese) |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **No. of Categories** | 8 unsafe acts | 7 unsafe acts | 4 layers of defense |
| **Focus** | Worker behavior | Worker behavior + system | **System + Latent failures** |
| **Cause Attribution** | 88% to humans | 85–90% to systems (people) | Up to 90% to **management system** |
| **Strength** | Simple, actionable | Statistically robust (1.75M accidents) | Captures *why* humans fail |
| **Weakness** | Blames the worker | Still human-centric | Harder to implement |
| **Modern Use** | Foundational reference | BBS foundation | ISO 45001, aviation safety |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990):** Every safety barrier (training, SOPs, guards, alarms) is a slice of Swiss cheese with holes. An accident occurs when **holes in all layers align**. The **unsafe act is the final "active failure"**, but the holes are caused by **"latent failures"** (management decisions, organizational culture). This shifts blame from the worker to the **system** — the foundation of modern **Just Culture** in safety.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 1: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Heinrich's Five-Domino Accident Causation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DOMINO 1: Ancestry and Social Environment"]:::domino1
    B["DOMINO 2: Fault of Person Carelessness Recklessness"]:::domino2
    C["DOMINO 3: Unsafe Act and Unsafe Condition DIRECT CAUSE"]:::domino3
    D["DOMINO 4: Accident Incident Near Miss"]:::domino4
    E["DOMINO 5: Injury Damage Loss"]:::domino5
    
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    
    F["SAFETY INTERVENTION Target: Remove Domino 3"]:::intervention
    
    C -.-> F
    
    classDef domino1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef domino2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Unsafe Act Causation & Control

```mermaid

    end
    
    subgraph UA["UNSAFE ACT LAYER 2"]
        U1["Operating Without Authority"]

    end
    
    subgraph ACC["ACCIDENT LAYER 3"]
        AC1["Slip Trip Fall"]
        AC2["Struck by Object"]

        C1["Elimination TOP MOST EFFECTIVE"]
        C2["Substitution"]

    end
    
    R1 --> U1
    R1 --> U3
    R2 --> U2
    R3 --> U4
    R4 --> U1
    R4 --> U2
    R4 --> U3

    U1 --> AC2
    U2 --> AC3

    U4 --> AC1
    
    CTRL -. BREAKS CHAIN .-> UA
```
