> **Real-World Analogy: Auditing = Annual Health Check-up**
>
> Imagine your **body** as a **factory**, your **doctor** as the **safety auditor**, and your **medical reports (ECG, blood tests, MRI)** as **audit evidence**.
>
> - **Pre-audit planning** = Booking the appointment, fasting before tests.
> - **On-site audit (execution)** = Doctor examining you, ordering tests, asking about lifestyle.
> - **Findings & NCs** = "Your cholesterol is high" (Minor NC) or "You have a heart blockage" (Major NC).
> - **Audit report** = The detailed medical report.
> - **Corrective action** = Medicines, diet plan, surgery.
> - **Follow-up audit** = The next quarterly visit to check if the medicines worked.
>
> Just as you do NOT skip annual health check-ups, an organization must NOT skip periodic safety audits. The audit is the **"early warning radar"** that catches fatal risks BEFORE they cause accidents.

## 1.4 Fundamental Objectives of Safety Audit Methodology

1. **Compliance Verification** — Ensuring adherence to statutory and regulatory safety requirements.
2. **Performance Evaluation** — Measuring the effectiveness of the existing safety management system.
3. **Hazard Identification** — Detecting latent hazards not identified through routine inspections.
4. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Driving the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle.
5. **Risk Reduction** — Quantifying the gap between "as-is" and "to-be" safety performance.
6. **Accountability & Documentation** — Creating an immutable legal trail of due diligence.

## 1.5 First Principles — The Audit "Triangle of Truth"

Every safety audit is anchored by three inseparable pillars:

- **Pillar 1 — INDEPENDENCE**: The auditor must be free from bias and operational responsibility.
- **Pillar 2 — EVIDENCE-BASED**: All findings must be supported by verifiable, documented proof.
- **Pillar 3 — SYSTEMATIC APPROACH**: A predefined, reproducible methodology must be used.

Without any one of these three pillars, the audit degenerates into a meaningless paperwork exercise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Safety Audits (Taxonomy)

### 2.1.1 By Scope
| Type | Description | Frequency |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| **Compliance Audit** | Checks conformity with statutory laws (Factories Act, OSHA, EPA) | Annual |
| **System Audit (Management System Audit)** | Evaluates SMS against ISO 45001 / OHSAS 18001 | Annual |
| **Process Audit** | Examines specific operational process (e.g., welding, confined space entry) | Quarterly |
| **Product Audit** | Checks the safety features of finished products | Per batch |
| **Project Audit** | Reviews safety in construction / commissioning of new projects | Milestone-based |

### 2.1.2 By Origin
| Type | Conducted By | Objective |
|------|--------------|-----------|
| **First-Party (Internal) Audit** | The organization's own trained auditors | Self-assessment |
| **Second-Party Audit** | Customer or supplier audits the organization | Contract compliance |
| **Third-Party (External) Audit** | Independent certification body (e.g., TÜV, BSI, DNV) | Certification |

## 2.2 Phases of Audit Methodology (The 6-Stage Life Cycle)

### **Stage 1: Audit Initiation & Scope Definition**
- Identify the audit objective, scope, and criteria.
- Define organizational boundaries, departments, processes, and time-frame.
- Appoint the **Lead Auditor** and **Audit Team**.

### **Stage 2: Pre-Audit Planning & Document Review**
- Review existing SMS documentation (policies, procedures, records of previous NCs).
- Prepare the **Audit Plan** (dated, with venue, time, attendees, logistics).
- Distribute audit schedule to the **Auditee** (the organization being audited).

### **Stage 3: Preparation of Audit Tools**
- Develop or update **Audit Checklists** mapped to ISO 45001 clauses / Factories Act sections.
- Prepare sampling plan, data-collection sheets, and field-visit forms.
- Conduct pre-audit meeting of the audit team (kick-off).

### **Stage 4: On-Site Audit Execution (Field Work)**
- **Opening Meeting** — Confirm scope, criteria, methodology, confidentiality.
- **Evidence Collection** — Document review, physical inspection, interviews, observation.
- **Findings Generation** — Match each evidence against audit criteria → Conformity / NC / Observation.
- **Closing Meeting** — Present preliminary findings to auditee management.

### **Stage 5: Audit Reporting**
- Compile the **Audit Report** with classified findings, root cause analysis, and risk prioritization.
- Distribute report to top management and relevant stakeholders within the agreed timeline (typically 2–4 weeks).

### **Stage 6: Follow-Up & Verification**
- Track **Corrective Action Plans (CAPs)** for every NC raised.
- Verify the effectiveness of corrective actions through a **Follow-Up Audit**.
- Close out NCs with objective evidence and management sign-off.

## 2.3 Core Audit Techniques

| Technique | Description | When Used |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| **Document Review** | Examine policies, permits, training records, log books | All audits |
| **Physical Inspection** | Walk-through of plant, machinery, PPE storage | All audits |
| **Interview Technique** | Structured questioning of workers, supervisors, managers | All audits |
| **Observation** | Watching work practices without interference | Process audits |
| **Sampling (Statistical)** | Selecting representative units from a large population | High-volume processes |
| **Trace-Back / Trace-Forward** | Following a process step-by-step in either direction | Root cause analysis |
| **Cross-Verification** | Triangulating evidence from 2+ sources (records + interview) | For Major NCs |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Quantitative Audit Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **frequently tested in KTU University Examinations** (Part B questions on Safety Performance Measurement).

### 2.4.1 Non-Conformity Rate (NCR)

$$
NCR = \frac{N_{NC}}{N_{Total}} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $N_{NC}$ = Number of non-conformities raised
- $N_{Total}$ = Total number of audit clauses / checklist items verified
- Unit: **Percentage (%)**

### 2.4.2 Audit Coverage Ratio (ACR)

$$
ACR = \frac{N_{Areas Audited}}{N_{Total Areas in Scope}} \times 100
$$

- Indicates how comprehensively the audit covered the declared scope.
- KTU benchmark: **ACR $\geq$ 85%** is considered acceptable.

### 2.4.3 Risk Priority Number (RPN) for Audit Findings

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity (1 to 10)
- $O$ = Occurrence / Probability (1 to 10)
- $D$ = Detection difficulty (1 to 10)
- **Threshold for "Critical NC"**: RPN $\geq$ 125 OR Severity $S \geq 9$

### 2.4.4 Audit Sample Size (Statistical Sampling)

For a large population, use the **N=5% sample rule** for routine audits:

$$
n = 0.05 \times N \quad \text{subject to} \quad n_{min} = 30
$$

For ISO 19011 compliant audits, use the **square root method** (preferred by TÜV/BSI):

$$
n = \lceil \sqrt{N} \rceil
$$

### 2.4.5 Audit Effectiveness Index (AEI)

$$
AEI = \frac{N_{NC Closed within T}}{N_{NC Raised}} \times \frac{1}{T_{avg}} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $N_{NC \, Closed \, within \, T}$ = NCs closed within target time $T$
- $T_{avg}$ = Average actual closure time (days)
- A higher AEI indicates better safety culture.

### 2.4.6 Cost of Non-Compliance (CoNC)

$$
CoNC = C_{Penalty} + C_{Legal} + C_{Production\,Loss} + C_{Reputation}
$$

- Used in management-level audit justification reports.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Audit Methodology

1. **Industrial Safety** — Pre-commissioning audits for hydrocarbon plants (oil & gas, petrochemical).
2. **Construction** — Monthly safety audits for high-rise and infrastructure projects.
3. **Fire Engineering** — Audits of fire detection, suppression, and evacuation systems.
4. **Manufacturing** — Compliance with ISO 45001 and ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) reporting.
5. **Aviation / Maritime** — ICAO and IMO mandatory safety audits.
6. **Mining** — DGMS (Directorate General of Mines Safety) compliance audits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Audit Clause Mapping (ISO 45001:2018 vs. Factories Act 1948)

| ISO 45001 Clause | Clause Title | Corresponding Factories Act Section | Audit Focus |
|------------------|--------------|--------------------------------------|-------------|
| 4.1 | Context of the Organization | Sec. 7A (Duties of Occupier) | Documented SMS scope |
| 4.2 | Needs & Expectations of Workers | Sec. 7A (b) | Worker consultation records |
| 5.1 | Leadership & Worker Participation | Sec. 40 (Safety Officer) | Top management commitment |
| 6.1.2 | Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment (HIRA) | Sec. 14, 35, 36 | HIRA register, JSA |
| 6.1.3 | Compliance Obligations | Schedules I–XII | Legal register, license copy |
| 7.5 | Documented Information | Sec. 11 (Cleanliness), Sec. 12 (Ventilation) | Updated SOPs |
| 8.1.2 | Emergency Preparedness | Sec. 36 (Fire), Sec. 38 (Emergency) | Mock drill records |
| 9.1.1 | Monitoring & Measurement | Sec. 73 (OHS Committee) | KPIs, accident records |
| 9.2 | Internal Audit | Sec. 40A (Safety Audit) | Annual audit report |
| 10.2 | Incident, Nonconformity & Corrective Action | Sec. 88 (Notice of Accident) | CAPA log |

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Calculating RPN and Classifying NC

> **Problem:** During a safety audit of a chemical reactor area, the auditor observed that the pressure relief valve (PRV) on Reactor R-101 has not been calibrated for **3 years**, against the statutory requirement of annual calibration. The reactor operates at **150 bar** with a release medium of **toxic H₂S**. The auditor assessed the parameters as:
> - Severity $S = 9$ (toxic release can cause fatality)
> - Occurrence $O = 4$ (PRV failure is uncommon but possible)
> - Detection $D = 7$ (failure is hard to detect without functional test)
>
> **Classify this audit finding and decide the corrective action priority.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the RPN formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN &= S \times O \times D \\
RPN &= 9 \times 4 \times 7 \\
RPN &= 252
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare against the criticality threshold:

- Threshold for Critical NC: $RPN \geq 125$ **OR** $S \geq 9$
- Here, $RPN = 252 \geq 125$ **AND** $S = 9 \geq 9$
- **Both conditions are satisfied → This is a CRITICAL (MAJOR) NC.**

**Step 3:** Classification logic:
- **Major NC** = Failure that may result in the probable shipment of unacceptable / hazardous product, OR a systemic procedural failure.
- The fact that the **entire process is non-compliant** with the calibration schedule (systemic lapse), and the **consequence is potentially fatal**, makes this a Major NC.

**Step 4:** Corrective action requirements:
- **Immediate (within 24 hours):** Plant shutdown, immediate calibration of PRV, and functional testing.
- **Short-term (within 7 days):** Update calibration tracker, add a SAP/EAM auto-trigger reminder.
- **Long-term (within 30 days):** Root Cause Analysis (e.g., 5-Why, Fishbone), and management review of calibration program.

**Step 5:** Verification at follow-up audit: Review calibration certificate (NABL/NPL traceable) and updated tracker.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Many students wrongly classify *any* RPN $\geq 100$ as Major. The correct KTU approach is the **OR condition**: RPN $\geq 125$ **OR** Severity $\geq 9$. Either alone is sufficient.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Audit Sample Size by Square Root Method

> **Problem:** A third-party auditor is auditing the welding department of a heavy-engineering plant. The department has **900 welders** on its rolls. The auditor wants to determine the **minimum sample size** to interview using the ISO 19011 square root method.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the population:

$$
N = 900 \text{ welders}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the square root formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \lceil \sqrt{N} \rceil \\
n &= \lceil \sqrt{900} \rceil \\
n &= \lceil 30.00 \rceil \\
n &= 30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation:
- The auditor must interview a **minimum of 30 welders**, distributed across shifts, weld types (SMAW, GTAW, GMAW), and experience levels (trainee, semi-skilled, certified).

**Step 4:** Sampling stratification (for richer evidence):
- Morning shift: 10 welders
- Afternoon shift: 10 welders
- Night shift: 10 welders

**Step 5:** Cross-verification:
- For each interviewed welder, **cross-check** with: welder qualification certificate, weld test record, and PPE issue register. This is the **triangulation rule** of audit evidence.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Non-Conformity Rate (NCR) Calculation

> **Problem:** An internal audit of the chemical plant covered 200 checklist items. The auditor raised 12 non-conformities, of which 3 were Major and 9 were Minor. Calculate NCR and comment.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the NCR formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCR &= \frac{N_{NC}}{N_{Total}} \times 100 \\
NCR &= \frac{12}{200} \times 100 \\
NCR &= 6.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Classify:
- $NCR \leq 2\%$ → Excellent SMS
- $2\% < NCR \leq 5\%$ → Acceptable
- $5\% < NCR \leq 10\%$ → Needs Improvement
- $NCR > 10\%$ → Systemic Failure

**Step 3:** Conclusion:
- With $NCR = 6.0\%$, the plant's SMS is in the **"Needs Improvement"** category.
- The presence of 3 Major NCs further escalates the seriousness — immediate management review is required.

**Step 4:** Major-to-Minor ratio:

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{3}{9} = 0.33
$$

A high Major-to-Minor ratio indicates **systemic gaps** (e.g., missing policies, untrained workforce, broken process), while a low ratio indicates **isolated lapses**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 6-Stage Audit Methodology — Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Audit Initiation and Scope Definition] --> B[Stage 2: Pre-Audit Planning and Document Review]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Preparation of Audit Tools and Checklists]
    C --> D[Stage 4: On-Site Audit Execution and Evidence Collection]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Audit Reporting and Findings Classification]
    E --> F[Stage 6: Follow-Up Verification and CAPA Closure]
    F --> G{Are All NCs Closed?}
    G -- No --> H[Re-Audit or Escalate to Top Management]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I[Audit Cycle Complete - Document and Archive]
    D --> D1[Opening Meeting]
    D --> D2[Field Inspection and Interviews]
    D --> D3[Closing Meeting]
    E --> E1[Major NC]
    E --> E2[Minor NC]
    E --> E3[Observation or OFI]
    D2 --> D2A[Document Review]
    D2 --> D2B[Physical Inspection]
    D2 --> D2C[Worker Interviews]
```

## 4.2 Audit Methodology — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        I1[Legal Requirements]
        I2[ISO 45001 Standards]
        I3[Organizational SMS Policy]
        I4[Previous Audit Reports]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS
        P1[Audit Planning Module]
        P2[Checklist Builder]
        P3[Auditor Competency Matrix]
        P4[Evidence Collection Engine]
        P5[NC Classification Engine]
        P6[Risk Prioritization RPN Calculator]
        P7[CAPA Tracker]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT
        O1[Audit Report]
        O2[NC Register]
        O3[Corrective Action Plan]
        O4[Follow-Up Schedule]
        O5[Management Review Input]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P5 --> O1
    P5 --> O2
    P6 --> O3
    P7 --> O4
    O1 --> O5
```

## 4.3 PDCA Cycle Mapped to Audit Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[PLAN: Define Audit Scope, Criteria, Schedule] --> D[DO: Conduct On-Site Audit and Collect Evidence]
    D --> C[CHECK: Compare Evidence Against Criteria and Classify NCs]
    C --> A[ACT: Implement CAPA and Verify Effectiveness]
    A --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:**
> The PDCA cycle (Deming cycle) is the philosophical backbone of every management system audit. When asked "How does audit methodology drive continuous improvement?", always anchor your answer to PDCA.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define safety audit. List any four types of safety audits.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

A **safety audit** is a systematic, independent, and documented evaluation of an organization's safety management system (SMS) to determine the extent to which audit criteria (statutory requirements, ISO standards, internal procedures) are being fulfilled.

**Four types of safety audits:**
1. **Compliance Audit** — Verifies adherence to legal/regulatory requirements.
2. **Management System Audit** — Evaluates SMS against ISO 45001 / OHSAS 18001.
3. **Process Audit** — Focuses on specific operational processes (e.g., welding, confined space).
4. **Product Audit** — Examines safety features of finished products or services.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing 4 types correctly = 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2: Distinguish between Major Non-Conformity and Minor Non-Conformity with one example each.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Major Non-Conformity | Minor Non-Conformity |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Definition** | Non-fulfillment of a requirement that has resulted in, or is likely to result in, a systemic failure or a hazardous product/service. | Non-fulfillment of a requirement that is an isolated lapse and does not indicate a systemic breakdown. |
| **Impact** | High — can cause fatality, regulatory shutdown, or loss of certification. | Low-to-moderate — does not immediately threaten safety. |
| **Example** | Pressure relief valve on a toxic reactor not calibrated for 3 years. | One welding operator's PPE inspection record missing for a single day. |
| **Action** | Immediate shutdown + RCA + management review. | Documented correction within 30 days. |

*(2 marks for distinction + 1 mark for examples = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write generic statements like "audits are important" without supporting them with **methodology steps or formulas**. Generic answers score below 7/14.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse **Audit** with **Inspection**. Audit is a *system-level, evidence-based, documented* process. Inspection is a *point-in-time visual check*.
> 3. **Always** support classifications with **RPN numerical calculations** — students who skip the numbers lose 2–3 marks easily.
> 4. **Always** mention **ISO 45001:2018** or **ISO 19011:2018** by name — examiners reward keyword recognition.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [Internal Choice 1] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

#### (a) Describe in detail the various phases of safety audit methodology. Explain the importance of the "Opening Meeting" and "Closing Meeting" in an audit cycle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Phases of Safety Audit Methodology (5 marks):**

1. **Audit Initiation & Scope Definition:** Define the purpose, boundaries, and criteria of the audit. Identify applicable standards (ISO 45001, Factories Act). Appoint the audit team.

2. **Pre-Audit Planning:** Review existing SMS documentation, prior audit reports, and legal register. Prepare a detailed **Audit Plan** with dates, locations, and persons to be interviewed.

3. **Preparation of Audit Tools:** Develop **Audit Checklists** mapped to ISO 45001 clauses. Prepare sampling plan and evidence-collection templates.

4. **On-Site Audit Execution:** Conduct the **Opening Meeting**, gather evidence through document review, physical inspection, and interviews. Classify findings into Conformity, NC (Major/Minor), or Observation.

5. **Audit Reporting:** Compile a structured **Audit Report** with executive summary, findings, RPN, and recommended CAPA. Distribute to top management.

6. **Follow-Up & Verification:** Track CAPA implementation, conduct follow-up audits, and close out NCs with documented evidence.

**Importance of Opening & Closing Meetings (2 marks):**

- **Opening Meeting:** Confirms scope, criteria, and methodology; introduces audit team; establishes communication channels; sets confidentiality rules; reduces auditee anxiety. Duration: typically 15–30 minutes.
- **Closing Meeting:** Presents preliminary findings; ensures auditee understands NCs; agrees on timelines for CAPA; provides a forum for clarification and dispute resolution. Duration: 30–60 minutes.

---

#### (b) Explain the concept of Risk Priority Number (RPN). An audit finding has S = 8, O = 5, D = 6. Calculate the RPN and classify the finding. Suggest a corrective action plan. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of RPN (2 marks):**

RPN is a quantitative tool used in audit methodology to prioritize findings based on their potential impact. It is calculated as:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity (1–10)
- $O$ = Occurrence (1–10)
- $D$ = Detection difficulty (1–10)

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN &= S \times O \times D \\
RPN &= 8 \times 5 \times 6 \\
RPN &= 240
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification (1 mark):**

- Threshold for Critical NC: $RPN \geq 125$ **OR** $S \geq 9$
- $RPN = 240 \geq 125$ → **Critical / Major Non-Conformity**

**Corrective Action Plan (2 marks):**

1. **Immediate (24 hours):** Stop the affected operation; isolate the hazard; notify line management.
2. **Short-term (7 days):** Conduct 5-Why root cause analysis; implement engineering or administrative control.
3. **Long-term (30 days):** Update SMS procedures; provide refresher training; add a detection mechanism (e.g., automatic alarm, sensor).
4. **Verification:** Document corrective action evidence; close out NC at follow-up audit.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [Internal Choice 2] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

#### (a) Discuss in detail the techniques used for evidence collection during a safety audit. Compare and contrast the document review technique with the interview technique. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Evidence Collection Techniques (4 marks):**

1. **Document Review** — Examination of policies, SOPs, training records, permits, log books, calibration certificates, and accident reports. Provides *written* evidence.

2. **Physical Inspection** — Walk-through of plant, machinery, storage, PPE, fire-fighting equipment, exit routes, and signage. Provides *visual* evidence.

3. **Interviews** — Structured or semi-structured questioning of workers, supervisors, and managers. Provides *oral* evidence of safety culture and competence.

4. **Observation** — Watching actual work practices (e.g., hot work, lifting, confined space entry) without interfering. Provides *behavioral* evidence.

5. **Sampling (Statistical)** — Selecting representative units for detailed review (e.g., square root method).

6. **Trace-Back / Trace-Forward** — Following a process step-by-step to verify integrity.

**Comparison: Document Review vs. Interview (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Document Review | Interview |
|--------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Nature of Evidence** | Written, recorded, archival | Verbal, real-time, subjective |
| **Strength** | Objective, traceable, time-stamped | Reveals gaps between "what is documented" and "what is practiced" |
| **Weakness** | May not reflect actual ground reality | Dependent on interviewee's honesty, recall, and willingness |
| **Triangulation Use** | Must be cross-verified by interview/observation | Must be cross-verified by document/observation |
| **Best Used For** | Compliance check, license verification | Culture assessment, competence check |

> **Conclusion:** Document review and interview are **complementary, not alternatives**. The most robust audits always triangulate evidence from at least 2 of the 3 sources: document + interview + observation.

---

#### (b) A factory employs 2500 workers across 3 shifts. An auditor needs to determine the minimum sample size for interviewing using the square root method. Also calculate the Audit Coverage Ratio if 18 out of 25 designated areas were audited. Comment on the adequacy of the audit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i): Sample Size by Square Root Method (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 2500 \text{ workers} \\
n &= \lceil \sqrt{N} \rceil \\
n &= \lceil \sqrt{2500} \rceil \\
n &= \lceil 50.00 \rceil \\
n &= 50
\end{aligned}
$$

The auditor must interview a **minimum of 50 workers**, distributed across the 3 shifts (~17 per shift).

**Part (ii): Audit Coverage Ratio (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACR &= \frac{N_{Areas Audited}}{N_{Total Areas}} \times 100 \\
ACR &= \frac{18}{25} \times 100 \\
ACR &= 72\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (iii): Comment on Adequacy (2 marks):**

- KTU benchmark for acceptable audit coverage: **$ACR \geq 85\%$**.
- Here, $ACR = 72\% < 85\%$ → **The audit is INADEQUATE in coverage.**
- 7 areas were not audited — these may include critical areas like **boiler house, effluent treatment plant, or hazardous chemical storage**.
- **Recommendation:** Either conduct a supplementary audit for the 7 missed areas, OR document a formal justification (with top management sign-off) for their exclusion, AND re-audit them in the next cycle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU University Exams**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Safety audit = systematic, independent, documented evaluation of SMS against audit criteria.
- ⭐ **Standards:** ISO 45001:2018 (SMS), ISO 19011:2018 (Audit Guidelines), ILO-OSH 2001, Factories Act 1948.
- ⭐ **6 Phases:** Initiation → Planning → Preparation → Execution → Reporting → Follow-up.
- ⭐ **3 Types by Origin:** First-party (internal), Second-party (customer), Third-party (certification body).
- ⭐ **3 Audit Techniques:** Document review, Interview, Observation (always triangulate).
- ⭐ **NC Classification:** Major (systemic, hazardous) vs. Minor (isolated lapse).
- ⭐ **RPN Formula:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$ — Critical threshold: $RPN \geq 125$ **OR** $S \geq 9$.
- ⭐ **Sample Size:** $n = \lceil \sqrt{N} \rceil$ (square root method) — minimum 30.
- ⭐ **ACR Formula:** $ACR = (Areas Audited / Total Areas) \times 100$ — benchmark $\geq 85\%$.
- ⭐ **NCR Formula:** $NCR = (N_{NC} / N_{Total}) \times 100$.
- ⭐ **PDCA Cycle:** Plan → Do → Check → Act — philosophical anchor of all SMS audits.
- ⭐ **Audit Report Must Include:** Scope, criteria, findings, evidence, RPN, CAPA, timelines, responsibility.
- ⭐ **CAPA = Corrective Action + Preventive Action** — every NC must have both, with a closure date.
- ⭐ **Auditor Competence:** Required per ISO 19011 Clause 7 — knowledge of standards, audit techniques, sector-specific competence, and personal attributes (ethical, open-minded, observant).
- ⭐ **Common Pitfall in Exam:** Writing about safety *inspections* instead of safety *audits* — these are NOT the same.

topics.title: Executing the Audit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT205 - PRINCIPLES OF SAFETY MANAGEMENT (SAFETY & FIRE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Executing the Audit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned)**
> *Executing the Audit* is the **fieldwork phase** of the safety audit cycle in which the auditor moves from the planning stage to the **on-site evidence-gathering stage**. It involves the systematic collection of verifiable audit evidence using recognized audit techniques (inspection, observation, inquiry, confirmation, recalculation, re-performance, and analytical review) in order to evaluate whether the auditee's Safety Management System (SMS) conforms to prescribed **statutory requirements (Factories Act 1948, OSHA, ISO 45001:2018, NFPA codes, OISD standards)** and to the auditee's own documented safety policies, procedures, and objectives.

In ISO 19011:2018 (Guidelines for auditing management systems) terminology, the execution phase is formally called the *"Conducting the Audit"* stage — encompassing the **Opening Meeting → Information Collection & Verification → Generation of Audit Findings → Preparation of Audit Conclusions → Closing Meeting** sequence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Safety Detective"**
> Think of the audit executor as a **medical diagnostician** examining a patient (the organization):
>
> | Doctor's Step | Auditor's Counterpart |
> |---|---|
> | Reviews patient's medical history | Reviews the Safety Policy, previous audit reports, incident records |
> | Conducts physical examination | Conducts **plant walk-through inspection** & observations |
> | Orders blood tests & scans | Performs **sampling, recalculation, and analytical review** of safety data |
> | Cross-questions the patient | Holds **interviews** with workers, supervisors, safety officers |
> | Writes a diagnosis report | Drafts **audit findings, non-conformities, and the final audit report** |
> | Prescribes treatment | Issues **Corrective Action Requests (CARs)** & recommendations |
>
> Just as a doctor cannot rely on the patient's *word alone* but must verify with **objective tests and physical evidence**, an auditor must not rely on management's verbal assurance but must **corroborate claims with documents, measurements, and on-site observations**.

## 1.3 Why "Executing the Audit" is the Heart of the Audit Cycle

The execution stage is where the **true compliance status** of an organization is revealed. Planning and reporting merely bookend this critical phase. Poor execution — incomplete evidence, biased sampling, or uncritical reliance on management assertions — compromises the entire audit, regardless of how thorough the planning was.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - Module 4)**
> Module 4 explicitly requires the student to understand:
> (a) Pre-execution preparation (audit programme, audit plan, checklists)
> (b) On-site evidence-gathering techniques
> (c) Audit sampling — statistical and judgmental
> (d) Audit tests — *Compliance Test* vs. *Substantive Test*
> (e) Working paper documentation standards
> (f) Materiality and Audit Risk determination
> (g) Generation of audit findings (conformity / non-conformity / observation)

## 1.4 Key Physical / Standard Metrics Used in Execution

| Metric | Typical Value / Benchmark | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Audit Risk (AR)** | Usually fixed at **5 %** or **10 %** | ISA 200 / 315 (adapted) |
| **Inherent Risk (IR)** | **High = 100 %**, Medium = 60 %, Low = 30 % | Auditor judgment |
| **Control Risk (CR)** | **High = 100 %**, Medium = 50 %, Low = 20 % | Auditor judgment |
| **Sample Confidence Level (Z)** | **1.96 (95 %)**, 2.58 (99 %) | Normal distribution |
| **Standard Materiality Threshold** | **5 % of pre-tax profit** or **0.5 %–1 % of revenue** | Industry norm (adapted) |
| **Audit Working Paper Retention** | Minimum **7 years** | ISO 19011 / Statutory |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Five-Phase Structure of Audit Execution

The execution phase, although appearing as a single continuous activity, is structurally composed of **five interlinked sub-phases**. Each sub-phase has distinct objectives, techniques, and deliverables.

### Phase A — Pre-Execution Preparation (Audit Programme & Plan)
- Review the **Audit Charter**, **Audit Programme** (multi-year), and the **Audit Plan** (specific engagement).
- Finalize the **Audit Checklist** based on ISO 45001:2018 clauses, statutory requirements, and the organization's own SMS.
- Assign audit team members, fix **materiality**, and pre-allocate working paper files.
- Send the **Audit Notification / Engagement Letter** to the auditee at least **2–4 weeks** in advance.

### Phase B — Opening Meeting
- Confirm audit scope, objectives, criteria, schedule, and confidentiality rules.
- Introduce audit team members and confirm escorts/guides.
- Resolve logistics — access permissions, safety PPE, restricted zones.

### Phase C — Fieldwork (The Core of Execution)
- Conduct **plant walk-downs**, **document reviews**, **interviews**, and **measurements**.
- Apply **audit sampling** to select transactions, incidents, or records for testing.
- Apply **audit tests** — Compliance and Substantive — on selected samples.
- Maintain contemporaneous **working papers**.

### Phase D — Analysis & Finding Generation
- Correlate evidence against audit criteria.
- Classify results into **Conformance (C)**, **Non-Conformance (NC)**, **Observation (O)**, or **Opportunity for Improvement (OFI)**.
- Determine **root cause** using 5-Why, Fishbone (Ishikawa), or Fault Tree methods.

### Phase E — Closing Meeting & Draft Report
- Present findings to top management.
- Discuss **Corrective Action Requests (CARs)** and agree on timelines.
- Issue the **Draft Audit Report**.

## 2.2 Seven Methods of Audit Evidence Collection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Seven Evidence-Gathering Techniques (ISA 500 / ISO 19011 Aligned)**
> 1. **Inspection** — Examination of records, documents, or physical assets.
> 2. **Observation** — Watching a process or control being performed.
> 3. **Inquiry** — Seeking information from knowledgeable persons (interview).
> 4. **Confirmation** — Obtaining a reply from an independent third party.
> 5. **Recalculation** — Verifying the arithmetical accuracy of records (e.g., cumulative noise dose, fire-load calculation).
> 6. **Re-performance** — Independently executing a procedure or control.
> 7. **Analytical Review** — Studying relationships among financial/operational data to identify unusual fluctuations.

> **Reliability Hierarchy (Most → Least Reliable):**
> *Re-performance > Recalculation > Inspection of documents > Observation > Inquiry (oral) > Inquiry (written)*

## 2.3 Audit Sampling — Statistical vs. Non-Statistical

| Parameter | Statistical Sampling | Non-Statistical (Judgmental) Sampling |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis of selection** | Probability (random) | Auditor's professional judgment |
| **Sample size** | Determined by formula | Determined by experience |
| **Objectivity** | High — quantifiable | Low — subject to bias |
| **Defensibility** | Defensible in court / third-party review | Less defensible |
| **Cost** | Higher (requires software) | Lower |
| **Use case** | Large populations, regulatory audits | Small populations, preliminary risk assessment |

### 2.3.1 Statistical Sample Size Formula (Infinite Population)
For estimating a proportion (e.g., compliance rate) in a large population:

$$
n \;=\; \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot q}{e^{2}}
$$

Where:
- $n$ = required sample size
- $Z$ = standard normal variate for chosen confidence level
- $p$ = expected population proportion (use 0.5 if unknown — gives maximum $n$)
- $q = 1 - p$
- $e$ = tolerable sampling error (margin of error)

### 2.3.2 Statistical Sample Size Formula (Finite Population Correction)
When population $N$ is small relative to $n$:

$$
n \;=\; \frac{N \cdot Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot q}{(N - 1)\,e^{2} \;+\; Z^{2}\,p\,q}
$$

### 2.3.3 Statistical Sampling — Attribute Sampling Table
For compliance audits, **attribute sampling** is used with the *Acceptable Risk of Incorrect Acceptance (ARIA)* and *Tolerable Rate of Deviation (TRD)*.

| TRD \ ARIA | 5 % | 10 % |
|---|---|---|
| 1 % | n = 299 | n = 230 |
| 2 % | n = 149 | n = 114 |
| 5 % | n = 59  | n = 45  |
| 10 % | n = 29 | n = 22 |

## 2.4 The Audit Risk Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Audit Risk (AR) Equation — Foundation of Modern Audit Planning**
>
> $$
> AR \;=\; IR \times CR \times DR
> $$
>
> Rearranged for sampling:
> $$
> DR \;=\; \frac{AR}{IR \times CR}
> $$

- **IR (Inherent Risk)** — susceptibility of an assertion to material error before considering controls.
- **CR (Control Risk)** — risk that internal controls fail to prevent/detect errors.
- **DR (Detection Risk)** — risk that the auditor's procedures fail to detect existing errors.
- **AR (Audit Risk)** — overall risk of issuing an inappropriate audit opinion.

## 2.5 Materiality Determination

**Materiality** is the magnitude of an omission or misstatement that influences the decision of a reasonable user of the safety audit report. Common formulas:

### 2.5.1 Quantitative Materiality
$$
M_{\text{overall}} \;=\; k \times \text{Benchmark (Profit / Revenue / Assets)}
$$

Typical benchmarks:
- **Profit-based**: $k = 5\%$
- **Revenue-based**: $k = 0.5\% \text{ to } 1\%$
- **Asset-based**: $k = 1\% \text{ to } 2\%$

### 2.5.2 Performance Materiality
$$
M_{\text{performance}} \;=\; (0.5 \text{ to } 0.75) \times M_{\text{overall}}
$$

### 2.5.3 Clearly Trivial Threshold
$$
M_{\text{trivial}} \;=\; 0.05 \times M_{\text{overall}}
$$

Any error below $M_{\text{trivial}}$ is disregarded.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is the only cheat-sheet required for solving Module 4 numerical questions in the KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n = \dfrac{Z^{2}\,p\,q}{e^{2}}$ | $n$ = sample size; $Z$ = 1.96 (95 %); $p, q$ = proportions; $e$ = error | Infinite population proportion |
| 2 | $n = \dfrac{N\,Z^{2}\,p\,q}{(N-1)\,e^{2} + Z^{2}\,p\,q}$ | $N$ = population size; rest as above | Finite population |
| 3 | $AR = IR \times CR \times DR$ | All in % or decimal | Audit risk model |
| 4 | $DR = \dfrac{AR}{IR \times CR}$ | Solve for detection risk | Reverse calculation |
| 5 | $M_{\text{overall}} = k \times \text{Benchmark}$ | $k$ = 5 %, 0.5 %, 1 %, 2 % | Planning materiality |
| 6 | $M_{\text{performance}} = 0.5 \text{ to } 0.75 \times M_{\text{overall}}$ | — | Working materiality |
| 7 | $M_{\text{trivial}} = 0.05 \times M_{\text{overall}}$ | — | Disregard threshold |
| 8 | Confidence Interval = $\hat{p} \pm Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$ | $\hat{p}$ = observed proportion | Estimating true compliance rate |
| 9 | Stratified mean = $\dfrac{\sum N_h \bar{x}_h}{\sum N_h}$ | $h$ = stratum; $N_h$ = stratum size | Stratified sampling |
| 10 | Allowance for Sampling Risk (ASR) = $\text{Point Estimate} - \text{Lower Limit}$ | — | Audit conclusion support |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> **Why does this matter in the field?**
> - **Industrial Hygiene Audits**: Sampling helps determine the number of personal noise dosimeter readings to be taken across a noisy plant to be 95 % confident that the population mean is below 85 dB(A) Threshold Limit Value (TLV).
> - **Fire Safety Audits**: Attribute sampling is applied to check whether fire extinguishers across a large facility (say 500 units) comply with annual maintenance — the auditor samples ~45 extinguishers (TRD 5 %, ARIA 10 %) and projects compliance to the entire population.
> - **Process Safety Audits (PSM / OISD)**: Materiality is used to prioritize critical equipment for re-validation — non-critical deviations below the trivial threshold are aggregated and reported, not chased individually.
> - **Construction Safety Audits**: The Audit Risk Model is used to assign audit hours to high-hazard activities (working at height, confined space, hot work) versus low-hazard activities (administrative offices).

---

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Statistical Sample Size for a Fire Extinguisher Audit

### Problem Statement
A safety auditor is conducting a fire-safety audit of a manufacturing plant. The plant has **N = 600** portable fire extinguishers. The auditor wishes to use statistical sampling to estimate the proportion of extinguishers that have undergone the mandated annual maintenance check. Assume:

- Confidence level = **95 %** → Z = 1.96
- Tolerable sampling error $e$ = **0.05**
- Expected proportion $p$ = 0.5 (most conservative, since prior data is unavailable)
- $q = 1 - p = 0.5$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the formula (finite population).**

Because the sample size may exceed 5 % of $N$, we use the finite-population correction:

$$
n \;=\; \frac{N \cdot Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot q}{(N - 1)\,e^{2} \;+\; Z^{2}\,p\,q}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

$$
n \;=\; \frac{600 \times (1.96)^{2} \times 0.5 \times 0.5}{(600 - 1) \times (0.05)^{2} \;+\; (1.96)^{2} \times 0.5 \times 0.5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

First, evaluate $Z^{2} = (1.96)^{2} = 3.8416$.

$$
\text{Numerator} \;=\; 600 \times 3.8416 \times 0.25
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} \;=\; 600 \times 0.9604 \;=\; 576.24
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator.**

First term: $(N - 1)\,e^{2} = 599 \times 0.0025 = 1.4975$.

Second term: $Z^{2}\,p\,q = 3.8416 \times 0.25 = 0.9604$.

$$
\text{Denominator} \;=\; 1.4975 + 0.9604 \;=\; 2.4579
$$

**Step 5 — Divide numerator by denominator and round up.**

$$
n \;=\; \frac{576.24}{2.4579} \;\approx\; 234.46
$$

$$
\boxed{n \;=\; 235 \text{ fire extinguishers (rounded up)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for the Field**
> The auditor must physically inspect **235 extinguishers** out of 600 to be 95 % confident that the true compliance rate is within ±5 percentage points of the observed rate. The finite-population correction (235 vs. 384 for infinite population) saves nearly **149 inspections**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Audit Risk Model (Detection Risk Calculation)

### Problem Statement
The auditor has assessed the following risks for a hazardous chemical storage area:

- Audit Risk (AR) = **5 %** (set by the audit firm's policy)
- Inherent Risk (IR) = **80 %** (highly hazardous chemicals — flammable, toxic)
- Control Risk (CR) = **40 %** (some controls exist, e.g., bund walls, but historical near-misses indicate gaps)

Calculate the **Detection Risk (DR)** and interpret it.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the Audit Risk equation.**

$$
AR \;=\; IR \times CR \times DR
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for DR.**

$$
DR \;=\; \frac{AR}{IR \times CR}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
DR \;=\; \frac{0.05}{0.80 \times 0.40}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
0.80 \times 0.40 \;=\; 0.32
$$

**Step 5 — Divide.**

$$
DR \;=\; \frac{0.05}{0.32} \;=\; 0.15625
$$

$$
\boxed{DR \;\approx\; 15.63\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**
> Because IR and CR are already high (combined 32 %), the auditor only needs to perform procedures that detect the remaining **15.63 %** of residual risk. A low DR implies the auditor must design **more rigorous substantive procedures** — for example, larger sample sizes, re-performance of critical safety tests, and independent re-calculation of inventory reconciliation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Materiality Determination for an SMS Audit

### Problem Statement
A medium-scale chemical company reports the following for FY 2024-25:

- **Revenue** = ₹ 80 crore
- **Pre-tax Profit** = ₹ 6 crore
- **Total Assets** = ₹ 50 crore

The auditor uses the following industry-standard benchmarks:

- **Profit-based** materiality factor = 5 %
- **Revenue-based** materiality factor = 0.5 %
- **Asset-based** materiality factor = 1 %

Compute **Overall Materiality** using the *smallest* of the three (most conservative), then derive **Performance Materiality** and **Clearly Trivial Threshold**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the three candidate materialities.**

$$
M_{\text{profit}} = 5\% \times 6 = 0.30 \text{ crore} = \text{₹ }30\text{ lakh}
$$

$$
M_{\text{revenue}} = 0.5\% \times 80 = 0.40 \text{ crore} = \text{₹ }40\text{ lakh}
$$

$$
M_{\text{asset}} = 1\% \times 50 = 0.50 \text{ crore} = \text{₹ }50\text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 2 — Select the smallest (most conservative).**

$$
M_{\text{overall}} \;=\; \min\{30,\ 40,\ 50\} \text{ lakh} \;=\; \text{₹ }30\text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 3 — Derive Performance Materiality (use 0.5–0.75 factor).**

$$
M_{\text{performance}} = 0.75 \times 30 = \text{₹ }22.5\text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive Clearly Trivial Threshold (5 % of overall).**

$$
M_{\text{trivial}} = 0.05 \times 30 = \text{₹ }1.5\text{ lakh}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Field Interpretation**
> Any *individual* safety non-conformity whose financial / risk impact exceeds **₹ 22.5 lakh** must be reported individually. Errors between **₹ 1.5 lakh and ₹ 22.5 lakh** are aggregated. Errors below **₹ 1.5 lakh** are deemed trivial and not pursued.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Confidence Interval for an Observed Compliance Rate

### Problem Statement
After inspecting **235 fire extinguishers** (from Example 3.1), the auditor finds **$x = 200$** extinguishers are compliant.

Compute the **95 % confidence interval** for the true compliance rate.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the observed proportion.**

$$
\hat{p} \;=\; \frac{x}{n} \;=\; \frac{200}{235} \;\approx\; 0.8511 \;\;(85.11\%)
$$

**Step 2 — State the confidence interval formula.**

$$
CI \;=\; \hat{p} \;\pm\; Z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}\,(1 - \hat{p})}{n}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$
CI \;=\; 0.8511 \;\pm\; 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.8511 \times 0.1489}{235}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the standard error.**

$$
0.8511 \times 0.1489 = 0.12674
$$

$$
\frac{0.12674}{235} = 0.000539
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.000539} \;\approx\; 0.02322
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by Z.**

$$
1.96 \times 0.02322 \;\approx\; 0.0455
$$

**Step 6 — Form the interval.**

$$
\text{Lower} = 0.8511 - 0.0455 = 0.8056
$$

$$
\text{Upper} = 0.8511 + 0.0455 = 0.8966
$$

$$
\boxed{CI_{95\%} \;\approx\; (80.56\%,\ 89.66\%)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Field Interpretation**
> The auditor can state with **95 % confidence** that the true proportion of compliant fire extinguishers in the population lies between **80.56 % and 89.66 %**. If the regulator's minimum compliance threshold is, say, 90 %, then the auditor must flag a **non-conformance** because the upper limit (89.66 %) is below 90 %.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Audit Test

| Audit Situation | Recommended Test | Sampling Approach | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Verifying whether monthly fire drills are conducted | **Compliance Test** | Attribute sampling (TRD 5 %, ARIA 10 %) | Tests existence/occurrence of a control |
| Verifying that the cumulative noise dose of workers is below TLV | **Substantive Test (Re-performance)** | Stratified sampling by department | Tests the *outcome* — actual exposure level |
| Verifying accuracy of the LTI (Lost Time Injury) frequency rate | **Substantive Test (Recalculation)** | Random sampling of incident reports | Tests arithmetical accuracy of an assertion |
| Verifying that hot-work permits are issued before work | **Compliance Test** | 100 % inspection (small population) | High inherent risk — exhaust population |
| Verifying the relationship between man-hours and reportable incidents | **Substantive Test (Analytical Review)** | Not applicable — full population | Uses trend analysis, not sample |
