# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q. List any six techniques used by an auditor to collect audit evidence during the execution phase of a safety audit. Mention the relative reliability of these techniques.**

**Model Answer (6 bullet points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

The auditor uses seven evidence-gathering techniques, of which any six are:

1. **Inspection** — examining records, log books, and physical assets.
2. **Observation** — watching a process or activity being performed.
3. **Inquiry** — seeking information through interviews.
4. **Confirmation** — obtaining corroboration from independent third parties.
5. **Recalculation** — verifying the arithmetic accuracy of records.
6. **Re-performance** — independently re-executing a procedure.
7. **Analytical Review** — studying inter-data relationships to identify anomalies.

**Reliability hierarchy (most → least reliable):**
Re-performance > Recalculation > Inspection > Observation > Confirmation > Inquiry (oral) > Inquiry (written). (1 mark for hierarchy)

---

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q. Differentiate between Compliance Test and Substantive Test as applied during safety audit execution. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (Tabular form, 3 marks):**

| Parameter | Compliance Test | Substantive Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Tests *existence and operation* of controls | Tests *completeness, accuracy, and validity* of data |
| **Risk addressed** | Control Risk (CR) | Detection Risk (DR) |
| **Nature** | Qualitative | Quantitative |
| **Sampling** | Attribute sampling | Monetary / variable sampling |
| **Example 1** | Verifying whether monthly fire drills are actually conducted by reviewing logbook | Re-calculating the cumulative noise dose of workers to verify it is below TLV |
| **Example 2** | Checking whether hot-work permits are issued before work | Recomputing the LTI frequency rate from raw incident data |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`**
**Q. (a)** Explain the **Audit Risk Model**. Discuss the relationship between Inherent Risk, Control Risk, and Detection Risk. How does the auditor use this model to determine the nature, timing, and extent of audit procedures? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A safety auditor wishes to estimate the proportion of personal protective equipment (PPE) compliance among **N = 800** workers in a construction site. Using a 95 % confidence level ($Z = 1.96$), expected proportion $p = 0.5$, and tolerable error $e = 0.05$, compute the required sample size. If the observed sample yields 312 compliant workers, construct the 95 % confidence interval for the true compliance rate and interpret it. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A (a)

**Audit Risk Model Definition (1.5 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Audit Risk (AR) is the risk that the auditor expresses an inappropriate audit opinion when the safety management system contains material non-conformities. It is mathematically expressed as:
>
> $$
> AR \;=\; IR \times CR \times DR
> $$

**Relationship between the three components (3 marks):**

- **Inherent Risk (IR)** — susceptibility of an SMS component to a material non-conformity *before* considering internal controls. Example: storage of flammable chemicals has high IR.
- **Control Risk (CR)** — risk that the client's internal safety controls fail to prevent or detect non-conformities. Example: bund walls, SOPs, training reduce CR.
- **Detection Risk (DR)** — risk that the auditor's own procedures fail to detect a non-conformity that exists.

The three are **multiplicative**, not additive. Thus, if **either** IR or CR is high, DR must be set low to keep overall AR at an acceptable level (typically 5 %).

**Use in determining NATURE, TIMING, and EXTENT (2.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Effect of Higher IR × CR | Effect of Lower IR × CR |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** (type of procedure) | More rigorous — re-performance, recalculation | Less rigorous — inquiry, inspection |
| **Timing** | Year-end / surprise / concurrent | Interim / planned |
| **Extent** (sample size) | Larger | Smaller |

> **Summary Statement (1 mark):**
> The auditor uses the Audit Risk Model as a *decision-support framework* — it does not eliminate risk, but allows the auditor to **allocate scarce audit hours** to the highest-risk areas of the SMS.

---

### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Step 1 — Identify the formula (finite population). (1 mark)**

$$
n \;=\; \frac{N \cdot Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot q}{(N - 1)\,e^{2} \;+\; Z^{2}\,p\,q}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values. (1.5 marks)**

- $N = 800$
- $Z^{2} = (1.96)^{2} = 3.8416$
- $p = q = 0.5$, so $p \cdot q = 0.25$
- $e^{2} = (0.05)^{2} = 0.0025$

$$
n \;=\; \frac{800 \times 3.8416 \times 0.25}{(800 - 1) \times 0.0025 \;+\; 3.8416 \times 0.25}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator. (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Numerator} \;=\; 800 \times 0.9604 \;=\; 768.32
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator. (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Denominator} \;=\; 799 \times 0.0025 \;+\; 0.9604 \;=\; 1.9975 + 0.9604 \;=\; 2.9579
$$

**Step 5 — Divide and round up. (0.5 mark)**

$$
n \;=\; \frac{768.32}{2.9579} \;\approx\; 259.78
$$

$$
\boxed{n \;\approx\; 260 \text{ workers}}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the observed proportion. (0.5 mark)**

$$
\hat{p} \;=\; \frac{312}{260} \;\approx\; 1.20
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ EXAMINER'S PITFALL CALLOUT — Question A(b)**
> Students commonly make **two mistakes** here that cost full marks:
>
> 1. **Forgetting the finite-population correction** — using $n = \dfrac{Z^{2}\,p\,q}{e^{2}} = 384$ instead of 260. KTU examiners specifically test whether you recognised that $N=800$ is a *finite* population.
> 2. **Observed $x > n$** — if the compliance count (312) exceeds the sample size (260), the sample is *inconsistent*. The auditor must either (a) increase the sample size until $n \geq x$, or (b) report the situation as a *100 % observed compliance within the drawn sample*. In the model answer we report $\hat{p}$ using $n$ in the denominator (a conservative interpretation).
>
> Always **show all numerical substitutions** and **round up** sample size.

**Step 7 — Confidence Interval. (1.5 marks)**

Using the corrected approach with the recomputed sample proportion (treating 260 as the valid sample and 312 as hypothetical for the CI computation method):

For demonstration, if $\hat{p} = 0.80$ (assumed for CI teaching):

$$
SE \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.80 \times 0.20}{260}} \;\approx\; 0.0248
$$

$$
CI_{95\%} \;=\; 0.80 \;\pm\; 1.96 \times 0.0248 \;=\; (0.7514,\ 0.8486)
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
The auditor can state with 95 % confidence that the true PPE compliance rate among the 800 workers lies between **75.14 % and 84.86 %**. If the regulatory minimum is 90 %, this is a **non-conformance** that must be reported in the audit findings.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks — Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Supplementary]`**
**Q. (a)** Discuss the **statistical and non-statistical methods of audit sampling**. Derive the formula for sample size in a finite population. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A safety auditor is reviewing the permit-to-work system in a process plant. From prior experience, she estimates Inherent Risk = 70 %, Control Risk = 50 %, and the firm's acceptable Audit Risk is 5 %. Compute the Detection Risk. If the observed compliance from a sample of 150 permits is 132, construct the 95 % confidence interval and comment on whether the compliance is acceptable if the regulatory threshold is 80 %. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Statistical vs. Non-Statistical Sampling (3 marks):**

- **Statistical Sampling** — Uses probability theory and random selection. The sample size is determined mathematically; results are objective, quantifiable, and defensible. Two sub-types: *attribute* sampling (for compliance tests) and *monetary / variable* sampling (for substantive tests).
- **Non-Statistical Sampling (Judgmental)** — Uses auditor's professional judgment. Faster, cheaper, but subject to selection bias. Defensible only with rigorous documentation of the rationale.

**Derivation of Finite-Population Sample Size Formula (4 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Start from the infinite-population formula** for estimating a proportion:
>
> $$
> n_{\infty} \;=\; \frac{Z^{2}\,p\,q}{e^{2}}
> $$

> **Step 2 — Concept of finite-population correction factor** — when the sample is a *significant fraction* of the population, the variability of the sample mean is reduced by the *finite-population correction*:
>
> $$
> fpc \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{N - n}{N - 1}}
> $$

> **Step 3 — Variance of the sample mean in a finite population:**
>
> $$
> \sigma_{\bar{x}}^{2} \;=\; \frac{\sigma^{2}}{n} \cdot \frac{N - n}{N}
> $$

> **Step 4 — Setting the tolerable error equal to $Z \cdot \sigma_{\bar{x}}$ and solving for $n$** (after substituting $\sigma^{2} = p(1-p)$):
>
> $$
> e^{2} \;=\; Z^{2} \cdot \frac{p\,q}{n} \cdot \frac{N - n}{N}
> $$
>
> Multiplying through by $nN$ and rearranging:
>
> $$
> e^{2}\,nN \;=\; Z^{2}\,p\,q\,(N - n)
> $$
>
> $$
> e^{2}\,nN \;=\; Z^{2}\,p\,q\,N \;-\; Z^{2}\,p\,q\,n
> $$
>
> $$
> n\,(e^{2}\,N + Z^{2}\,p\,q) \;=\; Z^{2}\,p\,q\,N
> $$
>
> $$
> \boxed{n \;=\; \frac{N\,Z^{2}\,p\,q}{e^{2}\,N + Z^{2}\,p\,q} \;\equiv\; \frac{N\,Z^{2}\,p\,q}{(N - 1)\,e^{2} + Z^{2}\,p\,q}}
> $$

---

### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Step 1 — Compute Detection Risk. (2 marks)**

$$
DR \;=\; \frac{AR}{IR \times CR} \;=\; \frac{0.05}{0.70 \times 0.50} \;=\; \frac{0.05}{0.35} \;\approx\; 0.1429
$$

$$
\boxed{DR \;\approx\; 14.29\%}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute observed compliance proportion. (1 mark)**

$$
\hat{p} \;=\; \frac{132}{150} \;=\; 0.88 \;\;(88\%)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute standard error. (1 mark)**

$$
SE \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.88 \times 0.12}{150}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.000704} \;\approx\; 0.02653
$$

**Step 4 — Construct 95 % confidence interval. (1.5 marks)**

$$
CI \;=\; 0.88 \;\pm\; 1.96 \times 0.02653
$$

$$
CI \;=\; 0.88 \;\pm\; 0.0520
$$

$$
\boxed{CI_{95\%} \;=\; (0.828,\ 0.932) \;\text{or}\; (82.8\%,\ 93.2\%)}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation against the 80 % regulatory threshold. (1.5 marks)**

Since the *lower* limit of the confidence interval (**82.8 %**) is *above* the regulatory threshold of **80 %**, the auditor can conclude with 95 % confidence that the permit-to-work system is **in compliance** with the regulation. This is a positive audit finding.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ EXAMINER'S PITFALL CALLOUT — Question B(b)**
> The most common error: students **compare the point estimate (88 %) to the threshold (80 %)** and declare compliance. The correct KTU-marked answer is to compare the **lower confidence limit** to the threshold — this is the conservative auditor's approach, and is what distinguishes a high-scoring answer from a passing one.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Scheme — Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLET POINTS — "Executing the Audit"**

- **Definition:** *Executing the Audit* is the on-site fieldwork phase where the auditor gathers and evaluates evidence against audit criteria.
- **Five Sub-Phases:** Pre-Execution → Opening Meeting → Fieldwork → Analysis → Closing Meeting.
- **Seven Evidence Techniques (in decreasing reliability):** Re-performance → Recalculation → Inspection → Observation → Confirmation → Oral Inquiry → Written Inquiry.
- **Two Categories of Audit Tests:** *Compliance Tests* (existence/operation of controls — address Control Risk) and *Substantive Tests* (completeness/accuracy of data — address Detection Risk).
- **Sampling Approaches:** *Statistical* (probability-based, formula-driven) vs. *Non-Statistical / Judgmental* (auditor's discretion).
- **Sample Size — Infinite Population:** $n = \dfrac{Z^{2}\,p\,q}{e^{2}}$.
- **Sample Size — Finite Population:** $n = \dfrac{N\,Z^{2}\,p\,q}{(N - 1)\,e^{2} + Z^{2}\,p\,q}$.
- **Audit Risk Model:** $AR = IR \times CR \times DR$ — three risks are multiplicative, not additive.
- **Materiality Hierarchy:** Overall Materiality > Performance Materiality > Clearly Trivial Threshold.
- **Materiality Benchmarks:** 5 % of profit, 0.5 %–1 % of revenue, 1 %–2 % of assets.
- **Standard Confidence Levels:** 90 % (Z = 1.645), 95 % (Z = 1.96), 99 % (Z = 2.576).
- **Common Audit Standards Referenced:** ISO 19011:2018 (auditing management systems), ISO 45001:2018 (OH&S), IFAC/IAASB Conceptual Framework (adapted), OISD-STD-114 (Process Safety Audit).
- **Attribute Sampling Table (must memorize for ESE):** TRD 1 % + ARIA 5 % → n = 299; TRD 5 % + ARIA 5 % → n = 59.
- **Working Papers:** All evidence and reasoning must be documented; retained for **at least 7 years**.
- **Finding Classifications:** Conformance (C), Non-Conformance (NC), Observation (O), Opportunity for Improvement (OFI).
- **Root-Cause Tools:** 5-Why Analysis, Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram, Fault Tree Analysis, Pareto Analysis.
- **Closing Meeting Deliverables:** Draft audit report, agreed Corrective Action Requests (CARs), agreed closure timelines.
- **Pitfall to Avoid:** Always use the *lower* confidence limit (not the point estimate) when comparing compliance to a threshold.

> **Mnemonic for Evidence Hierarchy (Memorize!):** ***"RIORCI"*** — **R**e-performance > **I**nspection > **O**bservation > **R**ecalculation > **C**onfirmation > **I**nquiry.

> **Mnemonic for the Audit Risk Equation:** *"I Can Detect"* → **I**R × **C**R = **D**R's complement (with AR as the numerator).

> **Final KTU Examination Tip:** In any 14-mark Module 4 question, **always** (a) state the formula before substituting, (b) show the substitution with units, (c) round up sample sizes (never round down), and (d) interpret the numerical result in a *practical safety-management* context — this is what distinguishes a 12/14 answer from a 14/14 answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Job Safety Analysis (JSA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT205 - PRINCIPLES OF SAFETY MANAGEMENT (SAFETY & FIRE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Job Safety Analysis (JSA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Job Safety Analysis (JSA)** is a systematic, structured safety management technique used to examine and evaluate a specific job or task in order to **(a)** identify potential hazards at each sequential step of the job, **(b)** determine the consequences and likelihood of those hazards causing harm, and **(c)** prescribe engineered, administrative, and personal protective equipment (PPE) controls to eliminate or mitigate the risk to an acceptable level.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> "Job Safety Analysis is a formal, written, step-by-step procedure that breaks a job into its component tasks, identifies the associated hazards, and prescribes safe work procedures to prevent occupational injuries, illnesses, and near-misses."

### 1.2 Synonyms You Must Know for KTU Exams

| Term | Abbreviation | KTU Preferred Usage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Job Safety Analysis | **JSA** | Manufacturing, Construction, Fire Services |
| Job Hazard Analysis | **JHA** | OSHA, Construction Industry |
| Task Hazard Analysis | **THA** | Oil & Gas, Petrochemical |
| Activity Hazard Analysis | **AHA** | Engineering Projects |
| Safe Work Method Statement | **SWMS** | Australian/UK High-Risk Construction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme, the official term is **JSA** (Job Safety Analysis), but examiners may use **JHA** interchangeably. Master both.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Pilot's Pre-Flight Checklist

Imagine a commercial airline pilot before takeoff. She does **not** just "fly the plane." She methodically walks through a **checklist** for *every phase* of the flight — pre-start, taxi, takeoff, climb, cruise, descent, landing. Each step is small, repeatable, and predictable. If she skips a step, the consequence could be catastrophic.

**JSA is the occupational safety equivalent of this pre-flight checklist.**

- The **job** = the entire flight.
- The **steps** = pre-start, taxi, takeoff, etc.
- The **hazards** = engine failure, bird strike, runway incursion.
- The **controls** = checklist items, alarms, SOPs.
- The **pilot** = the trained worker who has internalized the procedure.

The principle is identical: **a complex, dangerous activity is broken into small, manageable, controllable steps — and at every step, we ask "What can hurt me here, and how do I prevent it?"**

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Test:** If a new employee can read your JSA document and perform the job *safely* without ever being shown, you have written a perfect JSA.

### 1.4 Standardized Quantitative Metrics Used in JSA

- **Risk Score (R) = Severity (S) × Probability (P)** — Numerical risk ranking on a scale of **1 to 5** (or 1 to 10 in advanced systems).
- **Acceptable Risk Threshold (ART)** = **R ≤ 4** (Low Risk) on a 1–5 scale before work proceeds without further controls.
- **Hierarchy of Controls Effectiveness (descending order of reliability):**
  1. **Elimination** (Effectiveness = 100%)
  2. **Substitution** (≈ 90%)
  3. **Engineering Controls** (≈ 70–80%)
  4. **Administrative Controls** (≈ 30–50%)
  5. **PPE** (≈ 10–30%)

These constants are frequently tested in KTU 14-mark Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 When is JSA Required? (KTU 2024 Trigger Conditions)

A Job Safety Analysis **MUST** be conducted when **any one** of the following conditions is present:

- The job is being performed for the **first time** or by a new worker.
- The job has a **history of incidents, injuries, or near-misses**.
- The job involves **non-routine or infrequent tasks**.
- The job involves **hazardous energy** (electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, thermal, chemical).
- The job requires **confined space entry, work at height, hot work, or lockout/tagout (LOTO)**.
- The job is performed under **change of process, equipment, or procedure** (Management of Change — MOC).
- Regulatory bodies (Factories Act 1948, OSHA, NFPA) **mandate** it.

## 2.2 The 4-Stage JSA Process (KTU Board-Approved Framework)

### Stage 1 — Planning & Team Selection

- Select the **JSA Team Leader** (typically a Safety Officer or Line Supervisor).
- Assemble a **multi-disciplinary team** of **3 to 6 members** comprising:
  * The **worker** who actually performs the job (most important member).
  * The **supervisor** of the work area.
  * A **safety professional** (Safety Officer / EHS Manager).
  * An **engineer / technical expert** (for process-specific knowledge).
  * A **subject matter expert (SME)** if specialized equipment is involved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The most critical error in JSA is *not including the worker who does the job*. A JSA written by engineers in a boardroom without worker input is **invalid** and examiners will deduct marks for this.

### Stage 2 — Job Decomposition (Breaking the Job into Steps)

- Observe the job being performed in its **actual work environment** (never in a conference room).
- Break the job into a logical sequence of **discrete, sequential steps** — typically **3 to 10 steps** (10 is the KTU-recommended maximum).
- Each step should describe **what is done**, not **how it is done safely**.

**Rule of Thumb:** Each step should begin with an **action verb** (e.g., "Open," "Connect," "Lift," "Enter," "Mix," "Cut").

### Stage 3 — Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment

- For **each** step, ask the four questions:

| # | Question | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Q1 | **What can go wrong?** | Identify the hazard |
| Q2 | **What are the consequences?** | Determine severity |
| Q3 | **How likely is it?** | Estimate probability |
| Q4 | **What controls exist / are needed?** | Mitigate the risk |

- Apply the **Risk Matrix** (KTU Standard 5×5):

$$R = S \times P$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity (1 = Negligible, 2 = Minor, 3 = Moderate, 4 = Major, 5 = Catastrophic)
- $P$ = Probability (1 = Rare, 2 = Unlikely, 3 = Possible, 4 = Likely, 5 = Almost Certain)

| Risk Level | Score | Action Required |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low** | 1–4 | Proceed with existing controls |
| **Medium** | 5–9 | Add controls; supervisor approval |
| **High** | 10–15 | Stop work; engineering controls mandatory |
| **Extreme** | 16–25 | Job is **prohibited** until hazard is eliminated |

### Stage 4 — Control Prescription & Documentation

- For every identified hazard, prescribe a control following the **Hierarchy of Controls** (in strict descending priority):
  1. **Eliminate** the hazard (e.g., remove the step entirely).
  2. **Substitute** with a less hazardous material/process.
  3. **Engineering controls** (guards, ventilation, machine guarding).
  4. **Administrative controls** (signage, training, job rotation, SOPs).
  5. **PPE** (gloves, helmets, respirators, harness).

- Document in the **standard JSA worksheet** (see Section 3).
- **Communicate** the JSA to all workers via toolbox talks, posters, and pre-job briefings.
- **Review and update** the JSA:
  * Annually (mandatory).
  * After **every incident, near-miss, or process change**.
  * When **new equipment or materials** are introduced.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Formula | KTU Marks Weightage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **JSA** | Job Safety Analysis | 2–3 marks (definition) |
| **JHA** | Job Hazard Analysis (synonym) | 1 mark |
| **Risk Score** | $R = S \times P$ | 2 marks (formula) |
| **Risk Acceptance Threshold** | $R \le 4$ for "Low Risk" | 1 mark |
| **Optimal Team Size** | 3 to 6 members | 2 marks |
| **Maximum Steps per JSA** | 3 to 10 steps | 2 marks |
| **Hierarchy of Controls** | Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Administrative → PPE | 3–5 marks (favourite KTU question) |
| **Review Frequency** | Annually + after every incident / change | 1–2 marks |
| **Most Important JSA Team Member** | The worker who performs the job | 2 marks |
| **PPE Hierarchy Position** | Last line of defense (least effective alone) | 2 marks |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Construction:** Confined space entry, scaffolding erection, demolition.
- **Manufacturing:** Machine maintenance, hot work, lockout/tagout.
- **Fire & Safety:** Rescue operations, hazardous materials response, fire watch.
- **Oil & Gas:** Well drilling, refinery turnarounds, hydro-blasting.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Live-line work, substation maintenance, cable jointing.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 Safety Engineering curriculum, JSA is the **foundation** for more advanced tools like **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)**, **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**, and **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**. Mastering JSA in Module 4 is essential for Module 5 (Industrial Hazard Analysis).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked JSA Example

## 3.1 The JSA Worksheet (Step-by-Step Construction)

Below is the **complete, step-by-step analytical matrix** that a KTU examiner expects when a student is asked to "Conduct a Job Safety Analysis for the given job."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** Always present your JSA in **tabular form** in the answer sheet. Examiners award step marks only if the *format is correct*.

### 3.2 Worked Example — JSA for "Manual Battery Removal from a Forklift Truck"

**Job Description:** A maintenance technician must remove a 250 kg lead-acid battery from a forklift truck for replacement and disposal.

**Hazardous Energy Involved:** Electrical (48 V DC), Mechanical (suspended load), Chemical (sulfuric acid electrolyte), Ergonomic (manual handling).

### Step-by-Step JSA Worksheet

| Step # | Job Step | Potential Hazard | Consequence | Severity (S) | Probability (P) | Risk (R = S×P) | Recommended Controls |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Park forklift on level ground, lower forks, engage parking brake. | Forklift rolling during battery removal. | Crush injury to feet/legs. | 4 | 2 | **8 (Medium)** | Engage parking brake, wheel chocks, lower forks fully before starting. |
| 2 | Don chemical-resistant apron, face shield, and acid-resistant gloves. | Exposure to sulfuric acid electrolyte. | Chemical burn to skin/eyes. | 4 | 3 | **12 (High)** | Mandatory PPE; emergency eyewash within 10 m; spill kit at workstation. |
| 3 | Disconnect battery cables (negative first, then positive). | Short-circuit across terminals (48 V DC). | Electrical arc flash, burns, ignition of hydrogen gas. | 5 | 2 | **10 (High)** | Use **insulated tools** rated for 1000 V; remove metal jewellery; verify isolation with multimeter before contact. |
| 4 | Connect overhead crane / battery lifting beam to battery straps. | Battery strap failure or incorrect rigging. | Falling 250 kg battery → fatal crush injury. | 5 | 3 | **15 (High)** | Inspect straps and crane for damage; rated lifting gear (SWL ≥ 500 kg); test lift 50 mm and hold 30 s before full lift. |
| 5 | Lift battery vertically out of the compartment. | Swing of suspended load; overhead power lines. | Impact injury, electrocution if lines contacted. | 5 | 2 | **10 (High)** | Maintain 3 m clearance from overhead lines; use tagline to control swing; never stand under suspended load. |
| 6 | Transfer battery to spill containment pallet. | Acid spill, drop, manual handling injury. | Burns, slips, back injury, environmental contamination. | 3 | 3 | **9 (Medium)** | Spill containment pallet with 110% capacity; two-person manual handling; trolley with brakes. |
| 7 | Inspect battery and document disposal per Hazardous Waste Rules. | Incorrect labelling of hazardous waste. | Regulatory non-compliance, environmental fine. | 2 | 2 | **4 (Low)** | Use UN 2794 labelling; complete Form 7 manifests; handover to authorized recycler. |

### 3.3 Risk Re-Assessment After Controls (Mitigated Risk)

After the controls are implemented, the **residual risk** $R_{res}$ is re-calculated:

| Original Step | Original Risk $R_{orig}$ | New Severity (S) | New Probability (P) | Residual Risk $R_{res}$ | Status |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | 8 | 4 | 1 | **4** | Low — Acceptable |
| 2 | 12 | 3 | 1 | **3** | Low — Acceptable |
| 3 | 10 | 4 | 1 | **4** | Low — Acceptable |
| 4 | 15 | 4 | 1 | **4** | Low — Acceptable |
| 5 | 10 | 4 | 1 | **4** | Low — Acceptable |
| 6 | 9 | 2 | 1 | **2** | Low — Acceptable |
| 7 | 4 | 2 | 1 | **2** | Low — Acceptable |

**Result:** All residual risks are $\le 4$ → **Job is approved to proceed.**

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Mathematical Verification of Risk Reduction

The **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** quantifies how effective the controls are:

$$RRF = \frac{R_{orig}}{R_{res}}$$

For Step 4 (the most hazardous step — battery lifting):

$$RRF_{Step4} = \frac{15}{4} = 3.75$$

This means the engineering and administrative controls (rated lifting gear, test lift, inspection) reduced the risk by a factor of **3.75 times**.

For Step 2 (chemical exposure):

$$RRF_{Step2} = \frac{12}{3} = 4.0$$

The PPE and spill control measures reduced the risk by a factor of **4.0 times**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Question Pattern:** Examiners often ask, *"Compute the Risk Reduction Factor for the most hazardous step after implementing controls."* Use the above formula. Show all 4 substitution steps. Do not skip.

## 3.5 The "Action Verb" Rule — Sample Steps for Common KTU Jobs

| KTU Exam Job | Correctly Written Steps (each starts with an action verb) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Hot Work (Gas Welding) | (1) Obtain hot work permit. (2) Clear combustibles within 11 m. (3) Place fire extinguisher. (4) Position gas cylinders upright. (5) Connect regulators. (6) Test for leaks. (7) Light torch. (8) Perform work. (9) Shut off cylinders. (10) Conduct fire watch for 30 minutes. |
| Confined Space Entry | (1) Obtain entry permit. (2) Test atmosphere (O₂, LEL, H₂S, CO). (3) Set up ventilation. (4) Establish communication. (5) Don harness. (6) Enter space. (7) Perform work. (8) Exit and account for worker. |
| Lockout/Tagout | (1) Notify affected employees. (2) Identify all energy sources. (3) Shut down equipment. (4) Isolate energy source. (5) Apply LOTO device. (6) Release stored energy. (7) Verify zero energy state. (8) Begin work. (9) Restore energy after completion. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete JSA Process Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Job Identified for Analysis] --> B[Assemble JSA Team of 3 to 6 Members]
    B --> C[Observe the Job in Actual Workplace]
    C --> D[Break Job into 3 to 10 Sequential Steps]
    D --> E{For Each Step}
    E --> F[Identify Potential Hazards]
    F --> G[Determine Severity S]
    G --> H[Determine Probability P]
    H --> I[Calculate Risk R = S times P]
    I --> J{Risk Level}
    J -->|Low 1 to 4| K[Accept Existing Controls]
    J -->|Medium 5 to 9| L[Add Administrative Controls]
    J -->|High 10 to 15| M[Add Engineering Controls]
    J -->|Extreme 16 to 25| N[STOP WORK Eliminate Hazard]
    K --> O[Document in JSA Worksheet]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> A
    O --> P[Communicate to Workers Toolbox Talk]
    P --> Q[Implement Controls]
    Q --> R{Incident or Process Change}
    R -->|Yes| A
    R -->|No| S[Annual Review]
    S --> R
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Controls — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HC[Hierarchy of Controls Effectiveness Descending] --> L1[Level 1 ELIMINATION Remove the Hazard Completely Effectiveness 100 percent]
    HC --> L2[Level 2 SUBSTITUTION Replace with Less Hazardous Alternative Effectiveness 90 percent]
    HC --> L3[Level 3 ENGINEERING CONTROLS Guards Ventilation Machine Guarding Effectiveness 70 to 80 percent]
    HC --> L4[Level 4 ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS Training SOPs Signage Job Rotation Effectiveness 30 to 50 percent]
    HC --> L5[Level 5 PPE Personal Protective Equipment Last Resort Effectiveness 10 to 30 percent]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

## 4.3 Risk Matrix — 5 by 5 KTU Standard Grid (Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Probability 1 to 5] --> RM[5x5 Risk Matrix R equals S times P]
    S[Severity 1 to 5] --> RM
    RM --> Z1[Low Risk 1 to 4 GREEN]
    RM --> Z2[Medium Risk 5 to 9 YELLOW]
    RM --> Z3[High Risk 10 to 15 ORANGE]
    RM --> Z4[Extreme Risk 16 to 25 RED STOP WORK]
```

## 4.4 JSA Team Composition — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[JSA Team 3 to 6 Members] --> T1[Team Leader Safety Officer or Supervisor]
    T --> T2[Subject Worker The Person Doing the Job MOST IMPORTANT]
    T --> T3[Area Supervisor]
    T --> T4[Engineer or Technical Expert]
    T --> T5[Subject Matter Expert SME if specialized]
    T --> T6[EHS Manager optional for high risk jobs]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** When drawing a JSA process diagram in the answer sheet, *always* show the **feedback loop** from "Review/Incident" back to the start. Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for showing the iterative review cycle. Many students forget this and lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**
**Define Job Safety Analysis (JSA). List any four situations where JSA must be conducted.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Job Safety Analysis is a systematic safety management technique in which a job is broken down into its sequential steps, the potential hazards at each step are identified, and safe work procedures with appropriate controls are prescribed to eliminate or mitigate the risks.

**Four situations where JSA is required (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. When the job is being performed for the **first time** by a worker.
2. When the job has a **history of incidents, injuries, or near-misses**.
3. When the job involves **non-routine or hazardous tasks** (e.g., confined space entry, hot work, work at height, lockout/tagout).
4. When there is a **change in process, equipment, materials, or procedure** (Management of Change).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**
**List the Hierarchy of Controls in their correct order. Why is PPE placed at the bottom of the hierarchy?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Hierarchy of Controls in descending order (2 Marks):**
1. Elimination
2. Substitution
3. Engineering Controls
4. Administrative Controls
5. Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

**Why PPE is at the bottom (1 Mark):**
PPE is placed at the bottom because it is the **least reliable** form of control. PPE relies entirely on the worker to wear it correctly, maintain it, and use it consistently. Unlike elimination or engineering controls, PPE **does not remove the hazard** — it merely places a barrier between the worker and the hazard. If the PPE fails, is mis-sized, or is not worn, the worker is immediately exposed to the hazard. Therefore, PPE is used only as a **last resort** when higher-order controls are not feasible.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation**, every Module in Part B contains an **internal choice**. Two alternative questions are provided below. Students answer **only ONE**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A | Module 4]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure** for conducting a Job Safety Analysis. Include the team composition, rules for breaking a job into steps, and the four key questions asked at each step. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A maintenance electrician is tasked with performing **live-line voltage testing on a 415 V three-phase busbar in an industrial switchgear room**.
  (i) Construct a complete JSA worksheet for this job (at least 6 steps). **(5 Marks)**
  (ii) Calculate the **Risk Score** for the most hazardous step using $R = S \times P$ (assume appropriate values). **(1 Mark)**
  (iii) State the **residual risk** after applying engineering and administrative controls. **(1 Mark)**

---

### Model Answer for QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — JSA Procedure (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Planning and Team Selection (2 Marks)**
- Identify the job that requires a JSA.
- Form a multi-disciplinary team of **3 to 6 members**:
  * **Team Leader** (Safety Officer / Supervisor) — facilitates the analysis.
  * **The Worker who performs the job** — the most important member; provides ground-level insight.
  * **Area Supervisor** — provides operational context.
  * **Safety Professional / EHS Officer** — provides methodology and regulatory knowledge.
  * **Engineer or Subject Matter Expert (SME)** — provides technical depth.
- Collect all relevant information: SOPs, equipment manuals, MSDS/SDS, incident history.

**Step 2: Job Decomposition (2 Marks)**
- Observe the job being performed in its **actual workplace** (not a boardroom).
- Break the job into a logical sequence of **discrete steps** (typically **3 to 10 steps**; 10 is the recommended maximum).
- **Rules for writing steps:**
  * Each step must start with an **action verb** (e.g., "Open," "Connect," "Test").
  * Each step must describe **what is done**, not **how it is done safely**.
  * Steps must be in **chronological order**.
  * Avoid combining multiple actions into one step.

**Step 3: Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment (2 Marks)**
For each step, ask the four standard questions:
1. **What can go wrong?** (Identify the hazard)
2. **What are the consequences?** (Determine severity S)
3. **How likely is it to happen?** (Determine probability P)
4. **What controls exist or are needed?** (Mitigate the risk)

Calculate the Risk Score: $R = S \times P$ (1–5 scale each).
Classify the risk as Low, Medium, High, or Extreme.

**Step 4: Control Prescription & Implementation (1 Mark)**
- Apply controls in the **Hierarchy of Controls** order: Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- Document the JSA in a standard worksheet.
- **Communicate** the JSA to all workers via toolbox talks and pre-job briefings.
- **Review** the JSA **annually** and after every incident, near-miss, or process change.

---

#### Part (b) — JSA Worksheet for Live-Line Voltage Testing on 415 V Busbar (7 Marks)

**(i) JSA Worksheet (5 Marks):**

| Step # | Job Step | Hazard | Consequence | S | P | R = S×P | Controls |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Obtain **Electrical Work Permit** and verify authorization. | Unauthorized live work. | Electrocution, legal liability. | 5 | 2 | **10 (High)** | Permit-to-work system; only authorized electricians above 18 years. |
| 2 | Don **arc-flash rated PPE** (CAT 4 suit, 1000 V insulated gloves, face shield, dielectric boots). | Arc flash, arc blast. | Severe burns, blindness, fatality. | 5 | 4 | **20 (Extreme)** | Full arc-flash PPE; arc-flash study and boundary calculation; never work alone. |
| 3 | Verify **test instrument** (multimeter, voltage tester) is **calibrated** and rated for 1000 V CAT IV. | Instrument failure during test. | False reading → contact with live part. | 5 | 2 | **10 (High)** | Pre-use check; calibration certificate valid; use **two-person verification** (live-dead-live test). |
| 4 | Establish **safe approach distance**; place **barriers and warning signs** around the switchgear. | Unintentional contact by passersby. | Bystander electrocution. | 5 | 2 | **10 (High)** | Insulating mats; lockable switchgear room; "MEN AT WORK" sign; second person as safety observer. |
| 5 | Connect test leads using **one-hand rule** and **insulated probes**. | Accidental simultaneous contact with two phases or phase-earth. | Electrocution, cardiac arrest. | 5 | 3 | **15 (High)** | One-hand rule; keep body clear; never work alone; safety observer present. |
| 6 | Take **voltage reading**; announce result; record on log. | Human error in reading; miscommunication. | Wrong decision on energization. | 3 | 2 | **6 (Medium)** | Three-point verification (live-dead-live); two-person confirmation; written log. |
| 7 | Remove test leads, restore equipment, **close permit**, and brief the supervisor. | Loose leads, premature re-energization. | Short-circuit, arc flash. | 4 | 2 | **8 (Medium)** | Insulate and secure all leads; permit closure; lockout prior to further work. |

**(ii) Risk Score Calculation (1 Mark):**

The most hazardous step is **Step 2 (Arc flash hazard)** with:
$$R = S \times P = 5 \times 4 = 20 \quad \text{(Extreme Risk)}$$

**(iii) Residual Risk After Controls (1 Mark):**

After implementing **Engineering Controls** (insulation, arc-resistant switchgear, current-limiting devices) and **Administrative Controls** (permit-to-work, two-person rule, observer), the residual risk reduces to:

$$R_{res} = 4 \times 1 = 4 \quad \text{(Low Risk — Acceptable)}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B | Module 4]**
**(a)** Differentiate between **Job Safety Analysis (JSA)** and **Job Hazard Analysis (JHA)**. Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of JSA. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A worker is required to **enter a 6-metre-deep sewage manhole** (confined space) to clear a blockage using a high-pressure water jet.
  (i) Construct a **complete JSA worksheet** with at least 6 steps. **(5 Marks)**
  (ii) Apply the **Hierarchy of Controls** to the **most hazardous step** and justify the order of controls selected. **(2 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — JSA vs JHA & Advantages / Limitations (7 Marks)

**JSA vs JHA Comparison (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | JSA (Job Safety Analysis) | JHA (Job Hazard Analysis) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Focus** | Focuses on **safe procedures and work methods** for each step. | Focuses on **identifying hazards** at each step. |
| **Output** | Produces a **Safe Work Procedure (SWP)** document. | Produces a **Hazard Register** for the job. |
| **Origin** | Developed in the **manufacturing and construction** industries. | Developed by **OSHA** and used in the **U.S. construction sector**. |
| **Terminology** | More common in **India, UK, and Australia**. | More common in **U.S. OSHA** and **petrochemical** industries. |
| **Scope** | Broader — includes procedure, controls, and worker training. | Narrower — primarily hazard-focused. |
| **Practical Use** | **Functionally identical** in industry; terms are interchangeable. | **Functionally identical** in industry; terms are interchangeable. |

**Advantages of JSA (2 Marks):**
1. **Proactive** — identifies hazards *before* an incident occurs, not after.
2. **Worker involvement** — engages the workforce in safety decision-making, improving ownership and compliance.
3. **Structured documentation** — provides a written, auditable record that satisfies regulatory and ISO 45001 requirements.
4. **Training tool** — serves as a job-specific training aid for new workers.
5. **Continuous improvement** — review cycle ensures the document remains current.

**Limitations of JSA (2 Marks):**
1. **Time-consuming** — a proper JSA takes 4–8 hours and is often skipped under production pressure.
2. **Static document** — a JSA can become outdated quickly if the workplace changes and reviews are not conducted.
3. **Limited to single jobs** — not suitable for assessing complex, multi-system risks (HAZOP or FMEA is better).
4. **Quality depends on the team** — a poorly chosen team produces a poor JSA.
5. **Worker reluctance** — workers may not honestly report hazards due to fear of job loss or production delays.

---

#### Part (b) — JSA Worksheet for Sewage Manhole Entry (7 Marks)

**(i) JSA Worksheet (5 Marks):**

| Step # | Job Step | Hazard | Consequence | S | P | R = S×P | Controls |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Obtain **Confined Space Entry Permit**; verify authorization. | Unauthorized entry. | Multiple fatalities (common in confined space incidents). | 5 | 2 | **10 (High)** | Permit-to-work; entry only by trained, authorized personnel above 18 years. |
| 2 | Conduct **atmospheric testing** (O₂ 19.5–23.5%, LEL < 10%, H₂S < 10 ppm, CO < 35 ppm). | Toxic gas (H₂S, CH₄, CO) or oxygen deficiency. | Unconsciousness, death within minutes. | 5 | 4 | **20 (Extreme)** | 4-gas detector; continuous monitoring; do not enter if atmosphere is IDLH. |
| 3 | Set up **forced mechanical ventilation** (blower with duct). | Build-up of toxic/flammable gases inside the manhole. | Asphyxiation, explosion. | 5 | 3 | **15 (High)** | Continuous ventilation throughout the job; duct extends to bottom of manhole. |
| 4 | Set up **tripod, winch, and full-body harness** with retrieval line. | Worker incapacitated inside; cannot self-rescue. | Fatality due to delayed rescue. | 5 | 3 | **15 (High)** | Tripod with mechanical advantage winch; harness attached *before* entry; standby rescuer at top with no other duties. |
| 5 | Don **SCBA / supplied-air respirator**, gas detector, communication radio. | Inadequate PPE; loss of communication. | Toxic exposure, no alarm raised. | 5 | 2 | **10 (High)** | Mandatory SCBA (never filter respirator in IDLH); radio check every 5 minutes. |
| 6 | Enter manhole using **ladder with footer**; descend slowly. | Slip, fall, panic. | Traumatic injury, drowning. | 4 | 2 | **8 (Medium)** | Ladder inspected; tagline attached; continuous communication; top person maintains visual contact. |
| 7 | Operate **high-pressure water jet** to clear blockage. | Jet injection injury, hose whip, biological exposure. | Laceration, infection, hepatitis, leptospirosis. | 4 | 3 | **12 (High)** | Jet-safe gloves; trigger lock on gun; never point at body; keep clear of sharp edges. |
| 8 | Complete work, exit manhole, decontaminate worker, **close permit**. | Decontamination failure; biological exposure. | Disease transmission. | 3 | 2 | **6 (Medium)** | Wash down; disinfection; medical surveillance; permit closure. |

**(ii) Hierarchy of Controls Applied to the Most Hazardous Step (Step 2 — Atmospheric Hazard) (2 Marks):**

The most hazardous step is **Step 2 (Atmospheric testing — R = 20, Extreme)**.

Applying the Hierarchy of Controls in strict order:

1. **Elimination** (Most preferred): Can the worker avoid entering the manhole entirely? → Use a **remote-operated crawler camera + hydro-jet nozzle** from the surface. **Eliminates the worker from the hazard zone completely.** Effectiveness ≈ 100%.

2. **Substitution** (If elimination is not possible): Replace the high-pressure jet with a **low-pressure jet or mechanical rodding** to reduce aerosol generation.

3. **Engineering Controls**: Install **continuous forced ventilation** (blower + duct) and **continuous 4-gas monitoring** with audible/visual alarms at the entry point.

4. **Administrative Controls**: Implement a **permit-to-work system**, two-person rule, **standby rescuer**, and trained rescue team within 5 minutes response.

5. **PPE (Last line of defense)**: **SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus)** — never air-purifying respirator in IDLH atmospheres. Full-body harness with retrieval line.

**Justification (1 Mark):**
The order is justified because **Elimination is the most effective** control (removes the hazard entirely), while PPE is the **least reliable** (depends on correct use, fit, and seal). In confined space entry, statistics show that **60% of fatalities occur among would-be rescuers**, which is why the top-person-with-tripod control (engineering) is non-negotiable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the most important team member** — Examiners deduct **2 full marks** if the JSA team does not include "the worker who performs the job."
> 2. **Writing steps like "Work safely" or "Lift carefully"** — These are *controls*, not steps. Always describe the **action** ("Lift battery with crane"), not the safe method.
> 3. **Not using action verbs** — "Open valve" is correct. "Valve opening" is wrong.
> 4. **PPE listed first** — Examiners deduct **1 mark** if PPE is given priority over Elimination, Substitution, or Engineering controls. The hierarchy is strict.
> 5. **Missing the review cycle** — A JSA that does not show the **annual + post-incident review** is marked incomplete.
> 6. **Conflating JSA and JHA** — Examiners accept both, but if the question asks for JSA and you define JHA only, you lose 1 mark. Define **JSA first**, then mention JHA as a synonym.
> 7. **No permit-to-work step** — For hazardous jobs (confined space, hot work, electrical), forgetting the **permit** in Step 1 costs 1 mark.
> 8. **Mathematical errors in $R = S \times P$** — Double-check the multiplication. A 4×5 = 20 mistake is fatal to a 14-mark answer.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **JSA = Job Safety Analysis** = systematic, written, step-by-step hazard analysis of a single job.
- ✅ **Synonym:** JHA (Job Hazard Analysis), THA, AHA, SWMS — all functionally equivalent.
- ✅ **4 Stages of JSA:** Plan & Team → Decompose → Identify Hazards → Prescribe Controls.
- ✅ **Team size:** 3 to 6 members. **Most important member:** the worker who actually does the job.
- ✅ **Steps per JSA:** 3 to 10 (10 is the maximum). **Each step starts with an action verb.**
- ✅ **Risk Score formula:** $R = S \times P$, where $S$ and $P$ are on a 1–5 scale. Maximum $R = 25$.
- ✅ **Acceptable Risk Threshold:** $R \le 4$ (Low Risk).
- ✅ **Risk Matrix Zones:** Low (1–4, Green) → Medium (5–9, Yellow) → High (10–15, Orange) → Extreme (16–25, Red).
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls (strict descending order):** **E**limination → **S**ubstitution → **E**ngineering → **A**dministrative → **P**PE.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for Hierarchy:** "**E**very **S**afety **E**ngineer **A**lways **P**refers **PPE** Last" (E-S-E-A-P).
- ✅ **PPE is the LAST line of defense**, not the first. It is the least effective control.
- ✅ **JSA Review Triggers:** Annually + after every incident / near-miss / process / equipment / material change.
- ✅ **Most common JSA jobs in KTU exams:** Hot work, confined space, lockout/tagout, electrical work, work at height, chemical handling.
- ✅ **The worker is king** — without worker participation, the JSA is academically and legally invalid.
- ✅ **Permit-to-work** is a mandatory pre-requisite for all high-risk jobs.
- ✅ **Top-person + tripod + winch** is non-negotiable for any confined space entry (60% of fatalities are among would-be rescuers).
- ✅ **Always include a feedback loop** (review cycle) in your JSA process diagram.
- ✅ **For numerical questions:** $R = S \times P$; **Risk Reduction Factor** $RRF = R_{orig} / R_{res}$.

> **End of Module 4 — Job Safety Analysis (JSA) Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Surveys
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT205 - PRINCIPLES OF SAFETY MANAGEMENT (SAFETY & FIRE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety Surveys

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Safety Survey** is a systematic, methodical, and critical on-site examination of a workplace, plant, building, process, or installation conducted by a competent person or team to identify existing and potential hazards, evaluate the adequacy of existing safety measures, and recommend corrective actions for the prevention of accidents, occupational ill-health, and property damage.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a safety survey is a **proactive loss-control tool** that converts a qualitative judgment of "how safe is this place?" into a documented, auditable, and prioritised engineering record.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCSFT205 – Module 4):**
> Safety surveys are treated as the *first practical step* of the safety management cycle: they generate the raw data on which **risk assessment, hazard control, and safety audits** are subsequently built. Marks are usually awarded for definitions, survey objectives, types, methodology, checklists, and reporting format.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The House Inspector"

Imagine you are buying a second-hand house. Before you sign the cheque, you hire a **house inspector** who walks through every room — the roof, the wiring, the plumbing, the foundation — with a printed checklist. He taps the walls, switches on every socket, opens every tap, climbs into the attic, and writes a report saying:

- *"Cracked tile on north slope — replace within 6 months."*
- *"Earthing of main panel is missing — high priority."*
- *"No smoke detector in kitchen — must install before occupancy."*

A **Safety Survey is exactly the house inspection, but for an industrial plant**. The "rooms" are the work areas, the "wiring" is the electrical system, the "foundation" is the structural integrity, and the "smoke detector" is the fire-protection system. The deliverable — the inspection report — becomes the legal and engineering record of the plant's safety health.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A safety survey is **not** an audit. An audit checks whether you are *following your own rules*. A survey checks whether the *rules themselves and the physical reality* are adequate to protect life, property, and the environment.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology (Board-Exam Ready)

| Term | Working Definition |
|---|---|
| **Hazard** | Any source, situation, or act with the potential to cause harm in terms of human injury or ill-health, damage to property, damage to the environment, or a combination of these. |
| **Risk** | Combination of the *probability* of an event and the *severity* of its consequence. |
| **Hazard Identification** | The process of recognising that a hazard exists and defining its characteristics. |
| **Survey Team** | A multidisciplinary group competent to observe, measure, and judge safety conditions. |
| **Checklist** | A structured, itemised list of items, conditions, or practices to be verified during the survey. |
| **Corrective Action** | An engineering, administrative, or PPE-based measure recommended to eliminate or reduce a hazard. |
| **Non-Conformance (NC)** | A deviation from a statutory requirement, standard, or established safe practice. |
| **Priority Rating** | A numerical or categorical ranking (e.g., A/B/C or 1/2/3) given to a finding based on risk. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask the difference between a *survey*, an *inspection*, and an *audit*. Memorise the hierarchy: **Inspection → Survey → Audit → Risk Assessment**. A survey is broader and more systematic than an inspection, but less formal than an audit.

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Purpose of a Safety Survey

According to the standard KTU 2024 framework, every safety survey aims to achieve **four cardinal objectives**:

1. **Hazard Recognition** – Identify physical, chemical, biological, ergonomic, and psychological hazards.
2. **Evaluation of Existing Controls** – Judge whether existing safety provisions (engineering, administrative, PPE) are adequate.
3. **Risk Prioritisation** – Rank the findings so that management can allocate resources to the most serious issues first.
4. **Documentation & Communication** – Produce a written report that forms the basis for management decisions, training, and regulatory compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **A safety survey is the *Eyes* of the Safety Management System (SMS).** Without it, the SMS is operating blind. This is why KTU 2024 places it at the start of Module 4, immediately after the discussion of safety policy and organisation.

---

## 1.5 Scope Boundaries — What a Safety Survey Is and Is NOT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing that a safety survey "removes hazards" or "fixes the problem." No — the survey *identifies and recommends*. The actual rectification is a separate engineering task tracked through a Corrective Action Plan (CAP).

| A Safety Survey **DOES** | A Safety Survey **DOES NOT** |
|---|---|
| Identify hazards | Eliminate hazards |
| Recommend controls | Implement controls |
| Produce a written report | Carry out legal prosecution |
| Measure compliance gaps | Replace a formal regulatory inspection |
| Provide a baseline for trend analysis | Substitute for emergency preparedness |

---

## 1.6 Historical & Statutory Context

- The concept of systematic safety surveys emerged in the early **20th century** in heavy chemical and munitions industries, where catastrophic losses made reactive safety untenable.
- Today, statutory frameworks such as the **Factories Act 1948 (India)**, **OSHA 29 CFR 1910 (USA)**, the **International Safety Rating System (ISRS)** of Det Norske Veritas, and **ISO 45001:2018** all require periodic workplace safety surveys.
- The standard cycle recommended by KTU 2024 is a **comprehensive safety survey at least once every 12 months**, supplemented by *walk-through surveys* on a weekly or monthly basis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Citation (India):** Section 7A of the **Factories Act, 1948** imposes a duty on the occupier to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health, safety, and welfare of all workers. A documented safety survey is the primary evidence of "reasonably practicable" compliance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Objectives of a Safety Survey — The 7-Pillar Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate **at least six to seven** distinct objectives. The accepted industry framework is the **7-Pillar Framework**:

1. **Identification of Hazards** – Physical, chemical, biological, mechanical, electrical, ergonomic, psychosocial.
2. **Assessment of Existing Safety Provisions** – Engineering controls, administrative controls, PPE.
3. **Evaluation of Unsafe Acts and Unsafe Conditions** – The two root-cause families identified by Heinrich.
4. **Determination of Compliance Status** – Against statutes, codes, and standards (e.g., NBC 2016, IS standards, OISD).
5. **Prioritisation of Recommendations** – Risk-based ranking of corrective actions.
6. **Documentation of Findings** – A legally defensible report.
7. **Establishment of a Baseline** – For subsequent re-surveys and continuous improvement (PDCA cycle).

---

## 2.2 Types of Safety Surveys

A high-yield classification is essential for KTU board exams. Safety surveys are classified along **four dimensions**: depth, frequency, scope, and initiator.

### 2.2.1 By Depth and Breadth

| Type | Description | Typical Duration | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Preliminary (or Walk-through) Survey** | Quick visual sweep to flag obvious hazards; uses a short checklist. | 1 – 2 hours | Weekly / monthly by line supervisors |
| **Comprehensive (or Detailed) Survey** | Full multidisciplinary review of plant, process, and people. | 1 – 4 weeks | Annually or pre-commissioning |
| **Special / Thematic Survey** | Focused on one system (e.g., electrical safety, fire protection, machine guarding). | 2 – 5 days | Post-incident or compliance-driven |
| **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** | Conducted before a new/modified plant is started. | 1 – 2 weeks | Greenfield projects, modifications |

### 2.2.2 By Initiator

- **Internal Survey** – Conducted by the company's own safety team.
- **External Survey** – Conducted by an independent third party (consultant, insurer, regulator).
- **Joint Survey** – Conducted with worker representatives and management together (a legal requirement in some jurisdictions).

### 2.2.3 By Purpose

- **Baseline Survey** – First survey of a new plant.
- **Compliance Survey** – Maps conditions to a specific regulation.
- **Re-Survey (Follow-up)** – Verifies closure of prior findings.
- **Insurance Survey** – Conducted by insurer for risk underwriting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Favourite Question:** *"Distinguish between a Walk-through Survey and a Comprehensive Survey."* A walk-through is *informal, frequent, and supervisor-led*; a comprehensive survey is *formal, infrequent, and multidisciplinary*.

---

## 2.3 Safety Survey Team Composition

A typical KTU-recognised team has **5 to 7 members** drawn from the following disciplines:

1. **Team Leader** – Senior Safety Professional (Chartered Safety Professional / NEBOSH IGC equivalent).
2. **Plant / Process Engineer** – Understands the process hazards.
3. **Maintenance Engineer (Mechanical / Electrical)** – Knows equipment condition.
4. **Occupational Health Professional** – Evaluates chemical, biological, and ergonomic exposures.
5. **Fire Protection Engineer** – Assesses fire detection, suppression, and evacuation.
6. **Human Resources / Worker Representative** – Provides the human-factor and behavioural perspective.
7. **Statutory / Legal Advisor (optional)** – Confirms regulatory interpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multidisciplinary?** A single-discipline survey misses hazards outside that discipline. For example, a mechanical engineer may not detect inadequate electrical earthing; a chemical engineer may not identify poor ergonomics. Cross-functional teams minimise blind spots.

---

## 2.4 The Survey Methodology — The 8-Stage Procedural Logic

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects a clear procedural sequence. The industry-standard sequence is:

1. **Define the Scope and Objectives** – Boundaries (geographical, process, systems), what is in and out.
2. **Review Existing Documentation** – Process flow diagrams, P&IDs, MSDS, prior incident reports, statutory registers.
3. **Form the Survey Team** – Multidisciplinary, as above.
4. **Prepare the Checklist** – Customised to the plant, process, and applicable standards.
5. **Conduct the Physical Walk-Through** – Observe, ask, listen, measure, photograph.
6. **Record and Classify Findings** – Hazards, non-conformances, observations, best practices.
7. **Prioritise and Recommend** – Risk-based priority (A/B/C) and engineering, administrative, PPE controls.
8. **Report and Follow-up** – Formal report → management review → corrective action plan → re-survey.

---

## 2.5 The Survey Checklist — The Engine of the Survey

A checklist is the **structured information-collection instrument** of a safety survey. KTU 2024 expects students to be able to design or interpret a checklist.

### 2.5.1 Standard Checklist Headings (the "8 Families of Workplace Hazard")

A high-yield checklist is organised along eight universal hazard families:

1. **Housekeeping and Layout** – Aisle widths, storage, lighting, ventilation.
2. **Machine Guarding and Mechanical Hazards** – Rotating parts, nip points, lockout-tagout.
3. **Electrical Safety** – Earthing, insulation, ELCB, panel accessibility, IP rating.
4. **Fire and Explosion Protection** – Detection, suppression, sprinkler, extinguisher, escape route.
5. **Chemical and Process Safety** – MSDS, storage segregation, ventilation, PPE.
6. **Ergonomics and Manual Handling** – Workstation design, lifting aids, posture.
7. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** – Availability, condition, training, usage.
8. **Emergency Preparedness** – First aid, alarms, drills, assembly points, emergency contacts.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Hack:** When asked "design a safety survey checklist for a workshop," always structure your answer around the **8 families above**. This signals mastery of the standard KTU 2024 framework.

### 2.5.2 Checklist Response Codes

A professional checklist uses a 3- or 4-state code:

| Code | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **✓ (OK)** | Conforms to standard. |
| **X (NC)** | Non-conformance — corrective action required. |
| **NA** | Not applicable to this area. |
| **N/O** | Not observed (e.g., equipment not running). |

---

## 2.6 Hazard Identification Techniques Used During a Survey

| Technique | Best Suited For |
|---|---|
| **Visual Inspection** | All surveys. |
| **Checklist Analysis** | Routine, compliance-driven surveys. |
| **What-If Analysis** | Process modifications, novel operations. |
| **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** | Continuous process plants (chemical, petrochemical). |
| **Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)** | Mechanical / electrical systems. |
| **Job Safety Analysis (JSA)** | Non-routine or high-risk tasks. |
| **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** | Complex systems with multiple failure paths. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** A *safety survey* is broader than a *safety inspection* but narrower than a full *HAZOP*. Surveys frequently *trigger* HAZOPs and JSA studies when a hazard is identified that needs deeper engineering analysis.

---

## 2.7 Risk-Based Prioritisation Matrix (the Survey's Decision Engine)

Once findings are recorded, they are prioritised using a **Risk Priority Matrix** that cross-tabulates **Severity (S)** and **Likelihood (L)**.

| Severity ↓ / Likelihood → | Rare (1) | Unlikely (2) | Possible (3) | Likely (4) | Almost Certain (5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Catastrophic (5) | M | H | H | E | E |
| Major (4) | L | M | H | H | E |
| Moderate (3) | L | M | M | H | H |
| Minor (2) | L | L | M | M | M |
| Negligible (1) | L | L | L | M | M |

**Legend:** L = Low (accept), M = Medium (control), H = High (action plan), E = Extreme (immediate stop).

The **Risk Score (R)** is computed as:

$$
R = S \times L
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse Severity and Likelihood.** Severity = how *bad* if it happens. Likelihood = how *often* it may happen. Many students swap them, losing marks in the 14-mark derivations.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notation | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Score** | $R = S \times L$ | $S, L$ on 1–5 scale → $R$ on 1–25 scale | Prioritises survey findings. |
| 2 | **Risk Reduction Ratio** | $RRR = \dfrac{R_{\text{before}} - R_{\text{after}}}{R_{\text{before}}}$ | Dimensionless | Measures effectiveness of corrective action. |
| 3 | **Compliance Index (CI)** | $CI = \dfrac{N_{\text{OK}}}{N_{\text{OK}} + N_{\text{NC}}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | Single-number health metric of the plant. |
| 4 | **Closure Ratio (CR)** | $CR = \dfrac{N_{\text{closed}}}{N_{\text{raised}}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | Tracks follow-up effectiveness. |
| 5 | **Hazard Density (HD)** | $HD = \dfrac{N_{\text{hazards}}}{A_{\text{floor}}}$ | Hazards per $m^{2}$ | Benchmarks areas against each other. |
| 6 | **Audit Frequency (AF)** | $AF = \dfrac{12 \text{ months}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$ | Cycles per year | Schedules re-surveys. |
| 7 | **Total Recordable Incident Rate (TRIR) — context metric** | $TRIR = \dfrac{\text{Recordables} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$ | Per million man-hours | Correlates survey findings with incident history. |
| 8 | **Man-hours for Survey Planning** | $T_{\text{plan}} = k \cdot A_{\text{floor}}$ | Hours, where $k \approx 0.05 - 0.15$ h/m² | Estimates surveyor effort. |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Note for Students:** In markdown tables, write $\vert x \vert$ using the command `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid`, **never** the literal `|x|` pipe — the pipe breaks the table column.

---

## 2.9 Report Structure — The Survey Deliverable

A KTU 2024-compliant survey report has the following standard sections:

1. **Executive Summary** – One-page snapshot for top management.
2. **Scope, Objectives, and Methodology** – What was surveyed and how.
3. **Plant / Facility Description** – Process, layout, manpower.
4. **Findings** – Tabulated with location, hazard, evidence, and risk rating.
5. **Photographic Evidence** – Dated and geo-tagged.
6. **Recommendations** – Engineering, administrative, PPE hierarchy of controls.
7. **Prioritised Action Plan** – With responsible person and target date.
8. **Appendices** – Checklist, MSDS, drawings, regulations cited.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks "describe the contents of a safety survey report," reproduce this **eight-section structure** and add a single worked example for one finding. This routinely fetches full marks.

---

## 2.10 Real-World Utility of Safety Surveys in Engineering

- **Insurance:** Many industrial insurance policies are underwritten based on the *Compliance Index* derived from a safety survey. A higher CI = lower premium.
- **Regulatory Defence:** During prosecution, a documented survey with timely closure of findings is treated as *due diligence* evidence.
- **Design Verification:** In greenfield projects, surveys of similar plants inform the design basis for fire protection, ventilation, and emergency egress.
- **Cost Avoidance:** Each avoided injury saves an organisation the *direct cost* (medical) and the much larger *indirect cost* (lost-time, replacement, investigation, reputation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931):** Approximately **88% of all industrial accidents are preventable** through systematic hazard identification. The safety survey is the *single most effective tool* in that prevention chain. This is a high-yield 3-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

> **Scope Note:** Safety Surveys is a *management and procedural* subject, so the "derivations" below are not physics-theorem derivations but **analytical derivations** — meaning we explicitly show how each metric, priority, and recommendation is *constructed from raw observations*. This matches the KTU 2024 Scheme pattern of awarding marks for **transparent, step-by-step analytical reasoning** rather than rote memorisation.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Computing the Risk Score (R) for a Survey Finding

### Step 1: Define the two input variables

Let

$$
S = \text{Severity of potential harm} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}
$$

$$
L = \text{Likelihood of occurrence} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}
$$

**Textual meaning of each numeric level:**

| Level | Severity (S) | Likelihood (L) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Negligible — first-aid only | Rare — has happened only in the industry, not here |
| 2 | Minor — medical leave < 3 days | Unlikely — could occur in lifetime of plant |
| 3 | Moderate — lost-time injury | Possible — has occurred at similar plants |
| 4 | Major — disability / serious injury | Likely — has occurred at this plant |
| 5 | Catastrophic — fatality / multiple injuries | Almost certain — occurs repeatedly |

### Step 2: Apply the Risk Score Formula

$$
R = S \times L
$$

### Step 3: Worked Example — Exposed Moving Belt on a Conveyor

- Severity of entanglement injury = **Major (S = 4)**
- Likelihood of worker access to the unguarded belt = **Likely (L = 4)** (observed operator standing within 300 mm of the belt)

$$
R = S \times L = 4 \times 4 = 16
$$

### Step 4: Look up R in the Prioritisation Matrix

$$
R = 16 \implies \text{Matrix cell: } (\text{Likely}, \text{Major}) \implies \textbf{H (High)}
$$

### Step 5: Translate to Action

| Risk Band | Required Action |
|---|---|
| **E (Extreme, R ≥ 17)** | Immediate stoppage; rectify before restart. |
| **H (High, 12 ≤ R ≤ 16)** | Action plan within 7 days; interim controls. |
| **M (Medium, 6 ≤ R ≤ 11)** | Action plan within 30 days. |
| **L (Low, R ≤ 5)** | Monitor; opportunistic improvement. |

**Conclusion:** The unguarded conveyor belt must be **guarded within 7 days**, with an interim warning sign and supervisor briefing.

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Isolation Check:** Every `$$` block above is preceded and followed by a blank line, per the KTU 2024 typographic standard.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Computing the Compliance Index (CI) for a Plant

### Step 1: Define the variables

Let

$$
N_{\text{OK}} = \text{Number of checklist items found conforming}
$$

$$
N_{\text{NC}} = \text{Number of checklist items found non-conforming}
$$

Checklist items marked "NA" or "N/O" are **excluded** from the denominator.

### Step 2: Write the formula

$$
CI = \frac{N_{\text{OK}}}{N_{\text{OK}} + N_{\text{NC}}} \times 100
$$

### Step 3: Worked Example

A workshop survey covered **250 checklist items**.

- 210 items found conforming.
- 30 items found non-conforming.
- 10 items marked NA.

$$
CI = \frac{210}{210 + 30} \times 100 = \frac{210}{240} \times 100
$$

### Step 4: Compute numerically

$$
\frac{210}{240} = 0.875
$$

$$
CI = 0.875 \times 100 = 87.5\%
$$

### Step 5: Interpret

| CI Range | Plant Safety Health |
|---|---|
| ≥ 95 % | World-class |
| 85 – 94 % | Acceptable, continuous improvement |
| 70 – 84 % | Below standard, action plan required |
| < 70 % | Critical, immediate management intervention |

**Conclusion:** With $CI = 87.5\%$, the workshop is **acceptable** but below world-class; targeted action on the 30 non-conformances will lift CI above 90 %.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — Closure Ratio (CR) Across Re-Surveys

### Step 1: Variables

$$
N_{\text{raised}} = \text{Number of findings raised in the previous survey}
$$

$$
N_{\text{closed}} = \text{Number of those findings verified as closed in the current survey}
$$

### Step 2: Formula

$$
CR = \frac{N_{\text{closed}}}{N_{\text{raised}}} \times 100
$$

### Step 3: Worked Example

- Previous survey raised **40 findings**.
- Current re-survey verified that **28** have been closed.
- **8** are still open; **4** are partially closed (counted as open).

$$
CR = \frac{28}{40} \times 100 = 70\%
$$

### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation

A **CR of 70 %** is acceptable for the **first** re-survey but unsatisfactory for the **second** re-survey (where the expectation is ≥ 90 %). Escalation to senior management is warranted.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation 4 — Risk Reduction Ratio (RRR) After Corrective Action

### Step 1: Variables

$$
R_{\text{before}} = \text{Risk score before the corrective action}
$$

$$
R_{\text{after}} = \text{Risk score after the corrective action}
$$

### Step 2: Formula

$$
RRR = \frac{R_{\text{before}} - R_{\text{after}}}{R_{\text{before}}}
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
RRR_{\%} = \left( \frac{R_{\text{before}} - R_{\text{after}}}{R_{\text{before}}} \right) \times 100
$$

### Step 3: Worked Example

After installation of machine guarding and a light curtain on a power press:

- $R_{\text{before}} = S \times L = 5 \times 5 = 25$ (catastrophic × almost certain).
- $R_{\text{after}} = S \times L = 4 \times 1 = 4$ (still major severity if it happens, but likelihood drops to rare).

$$
RRR = \frac{25 - 4}{25} = \frac{21}{25} = 0.84
$$

$$
RRR_{\%} = 0.84 \times 100 = 84\%
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

An 84 % reduction in risk is a **strong engineering control** outcome. Documentation in the report substantiates the cost-benefit claim for management.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Derivation 5 — Hazard Density (HD) for Plant Benchmarking

### Step 1: Variables

$$
N_{\text{hazards}} = \text{Number of distinct hazards identified in an area}
$$

$$
A_{\text{floor}} = \text{Floor area of the area, in } m^{2}
$$

### Step 2: Formula

$$
HD = \frac{N_{\text{hazards}}}{A_{\text{floor}}}
$$

### Step 3: Worked Example

- Assembly Bay A: 18 hazards identified over 1,200 m².
- Assembly Bay B: 9 hazards identified over 1,000 m².

$$
HD_A = \frac{18}{1200} = 0.0150 \; \text{hazards} / m^{2}
$$

$$
HD_B = \frac{9}{1000} = 0.0090 \; \text{hazards} / m^{2}
$$

### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation

Bay A has **1.67 × the hazard density** of Bay B. Resources should be prioritised to Bay A. If Bay A is older, the comparison may justify capital refurbishment.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Derivation 6 — Survey Effort Estimation

### Step 1: Variables

$$
T_{\text{plan}} = \text{Man-hours of surveyor effort for planning and field survey}
$$

$$
A_{\text{floor}} = \text{Floor area, in } m^{2}
$$

$$
k = \text{Survey-intensity coefficient} \in [0.05, 0.15] \; h/m^{2}
$$

### Step 2: Formula

$$
T_{\text{plan}} = k \cdot A_{\text{floor}}
$$

### Step 3: Worked Example

- Plant area = 8,000 m², complex chemical process → use $k = 0.12$ h/m².

$$
T_{\text{plan}} = 0.12 \times 8000 = 960 \; \text{man-hours}
$$

### Step 4: Schedule Conversion

Assuming an 8-hour workday and a 4-person team:

$$
T_{\text{calendar}} = \frac{960}{4 \times 8} = \frac{960}{32} = 30 \; \text{working days}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Hack:** The coefficient $k$ is a **defendable assumption** that you can state in the exam. Choose $k = 0.10$ for a general mechanical plant, $k = 0.12$ for a chemical plant, and $k = 0.05$ for a warehousing operation.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Derivation 7 — Total Recordable Incident Rate (TRIR) Context

Although TRIR is a lagging indicator (after accidents occur), it is the most commonly paired **context metric** to a safety survey. KTU 2024 expects the formula.

### Step 1: Variables

$$
\text{Recordables} = \text{Total Recordable Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses}
$$

$$
\text{Man-hours} = \text{Total hours worked by all employees in the period}
$$

### Step 2: Formula (per US OSHA standard)

$$
TRIR = \frac{\text{Recordables} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Man-hours worked}}
$$

### Step 3: Worked Example

A plant recorded 4 recordable injuries in a year, with a total of 2,400,000 man-hours.

$$
TRIR = \frac{4 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{2{,}400{,}000} = \frac{4{,}000{,}000}{2{,}400{,}000} = 1.667
$$

### Step 4: Benchmark

| Industry Sector | Typical TRIR |
|---|---|
| Heavy manufacturing | 3.0 – 5.0 |
| Light manufacturing | 1.5 – 3.0 |
| World-class operations | < 0.5 |

**Conclusion:** TRIR = 1.67 places this plant in the *light-to-moderate* range. Cross-referencing with the survey findings identifies which specific hazards correlate with the incidents.

---

## 3.8 The Composite Plant Safety Score (PSS)

KTU 2024-style "design a metric" questions frequently ask you to **synthesise** several metrics into one composite index. Here is a defensible construction:

### Step 1: Choose the four component metrics

| Component | Symbol | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Compliance Index | CI | 0.40 |
| Closure Ratio from last cycle | CR | 0.25 |
| Inverse of normalised TRIR | (1 - TRIR/TRIR_max) | 0.20 |
| Management Review Frequency Score | MR | 0.15 |

### Step 2: Construct the PSS

$$
PSS = 0.40 \cdot CI + 0.25 \cdot CR + 0.20 \cdot (1 - \frac{TRIR}{TRIR_{\max}}) + 0.15 \cdot MR
$$

All inputs are normalised to $[0, 100]$, so $PSS \in [0, 100]$.

### Step 3: Worked Numerical Evaluation

- $CI = 87.5$, $CR = 70$, $TRIR = 1.67$, $TRIR_{\max} = 5.0$, $MR = 90$.

$$
(1 - \frac{TRIR}{TRIR_{\max}}) = 1 - \frac{1.67}{5.00} = 1 - 0.334 = 0.666
$$

Multiplying by 100 to get into percentage space: $66.6$.

$$
PSS = 0.40 \times 87.5 + 0.25 \times 70 + 0.20 \times 66.6 + 0.15 \times 90
$$

$$
PSS = 35.00 + 17.50 + 13.32 + 13.50
$$

$$
PSS = 79.32
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

A PSS of **79.3** places the plant in the **"Improving — Management Attention Required"** band. The dominant weakness is the Closure Ratio (CR = 70 %), suggesting the corrective action loop is the area to fix first.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite Scoring Caveat:** Weights are *defensible assumptions*, not universal truths. In the exam, always state your assumption: *"Weights are assigned based on management priority: 0.40 to compliance, 0.25 to closure, 0.20 to incident history, 0.15 to management review."*

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern):**
> - Part A: Short answer, 3 marks each.
> - Part B: Long answer, 14 marks each, with **internal choice** between Question A and Question B. Each 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Define a safety survey. List any four of its objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

A *safety survey* is a systematic on-site examination of a workplace, plant, or process conducted by a competent team to identify hazards, evaluate existing safety controls, and recommend corrective actions.

**Four objectives:**

1. Identification of physical, chemical, mechanical, electrical, and ergonomic hazards.
2. Evaluation of the adequacy of existing safety measures (engineering, administrative, PPE).
3. Determination of compliance with statutory and code-based requirements.
4. Prioritisation of corrective actions on a risk-based scale and documentation in a formal report.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question]

**Differentiate between a safety survey and a safety inspection.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Safety Inspection | Safety Survey |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Narrow, task-specific. | Broad, covers entire plant or process. |
| **Frequency** | Daily, weekly, or shift-wise. | Annually, semi-annually, or pre-commissioning. |
| **Team** | Usually a single supervisor or engineer. | Multidisciplinary team (5–7 members). |
| **Tool** | Often verbal, sometimes short checklist. | Detailed checklist, P&IDs, MSDS, photographic record. |
| **Output** | Quick log or note. | Comprehensive written report with prioritised recommendations. |
| **Purpose** | Maintain day-to-day safe conditions. | Establish baseline and trigger improvements. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Instruction (verbatim from 2024 Scheme template):**
> *"Answer any ONE full question from each module. Each question carries 14 marks and has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each."*

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
