### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Basic Risk to Acceptable Risk

We begin with the engineering definition of risk as the product of **frequency** and **consequence**:

$$R = f \times C$$

For a complex system with $n$ independent hazardous events, the total risk is:

$$R_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot C_i$$

**Conversion Logic — Step by Step:**

$$R_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot C_i \tag{Initial definition}$$

We introduce the acceptability threshold $R_a$ such that the system is deemed safe iff:

$$R_{\text{total}} \leq R_a \tag{Acceptance criterion}$$

In practice, no engineered system reaches $R_{\text{total}} = 0$. So we decompose:

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{inherent}} - R_{\text{reduction}} \tag{After applying controls}$$

Combining:

$$\boxed{R_{\text{inherent}} - R_{\text{reduction}} \leq R_a}$$

Or equivalently, the **minimum required risk reduction**:

$$R_{\text{reduction}} \geq R_{\text{inherent}} - R_a$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost-Benefit Justification for Safety Investment

Suppose a hazard currently produces an annual expected loss of $L_0$ currency. A proposed safety measure reduces this loss to $L_1$, but costs $C_m$ per year to install and maintain.

**Net Annual Benefit:**

$$\Delta B = (L_0 - L_1) - C_m$$

For the measure to be economically justifiable under ALARP:

$$\Delta B \geq 0 \implies C_m \leq L_0 - L_1$$

However, regulators apply a **gross disproportion factor** $G$:

$$C_m \leq \frac{L_0 - L_1}{G} \tag{Safety-favored inequality}$$

$$C_m \leq \frac{\Delta R \cdot V}{G} \tag{Using } \Delta R \cdot V = L_0 - L_1$$

**Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style):**

Suppose a machine has a failure rate causing **0.05 fatalities per year**. The statistical value of a life (VSL) is taken as **₹1 crore** ($10^7$). A new guard costs **₹8 lakh/year** ($8 \times 10^5$) and reduces the fatality rate to **0.01 per year**. With $G = 3$ (worker scenario), determine acceptability.

**Step 1 — Current Expected Loss:**

$$L_0 = 0.05 \times 10^7 = 5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{INR/year}$$

**Step 2 — Residual Expected Loss:**

$$L_1 = 0.01 \times 10^7 = 1 \times 10^{5}\ \text{INR/year}$$

**Step 3 — Risk Reduction in Currency:**

$$\Delta R \cdot V = L_0 - L_1 = 5 \times 10^5 - 1 \times 10^5 = 4 \times 10^{5}\ \text{INR/year}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Justifiable Cost:**

$$C_{\max} = \frac{4 \times 10^{5}}{3} = 1.33 \times 10^{5}\ \text{INR/year}$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

$$\text{Since } C_m = 8 \times 10^5 > C_{\max} = 1.33 \times 10^5$$

$$\therefore \text{Decision: REJECT — economically unjustified under ALARP}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a *counterintuitive but legally valid* outcome. ALARP does **not** say "spend anything to save a life." It says "spend what is *reasonably practicable* — not what is *technically possible*."

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The F-N Curve (Societal Risk Criterion)

For major hazard installations, **Societal Risk** is plotted on an F-N diagram. The acceptance criterion is:

$$F \leq \frac{C}{N^{a}}$$

Where:
- $F$ = cumulative frequency of accidents causing $N$ or more fatalities
- $C$ = constant (anchors the line)
- $a$ = aversion factor ($a = 1$ neutral, $a = 2$ highly averse)

For HSE-UK's *Upper Bound* (intolerable line):

$$F = \frac{10^{-2}}{N^{2}}\quad \text{(per year)}$$

**Numerical Check:**

For $N = 10$ fatalities, the upper tolerable frequency is:

$$F_{\text{tol}} = \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{2}} = \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{2}} = 10^{-4}\ \text{per year}$$

For $N = 100$ fatalities:

$$F_{\text{tol}} = \frac{10^{-2}}{100^{2}} = \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{4}} = 10^{-6}\ \text{per year}$$

This shows that **larger accidents are exponentially less tolerable** — the heart of risk aversion logic.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Factors Modifying Risk Acceptability

| Factor | Effect on Acceptability | Direction |
|--------|--------------------------|-----------|
| **Voluntariness** of exposure | Voluntary risks accepted at higher threshold | ↑ |
| **Benefit** to the exposed party | Greater benefit → higher acceptance | ↑ |
| **Catastrophic potential** | Lower threshold for high-N events | ↓ |
| **Dread** (familiar vs unfamiliar) | Unfamiliar risks less accepted | ↓ |
| **Controllability** | Controllable risks more accepted | ↑ |
| **Distribution** (fairness of burden) | Imposed risks less accepted | ↓ |
| **Reversibility** | Irreversible harm lowers acceptance | ↓ |
| **Affected population** (workers vs public) | Public standard stricter (G = 10) | ↓ |
| **Trust in operator** | Distrust lowers acceptance | ↓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Star (*) marking for KTU high-yield:** Notice that *voluntariness* and *catastrophic potential* are the **two most frequently tested** modifying factors in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Tripartite (Three-Region) Risk Decision Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Quantified Risk from Assessment] --> B{Risk Level vs Criteria}
    B -->|Risk > Unacceptable Line| C[INTOLERABLE REGION]
    B -->|Risk between Lines| D[ALARP / TOLERABLE REGION]
    B -->|Risk < Acceptable Line| E[BROADLY ACCEPTABLE REGION]

    C --> F[STOP Activity or Apply Mandatory Controls]
    D --> G[Apply ALARP Test]
    E --> H[Monitor and Review Only]

    F --> I[Re-evaluate]
    G --> J{Cost-Benefit Justified?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Implement Additional Control]
    J -->|No| L[Document Residual Risk - Accept with Justification]
    K --> I
    L --> I
    H --> I
    I --> M[END: Decision Documented and Validated]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Risk Acceptability Decision System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT1[Hazard Identification Output] --> PROC1
    INPUT2[Consequence Modelling Output] --> PROC1
    INPUT3[Frequency / Probability Data] --> PROC1
    INPUT4[Population Exposure Data] --> PROC1
    INPUT5[Cost of Mitigation Measures] --> PROC1

    PROC1[Risk Quantification Engine] --> PROC2

    PROC2{Risk Classification Module}
    PROC2 -->|Individual| IR[Individual Risk Comparator]
    PROC2 -->|Societal| SR[Societal Risk F-N Plotter]
    PROC2 -->|Qualitative| RM[5x5 Risk Matrix Scorer]

    IR --> CRITERIA
    SR --> CRITERIA
    RM --> CRITERIA

    CRITERIA[Acceptance Criteria Database HSE TOR IAEA] --> DECISION

    DECISION{Acceptable?}
    DECISION -->|YES| ACCEPT[Accept and Monitor]
    DECISION -->|NO - INTOLERABLE| REJECT[Halt or Redesign]
    DECISION -->|NO - ALARP| ALARPCHK[Cost Benefit ALARP Engine]
    ALARPCHK -->|Justified| APPLY[Apply Additional Controls]
    ALARPCHK -->|Not Justified| DOC[Document Residual Decision]
```

### 4.3 ALARP Decision Tree (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Residual Risk Identified] --> B{Is Risk Below Broadly Acceptable Level?}
    B -->|YES| C[No Action Required - Move On]
    B -->|NO| D{Are Further Control Measures Available?}
    D -->|NO| E[Document Reason for No Further Action]
    D -->|YES| F{Is Cost Grossly Disproportionate to Benefit?}
    F -->|YES - Sacrifice Too High| G[Document Residual Risk - Tolerate with Justification]
    F -->|NO - Reasonable| H[Implement Control Measure]
    H --> I[Recalculate Residual Risk]
    I --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When answering 14-mark questions, always reproduce a simplified version of one of these diagrams — examiners explicitly reward *graphical representation* of risk acceptability frameworks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the term "Risk Acceptability" as used in modern safety management. State any two standard numerical criteria for individual risk acceptance.*

**Model Answer:**

Risk Acceptability is the threshold criterion (3 marks) defining the maximum level of risk an organization or society is willing to tolerate in pursuit of specific benefits. It establishes the boundary between tolerable and intolerable risk on the **Tolerability of Risk (TOR)** framework.

Two standard numerical criteria:
- Maximum tolerable individual risk for **public**: **$1 \times 10^{-4}$ per year**
- Maximum tolerable individual risk for **workers**: **$1 \times 10^{-3}$ per year**

*(Alternatively: Broadly acceptable level = $1 \times 10^{-6}$ per year.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*State the ALARP principle. Why is it considered the cornerstone of modern safety law?*

**Model Answer:**

**ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** is the legal and engineering principle that residual risk must be reduced until the cost (sacrifice) of further reduction becomes **grossly disproportionate** to the safety benefit gained.

It is the cornerstone of modern safety law (2 marks) because:
1. It balances **technical feasibility** with **economic practicality** — recognizing that zero risk is impossible.
2. It provides a **defensible legal standard** — organizations can demonstrate compliance via cost-benefit analysis.
3. It is the foundation of major statutes: UK HSE regulations, Indian Factories Act (Section 7A-7C), EU Seveso Directives, and ISO 12100.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module 4 Internal Choice

---

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Tolerability of Risk (TOR) framework** with a neat sketch of the tripartite risk diagram. Discuss the three regions and the decision logic in each region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical reactor has a probability of runaway reaction of **$5 \times 10^{-4}$ per year**, which could result in **3 fatalities** if it occurs. The company installs a relief valve system that reduces this probability to **$5 \times 10^{-6}$ per year**. Using the **ALARP cost-benefit test** with statistical value of life = **₹2 crore** and gross disproportion factor **G = 10**, determine whether a further proposed safety interlock costing **₹12 lakh per year** (with no additional risk reduction) is justifiable. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Tripartite Risk Diagram (TOR Framework):**

The TOR framework divides risk into three regions along a frequency–severity plot:

**Region 1 — Intolerable Region (Top):**
- Risk is so high that the activity **must not be allowed** to proceed (or operate only under special dispensation).
- No amount of cost-benefit analysis justifies operating here.
- *Example:* Uncontrolled chlorine storage in a residential zone.

**Region 2 — ALARP / Tolerable Region (Middle):**
- Risk is tolerated **only on condition** that it is reduced to ALARP.
- Cost-benefit analysis governs — further reductions required unless *grossly disproportionate*.
- *Example:* Oil refinery in an industrial zone with engineered controls.

**Region 3 — Broadly Acceptable Region (Bottom):**
- Risk is **negligible** — no further control is required by regulation.
- Operator may still monitor for change.
- *Example:* Crossing a properly designed pedestrian zebra crossing.

**Decision Logic Summary Table:**

| Region | Decision | Action |
|--------|----------|--------|
| Intolerable | Reject / Halt | Mandatory risk reduction, regardless of cost |
| ALARP / Tolerable | Tolerate with controls | Apply cost-benefit test for further reduction |
| Broadly Acceptable | Accept | Document and monitor |

> For diagram sketch, draw the standard *three-band TOR diagram* with frequency on Y-axis (log scale) and consequence/cost on X-axis.

---

##### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Step 1 — Identify the Risk Reduction Already Achieved:**

$$\Delta R = 5 \times 10^{-4} - 5 \times 10^{-6} = 4.95 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{fatalities/year}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Risk Reduction to Monetary Value:**

Using VSL = ₹2 crore = $2 \times 10^{7}$ INR per statistical life saved:

$$\Delta R \cdot V = 4.95 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{7} = 99\ \text{INR/year}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ALARP Test for the New Interlock:**

Given: The new interlock provides **no additional risk reduction** ($\Delta R_{\text{new}} = 0$), but costs ₹12 lakh/year ($1.2 \times 10^{6}$ INR/year).

$$\Delta R_{\text{new}} \cdot V = 0 \times 2 \times 10^{7} = 0\ \text{INR/year}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Maximum Justifiable Cost:**

$$C_{\max} = \frac{\Delta R_{\text{new}} \cdot V}{G} = \frac{0}{10} = 0\ \text{INR/year}$$

**Step 5 — Compare and Conclude:**

$$\text{Since } C_{\text{interlock}} = 1.2 \times 10^{6} \gg C_{\max} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\text{Decision: REJECT — the interlock is grossly disproportionate to benefit (zero).}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students incorrectly assume the *already-installed* relief valve's cost is also part of this calculation. **No** — the ALARP test is performed *incrementally* for each proposed measure. If a new measure provides **zero risk reduction**, $C_{\max} = 0$, and **any positive cost fails** the test. This is a *guaranteed 2-mark deduction* if missed.

---

#### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled F-N curve diagram, explain the concept of **Societal Risk Acceptance Criteria**. Discuss how the aversion factor $a$ influences the slope and the engineering decisions derived from such curves. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fireworks manufacturing unit is being assessed for licensing. Historical data shows the probability of a major explosion is **$2 \times 10^{-5}$ per year** with expected casualties of **8 fatalities**, and another scenario with probability **$5 \times 10^{-7}$ per year** causing **50 fatalities**. Using the HSE-UK upper bound criterion $F \leq 10^{-2}/N^{2}$, evaluate which scenario (if any) breaches the acceptability threshold. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

**Societal Risk & F-N Curves:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Societal Risk expresses the relationship between the **cumulative frequency (F)** of an accident and the **number of fatalities (N)** it causes. It is plotted on a log-log graph called an **F-N curve**.

**Three Reference Lines on the F-N Diagram:**
1. **Upper Bound Line** — *Intolerable limit.* Risk above this line is rejected.
2. **Lower Bound Line** — *Broadly acceptable limit.* Risk below this is acceptable.
3. **ALARP Region** — *Tolerable band* between the two lines.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$F \leq \frac{C}{N^{a}}$$

Where:
- $C$ = constant anchoring the line
- $a$ = **aversion factor** controlling the slope

**Effect of Aversion Factor (Key Concept):**

| Aversion Factor $a$ | Interpretation | Slope Behavior |
|---------------------|----------------|----------------|
| $a = 0$ | Risk neutral — every fatality equal | Horizontal line |
| $a = 1$ | Linear aversion — risk $\propto 1/N$ | Moderate slope |
| $a = 2$ | High aversion — catastrophe heavily weighted | Steep slope |
| $a \geq 2$ | Strongly averse — large accidents effectively forbidden | Very steep |

> The **higher the aversion factor**, the **steeper the line**, meaning society tolerates **drastically lower frequencies** for accidents causing many fatalities. This is why the HSE-UK uses $a = 2$ — reflecting strong societal dread of catastrophes.

---

##### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Given:**
- Scenario 1: $f_1 = 2 \times 10^{-5}$/year, $N_1 = 8$ fatalities
- Scenario 2: $f_2 = 5 \times 10^{-7}$/year, $N_2 = 50$ fatalities
- Acceptance criterion: $F_{\text{tol}} = 10^{-2}/N^{2}$

**Step 1 — Compute Tolerable Frequency for Scenario 1:**

$$F_{\text{tol},1} = \frac{10^{-2}}{8^{2}} = \frac{10^{-2}}{64} = 1.5625 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{per year}$$

**Compare:**

$$f_1 = 2 \times 10^{-5} < F_{\text{tol},1} = 1.5625 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{✓ ACCEPTABLE}$$

Scenario 1 lies **within acceptable limits**.

**Step 2 — Compute Tolerable Frequency for Scenario 2:**

$$F_{\text{tol},2} = \frac{10^{-2}}{50^{2}} = \frac{10^{-2}}{2500} = 4 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{per year}$$

**Compare:**

$$f_2 = 5 \times 10^{-7} < F_{\text{tol},2} = 4 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{✓ ACCEPTABLE}$$

Scenario 2 also lies **within acceptable limits**, but with a smaller margin.

**Step 3 — Quantitative Margin (Safety Buffer):**

$$\text{Buffer}_1 = \frac{F_{\text{tol},1}}{f_1} = \frac{1.5625 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.81\ \text{(buffer factor)}$$

$$\text{Buffer}_2 = \frac{F_{\text{tol},2}}{f_2} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 8.0\ \text{(buffer factor)}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

Both scenarios are individually below their tolerable frequencies; therefore **neither breaches** the absolute acceptability threshold. However, **Scenario 2 is far more critical from a societal perspective** because of its high fatality potential. The combined societal risk (using the F-N summation principle) should also be checked, but since both lie below their respective tolerance lines, **no immediate rejection** is warranted, though **Scenario 2 requires ALARP-level controls** (5 marks).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students commonly compute only **one** scenario's tolerance. **Both** must be evaluated. Additionally, do **not** sum the frequencies naively — the F-N criterion uses **cumulative** frequency, meaning all accidents with $N \geq N_i$ must be added. This is an advanced nuance worth 1 bonus mark in valuation if mentioned.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Risk Acceptability (Module 4)**

- ✅ Risk Acceptability defines **how safe is safe enough** — never zero.
- ✅ Three regions: **Intolerable / ALARP / Broadly Acceptable**.
- ✅ **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — cornerstone of safety law.
- ✅ ALARP test: $C_{\text{mitigation}} \leq \Delta R \cdot V / G$.
- ✅ Gross Disproportion Factor: **G = 3 (workers)**, **G = 10 (public)**.
- ✅ HSE-UK Individual Risk benchmarks: $10^{-3}$, $10^{-4}$, $10^{-6}$ per year.
- ✅ F-N curve slope controlled by **aversion factor $a$** — higher $a$ means stricter.
- ✅ 5×5 Risk Matrix is the workhorse of qualitative acceptability assessment.
- ✅ Voluntariness ↑, Catastrophic potential ↓ are the most-tested modifying factors.
- ✅ **Documentation** of risk acceptance decisions is legally mandatory under most statutes.
- ✅ Numerical examples use **VSL (Value of Statistical Life)** — typically ₹1–2 crore in Indian academic contexts.
- ✅ Always state **units** explicitly — examiners award 1 mark for unit clarity.
- ✅ Remember: **ALARP is incremental** — each proposed measure is tested *separately*.

> **End of Notes — Module 4: The Practice of Safety Management — The Significance of Risk Acceptability**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk Management - Introduction to ISO 31000
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT205 - PRINCIPLES OF SAFETY MANAGEMENT (SAFETY & FIRE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Risk Management - Introduction to ISO 31000

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Risk** is formally defined by **ISO 31000:2018** as the *"effect of uncertainty on objectives"*, where an effect is a deviation from the expected outcome (positive, negative, or both), and objectives can have different aspects (financial, health and safety, environmental) and apply at different levels (strategic, organization-wide, project, product, process).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT:** The term "effect of uncertainty on objectives" is the *canonical* definition that must be reproduced verbatim in 14-mark questions. Examiners reward students who quote the ISO source directly. The older 2009 definition ("combination of the probability of an event and its consequences") is now **superseded** but may appear as a contrast in Part A.

**ISO 31000** is the international standard for *Risk Management — Guidelines*, providing a universal framework and process that can be applied to any organization regardless of its size, activity, or industry sector. It is **principle-based** (not prescriptive), meaning it tells you *what to do*, not *how to do it*.

**Risk Management** is defined as *"coordinated activities to direct and control an organization with regard to risk"* (ISO 31000:2018, Clause 3.2).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Road Trip" Intuition

Imagine you are planning a long road trip from **Thiruvananthapuram to Delhi**.

| Trip Element | ISO 31000 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Destination & arrival time | **Objectives** of the organization |
| Weather, traffic, road blocks | **Risk sources** (uncertainty) |
| Flat tyre, engine failure, accident | **Risk events** |
| Carrying a spare tyre, first-aid kit, GPS | **Risk treatment / controls** |
| Checking weather & traffic before leaving | **Risk assessment** |
| Telling passengers about the plan & delays | **Communication & consultation** |
| Reviewing the trip at the end | **Monitoring & review** |

The road trip is the **organization's objective**, the road conditions are the **risks**, and your preparation is **risk management**. ISO 31000 simply gives you a *universal, proven* checklist to plan any such journey — whether it is a 2,000 km drive or a multi-crore construction project.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Mandatory for KTU 3-Mark Questions)

- **Hazard**: A source of potential harm (ISO 31000 distinguishes this from risk).
- **Risk Source**: An element that, alone or in combination, has the potential to give rise to risk.
- **Consequence**: The outcome of an event affecting objectives.
- **Likelihood**: The chance of something happening.
- **Risk Criteria**: Terms of reference against which the significance of risk is evaluated.
- **Risk Owner**: The person or entity accountable for managing the risk.
- **Control**: A measure that modifies risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Nuance:** KTU examiners frequently ask the difference between *Hazard* and *Risk*. A *Hazard* is the *thing that can cause harm* (e.g., a chemical); *Risk* is the *probability multiplied by severity* of that harm actually occurring (e.g., 1 in 10,000 chance of fatality). This is the most common 3-mark question in this module.

### 1.4 Why ISO 31000 Matters for Safety & Fire Engineers

For KTU's **Safety & Fire Engineering** stream, ISO 31000 is the *backbone* standard. It links directly to:

1. **OHSAS 18001 / ISO 45001** — Occupational health & safety.
2. **ISO 14001** — Environmental management.
3. **NFPA & BIS codes** — Fire safety design.
4. **KTU Module 4** learning outcomes on integrating safety into management systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Principles:** "**L**eaders **I**ntegrate **D**esign **C**ommunications **M**onitor **V**alues" → **L-I-D-C-M-V** (Linked, Integrated, Dynamic, Customized, Multi-structured, Value-creating — wait, see official list in Section 2).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of ISO 31000:2018

The standard is structured into **three major components** (KTU examiners love this 3-bullet structure for 7-mark questions):

#### Pillar 1: Principles (Clause 4)
These are the *philosophy* of risk management. The 8 principles are:

1. **Integrated** — Risk management is an integral part of all organizational activities.
2. **Structured and comprehensive** — Contributes to consistent and comparable results.
3. **Customized** — Aligned with the organization's external and internal context and risk profile.
4. **Inclusive** — Appropriate and timely involvement of stakeholders.
5. **Dynamic** — Anticipates, detects, acknowledges, and responds to changes.
6. **Best available information** — Based on historical data, experience, stakeholder input, observation, forecasts, and expert judgment.
7. **Human and cultural factors** — Recognizes human behavior and culture.
8. **Continual improvement** — Risk management is continually improved through learning and experience.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trap:** Many students confuse the *Principles* (philosophical) with the *Framework* (structural) and the *Process* (operational). Examiners will deduct marks if you mix them up. Memorize the distinction.

#### Pillar 2: Framework (Clause 5)
The framework ensures risk management is embedded in the organization. It has **6 elements**:

1. **Mandate & Commitment** (Leadership)
2. **Design of Framework** (Understanding context, articulating mandates)
3. **Implementing Risk Management** (Plan, resources, accountability)
4. **Evaluation** (Periodic measurement against criteria)
5. **Improvement** (Adjustment based on evaluation)
6. **Integration** (Into all organizational processes)

#### Pillar 3: Process (Clause 6)
The process is the operational cycle:

1. **Communication & Consultation** (continuous)
2. **Scope, Context, Criteria** (define boundaries)
3. **Risk Assessment** (3 sub-steps)
4. **Risk Treatment**
5. **Monitoring & Review**
6. **Recording & Reporting**

### 2.2 The Risk Assessment Sub-Process (Highest Weightage in KTU)

Risk Assessment is the heart of the standard and carries the **maximum marks** in 14-mark questions.

| Step | Name | KTU Description | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3.1 | **Risk Identification** | Find, recognize, and describe risks. | Risk register |
| 3.2 | **Risk Analysis** | Comprehend nature, sources, causes. Determine consequence & likelihood. | Risk score |
| 3.3 | **Risk Evaluation** | Compare against criteria; prioritize. | Risk matrix position |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The mathematical core of risk assessment is captured in the following equations. These are **mandatory** for any Part B numerical/analytical question.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Risk Equation** | $\text{Risk} = f(P, C)$ | $P$ = Probability, $C$ = Consequence. Dimensionless index. |
| 2 | **Quantitative Risk** | $R = P \times C$ | $P$ in events/year, $C$ in monetary / fatalities. |
| 3 | **Individual Risk (IR)** | $IR = P_f \times P_{d \vert f}$ | Fatality probability per year per person exposed. |
| 4 | **Societal Risk (F-N Curve)** | $F(N) = \sum_{i} f_i$ | Cumulative frequency of $\geq N$ fatalities. |
| 5 | **Risk Matrix Score** | $S = L \times S_{core}$ | $L$ (Likelihood 1–5) $\times$ $S_{core}$ (Severity 1–5). Max = 25. |
| 6 | **ALARP Inequality** | $\text{Risk} \leq \text{Tolerable}, \text{ and } \text{Cost of further reduction} > \text{Risk reduction achieved}$ | As Low As Reasonably Practicable. |
| 7 | **Cost-Benefit Ratio** | $\text{CBR} = \dfrac{\text{Cost of treatment}}{\text{Risk reduction value}}$ | CBR $<$ 1 = justified treatment. |
| 8 | **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | $RRF = \dfrac{R_{before}}{R_{after}}$ | RRF $>$ 1 means risk reduced. |
| 9 | **Residual Risk** | $R_{residual} = R_{inherent} \times (1 - E)$ | $E$ = effectiveness of control (0 to 1). |
| 10 | **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = ARO \times SLE$ | Annualized Rate of Occurrence $\times$ Single Loss Expectancy. |
| 11 | **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_I}{2}$ | For a single proof-tested component (IEC 61508). |
| 12 | **Tolerable Risk Threshold (KTU reference)** | $IR \leq 10^{-6} \text{ per year}$ (worker), $IR \leq 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$ (public) | UK HSE benchmarks. |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Pipe Rule:** In the table above, the $\vert$ symbol inside cells has been replaced with the safe `\vert` / descriptive text to keep the markdown table parser happy. Students writing on paper should draw clean vertical bars.

### 2.4 The Risk Matrix (5x5) — Standard KTU Tool

This is the **most frequently asked** diagram in KTU exams. You must draw and label it.

| Likelihood ↓ / Severity → | 1. Negligible | 2. Minor | 3. Moderate | 4. Major | 5. Catastrophic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **5. Almost Certain** | 5 (Med) | 10 (High) | 15 (V.High) | 20 (V.High) | 25 (V.High) |
| **4. Likely** | 4 (Low) | 8 (Med) | 12 (High) | 16 (V.High) | 20 (V.High) |
| **3. Possible** | 3 (Low) | 6 (Med) | 9 (High) | 12 (High) | 15 (V.High) |
| **2. Unlikely** | 2 (Low) | 4 (Low) | 6 (Med) | 8 (Med) | 10 (High) |
| **1. Rare** | 1 (Low) | 2 (Low) | 3 (Low) | 4 (Low) | 5 (Med) |

**Action Levels:**
- **Low (1–6):** Accept risk; monitor routinely.
- **Medium (8–12):** Mitigate by controls; assign owner.
- **High / Very High (15–25):** Senior management intervention; mandatory treatment; ALARP demonstration.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility for Safety & Fire Engineers

- **Fire Risk Assessment** in buildings (aligned with NBC 2016 & NFPA 101).
- **Process Safety** in chemical plants (linked to HAZOP & LOPA).
- **Construction Safety** — OHS planning per ISO 45001.
- **Emergency Planning** — Scenario-based risk modelling.
- **Audit & Compliance** — IS/ISO 31000 is the *parent standard* for safety management systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: From Inherent to Residual Risk

**Scenario:** A petroleum storage tank farm. The *inherent* risk of an unconfined vapour cloud explosion is unacceptable. A series of controls (gas detection, foam system, bund wall, emergency shutdown) are applied. We must mathematically show how the risk reduces.

**Step 1: Identify Inherent Risk (R_inh)**

The probability of a release event is determined from historical incident databases. Assume:

$$P_{release} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \text{ events/year}$$

The consequence, in terms of expected fatality, is modelled as:

$$C_{inh} = 5 \text{ fatalities (modelling a major explosion)}$$

Therefore, the inherent risk score is:

$$R_{inh} = P_{release} \times C_{inh} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 5 = 5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ fatalities/year}$$

**Step 2: Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) — Apply Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)**

Each control has a Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD). We assume independence.

| Layer | Control | PFD |
|---|---|---|
| IPL 1 | Gas detection + isolation | $1 \times 10^{-1}$ |
| IPL 2 | Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valve | $1 \times 10^{-1}$ |
| IPL 3 | Bund wall (prevents pool spread) | $1 \times 10^{-1}$ |
| IPL 4 | Foam suppression | $1 \times 10^{-1}$ |
| IPL 5 | Evacuation procedure | $1 \times 10^{-1}$ |

**Step 3: Compute Combined PFD (assuming independence)**

For independent protection layers, probabilities multiply:

$$PFD_{total} = \prod_{i=1}^{5} PFD_i$$

$$PFD_{total} = (1 \times 10^{-1})^5$$

$$PFD_{total} = 1 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4: Compute Mitigated Probability**

$$P_{mitigated} = P_{release} \times PFD_{total} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 1 \times 10^{-5} = 1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ events/year}$$

**Step 5: Compute Residual Risk**

Assuming the same consequence (5 fatalities):

$$R_{residual} = P_{mitigated} \times C = 1 \times 10^{-7} \times 5 = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ fatalities/year}$$

**Step 6: Compare Against Tolerable Risk**

HSE UK benchmark for workers:

$$IR_{tolerable} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per year}$$

Check:

$$R_{residual} = 5 \times 10^{-7} < 1 \times 10^{-6} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** Residual risk is **below tolerable**; the design satisfies ALARP.

**Step 7: Compute Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**

$$RRF = \dfrac{R_{inh}}{R_{residual}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 1 \times 10^{5}$$

The risk has been reduced by a factor of **100,000** (5 orders of magnitude).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: ISO 31000 vs. Other Standards

| Feature | ISO 31000 | ISO 45001 (OHS) | ISO 14001 (Env) | ISO 9001 (Quality) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Universal risk mgmt | Occupational H&S | Environmental | Quality mgmt |
| **Mandate** | Voluntary | Certifiable | Certifiable | Certifiable |
| **Process model** | Risk mgmt process | Plan-Do-Check-Act | Plan-Do-Check-Act | Plan-Do-Check-Act |
| **Risk approach** | All uncertainties | Hazards to people | Environmental aspects | Product/process risks |
| **KTU relevance** | Foundational (PCSFT205) | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Year (latest)** | 2018 | 2018 | 2015 | 2015 |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Risk Treatment Options (Hierarchy of Controls)

This is the **second most important** analytical diagram for KTU, drawn from NIOSH / ISO 31000 Annex.

| Level | Treatment | Effectiveness | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Elimination** | Highest (100%) | Remove the hazard entirely |
| 2 | **Substitution** | Very High | Replace toxic chemical with safer one |
| 3 | **Engineering Controls** | High | Machine guarding, ventilation |
| 4 | **Administrative Controls** | Moderate | Training, SOPs, signage |
| 5 | **PPE** | Lowest | Helmets, gloves, respirators |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**E**ngineers **S**ometimes **E**ngage **A**dministrative **P**rotection" → **E-S-E-A-P** (Eliminate, Substitute, Engineering, Administrative, PPE).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ISO 31000:2018 — Complete Architecture

This block-level flow shows how the **Principles, Framework, and Process** integrate.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[ISO 31000:2018 Standard] --> A2[Clause 4: Principles]
    A1 --> A3[Clause 5: Framework]
    A1 --> A4[Clause 6: Process]
    
    A2 --> B1[Integrated]
    A2 --> B2[Structured]
    A2 --> B3[Customized]
    A2 --> B4[Inclusive]
    A2 --> B5[Dynamic]
    A2 --> B6[Best Info]
    A2 --> B7[Human Factors]
    A2 --> B8[Continual Improvement]
    
    A3 --> C1[Mandate and Commitment]
    A3 --> C2[Design of Framework]
    A3 --> C3[Implementing RM]
    A3 --> C4[Evaluation]
    A3 --> C5[Improvement]
    A3 --> C6[Integration]
    
    A4 --> D1[Communication and Consultation]
    A4 --> D2[Scope Context Criteria]
    A4 --> D3[Risk Assessment]
    A4 --> D4[Risk Treatment]
    A4 --> D5[Monitoring and Review]
    A4 --> D6[Recording and Reporting]
    
    D3 --> E1[Identification]
    D3 --> E2[Analysis]
    D3 --> E3[Evaluation]
    
    D4 --> F1[Eliminate]
    D4 --> F2[Substitute]
    D4 --> F3[Engineering Control]
    D4 --> F4[Admin Control]
    D4 --> F5[PPE]
```

### 4.2 Risk Management Process Cycle (Operational View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Communication and Consultation] --> stepB[Scope Context Criteria]
    stepB --> stepC[Risk Assessment]
    stepC --> stepC1[Identification]
    stepC --> stepC2[Analysis]
    stepC --> stepC3[Evaluation]
    stepC3 --> stepD[Risk Treatment]
    stepD --> stepE[Monitoring and Review]
    stepE --> stepF[Recording and Reporting]
    stepF --> stepA
    
    stepD -.->|Feedback| stepA
    stepE -.->|Learning| stepA
```

### 4.3 Risk Treatment Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Identified Risk] --> node2{Risk > Tolerable?}
    node2 -->|No| node3[Accept and Monitor]
    node2 -->|Yes| node4{Risk >> Tolerable?}
    node4 -->|Yes| node5[MUST TREAT - Eliminate]
    node4 -->|No| node6{ALARP Demonstrated?}
    node6 -->|Yes| node7[Accept with Controls]
    node6 -->|No| node8[Apply Hierarchy: Substitute]
    node8 --> node9[Engineering Controls]
    node9 --> node10[Administrative Controls]
    node10 --> node11[PPE]
    node11 --> node12[Recompute Residual Risk]
    node12 --> node2
```

### 4.4 Context Establishment Matrix (Clause 6.2)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ctx1[Context Establishment] --> ctx2[External Context]
    ctx1 --> ctx3[Internal Context]
    ctx1 --> ctx4[Risk Process Context]
    ctx1 --> ctx5[Risk Criteria]
    
    ctx2 --> ctxA[Legal Environment]
    ctx2 --> ctxB[Social Cultural]
    ctx2 --> ctxC[Technological]
    ctx2 --> ctxD[Market Dynamics]
    
    ctx3 --> ctxE[Vision Mission]
    ctx3 --> ctxF[Governance]
    ctx3 --> ctxG[Culture]
    ctx3 --> ctxH[Capabilities]
    
    ctx4 --> ctxI[Goals Objectives]
    ctx4 --> ctxJ[Scope Boundaries]
    
    ctx5 --> ctxK[Risk Appetite]
    ctx5 --> ctxL[Tolerable Thresholds]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define "Risk" as per ISO 31000:2018. How does this definition differ from the earlier ISO 31000:2009 definition?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**ISO 31000:2018 Definition (1 mark):**
Risk is defined as the *"effect of uncertainty on objectives"*, where objectives can be strategic, operational, financial, or safety-related at any level of the organization.

**Difference from 2009 Version (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | ISO 31000:2009 | ISO 31000:2018 |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | "Combination of the probability of an event and its consequences" | "Effect of uncertainty on objectives" |
| Scope | Treated risk as a *value* (P×C) | Recognizes risk as both *positive* and *negative* deviation |
| Focus | Quantification emphasis | Strategic + value-based |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The 2018 version broadens the scope beyond negative outcomes, acknowledging that uncertainty can create opportunities, not just threats.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List and briefly explain the three sub-steps of Risk Assessment as per ISO 31000.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Risk Assessment process comprises three sub-steps:

1. **Risk Identification (1 mark):** The systematic process of finding, recognizing, and describing risks. It involves identifying sources of risk, areas of impact, events, causes, and potential consequences. Tools include checklists, brain-storming, HAZOP, and incident databases.

2. **Risk Analysis (1 mark):** Comprehending the nature of risk and its characteristics. It involves determining the *likelihood* and *consequence* of each identified risk, either qualitatively, semi-quantitatively, or quantitatively.

3. **Risk Evaluation (1 mark):** Comparing the results of risk analysis against the pre-defined *risk criteria* to determine whether the risk is acceptable, tolerable, or requires treatment. Outputs a prioritized risk list for decision-makers.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For 14-mark questions, you **MUST**:
> 1. Draw at least one neat, labelled diagram (process flow or risk matrix).
> 2. Use **headings and sub-headings** (a, b, c, d...) — not paragraph essays.
> 3. Quote the **ISO 31000 clause numbers** wherever relevant.
> 4. Provide an **Indian context** (e.g., reference to Indian factories Act, NBC 2016, BIS).
> 5. Conclude with a **practical application** paragraph.
> 
> **Common pitfalls where students lose marks:**
> - Confusing Principles with Process (–2 marks)
> - Skipping the diagram (–2 marks)
> - Writing the risk matrix without action levels (–1 mark)
> - No mention of Communication & Consultation (–1 mark)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain in detail the **principles and framework** clauses of ISO 31000:2018. State how they are interconnected.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **risk treatment** options available to a safety manager in a chemical plant, with a suitable hierarchy of controls. Illustrate with an example.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) Principles and Framework of ISO 31000:2018** *(7 marks)*

**Introduction (1 mark):**
ISO 31000:2018 comprises three pillars: Principles (Clause 4), Framework (Clause 5), and Process (Clause 6). The principles provide the philosophy; the framework provides the structure; the process provides the operational steps.

**The 8 Principles (Clause 4) — 3 marks:**

The principles are:

1. **Integrated** — Risk management is part of all organizational activities.
2. **Structured and comprehensive** — Produces consistent results.
3. **Customized** — Tailored to the organization's context.
4. **Inclusive** — Involves stakeholders appropriately.
5. **Dynamic** — Adapts to changes.
6. **Best available information** — Based on data and expert judgment.
7. **Human and cultural factors** — Recognizes behavioral aspects.
8. **Continual improvement** — Enhanced through learning.

**The 6 Elements of the Framework (Clause 5) — 2 marks:**

1. **Mandate and Commitment** — Top management ownership.
2. **Design of Framework** — Context, mandate, policies.
3. **Implementing Risk Management** — Plan, resources, accountability.
4. **Evaluation** — Periodic measurement.
5. **Improvement** — Adjustments based on evaluation.
6. **Integration** — Embedding into processes.

**Interconnection (1 mark):**
The principles act as the *guiding philosophy*. The framework provides the *organizational structure* to implement the principles. The process (Clause 6) then executes the operations within the framework. The three are interdependent — without principles there is no guidance, without framework there is no structure, without process there is no action.

**Diagram (suggested) — 0 marks but mandatory:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pnode1[Principles - Clause 4] --> fnode1[Framework - Clause 5]
    fnode1 --> procnode1[Process - Clause 6]
    pnode1 -.-> procnode1
```

---

**(b) Risk Treatment Options in a Chemical Plant** *(7 marks)*

**Definition (0.5 mark):**
Risk treatment is the process to **modify risk** (ISO 31000 Clause 6.4). It can involve avoiding, removing, changing the likelihood, changing the consequence, sharing, or retaining by informed decision.

**Hierarchy of Controls (4 marks):**
The most effective to least effective options are:

1. **Elimination** (Most effective) — Remove the hazard entirely (e.g., stop using benzene in a process).
2. **Substitution** — Replace with a less hazardous alternative (e.g., water-based paint instead of solvent-based).
3. **Engineering Controls** — Physical modifications (e.g., closed transfer systems, ventilation, containment).
4. **Administrative Controls** — Procedures, training, signage, work permits.
5. **PPE** (Least reliable) — Respirators, gloves, eye protection.

**Example — Methyl Isocyanate Storage (2 marks):**
At the Bhopal Union Carbide plant (1984), MIC release caused thousands of fatalities. Application of the hierarchy would have meant:
- *Elimination:* Stop storing MIC in such large quantities on-site.
- *Substitution:* Use less toxic intermediate chemicals.
- *Engineering:* Refrigerated storage, scrubbers, flare systems.
- *Administrative:* Tight maintenance protocols, training, public communication.
- *PPE:* Only as a last line of defense.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Risk treatment is not about choosing one option but applying a *layered defense* (defense-in-depth), aligned with ALARP, ensuring residual risk falls within tolerable limits.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe the **Risk Assessment process** under ISO 31000:2018, with reference to risk identification techniques suitable for a high-rise building fire safety assessment.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Construct a **5×5 Risk Matrix** for a fire safety scenario. Compute the risk score for a case where *Likelihood = 4 (Likely)* and *Severity = 5 (Catastrophic)*. Discuss the action plan.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Risk Assessment Process for High-Rise Building Fire Safety** *(7 marks)*

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Risk Assessment (Clause 6.3) is the overall process of risk identification, analysis, and evaluation. It is the cornerstone of any fire safety audit under ISO 31000.

**Step 1: Risk Identification (2 marks):**
The objective is to find, recognize, and record fire-related risks. Techniques include:

- **Checklist Analysis** — Using IS 14435 / NBC 2016 checklists.
- **What-If Analysis** — Brainstorming failure modes.
- **HAZOP** — Detailed process deviations.
- **FMEA** — Equipment failure modes.
- **Historical Incident Analysis** — Past fire data in similar buildings.
- **Site Walk-downs** — Physical inspection.

For a high-rise building, identified risks may include: blocked fire exits, non-functional sprinklers, flammable cladding, inadequate compartmentalization, disabled alarms, overcrowding.

**Step 2: Risk Analysis (2 marks):**
Determine the *likelihood* and *consequence* of each risk.

- **Likelihood** rated 1–5 (Rare to Almost Certain).
- **Consequence** rated 1–5 (Negligible to Catastrophic) in terms of life loss, property damage, business interruption.

Example: Blocked fire exit — Likelihood = 3 (Possible), Consequence = 5 (Catastrophic, mass casualties).

**Step 3: Risk Evaluation (1 mark):**
Compare the analyzed risk score against the *risk criteria* established by the building's safety policy. The risk is then classified as Low, Medium, High, or Very High, and prioritized for treatment.

**Communication and Consultation (0.5 mark):**
Throughout these three steps, continuous communication with stakeholders (residents, fire authorities, builders, local disaster mgmt) is mandatory.

**Diagram (suggested) — flowchart of the 3 sub-steps (0.5 mark of clarity).**

---

**(b) 5×5 Risk Matrix Computation** *(7 marks)*

**The 5×5 Risk Matrix (3 marks):**

| Likelihood ↓ / Severity → | 1 Negligible | 2 Minor | 3 Moderate | 4 Major | 5 Catastrophic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 Almost Certain | 5 (M) | 10 (H) | 15 (VH) | 20 (VH) | 25 (VH) |
| 4 Likely | 4 (L) | 8 (M) | 12 (H) | 16 (VH) | 20 (VH) |
| 3 Possible | 3 (L) | 6 (M) | 9 (H) | 12 (H) | 15 (VH) |
| 2 Unlikely | 2 (L) | 4 (L) | 6 (M) | 8 (M) | 10 (H) |
| 1 Rare | 1 (L) | 2 (L) | 3 (L) | 4 (L) | 5 (M) |

**Risk Score Computation (2 marks):**

Given:
$$L = 4 \quad (\text{Likely})$$
$$S = 5 \quad (\text{Catastrophic})$$

The risk score is:
$$R_{score} = L \times S = 4 \times 5 = 20$$

**Classification:** 20 falls in the **Very High** risk zone (top-right).

**Action Plan for Score 20 (2 marks):**

1. **Senior management intervention** is mandatory (per ISO 31000 escalation matrix).
2. **Immediate risk treatment** must be applied — eliminate the hazard where possible.
3. **Engineering controls** such as automatic sprinklers, smoke control, fire-resistant compartmentation.
4. **Administrative controls** — strict housekeeping, evacuation drills, public addressing.
5. **PPE** — Self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) for fire wardens.
6. **Demonstrate ALARP** — Show that the cost of further risk reduction is grossly disproportionate to the safety benefit achieved.
7. **Re-assess residual risk** to ensure it drops to Low (1–6) zone.

**Conclusion:** A score of 20 demands the highest level of organizational response, and treatment is non-negotiable under ISO 31000 and Indian fire safety statutes (NBC 2016, IS 14435).

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the day of the KTU exam:**

- ✅ **Definition (verbatim):** Risk = *effect of uncertainty on objectives* (ISO 31000:2018, Clause 3.1).
- ✅ **Three pillars:** Principles (4), Framework (5), Process (6) — *don't mix them up*.
- ✅ **8 Principles** must be listed in order if asked.
- ✅ **6 Framework elements** — Mandate, Design, Implement, Evaluate, Improve, Integrate.
- ✅ **6 Process steps** — Communication, Scope, Assessment, Treatment, Monitoring, Recording.
- ✅ **Risk Assessment = 3 sub-steps** — Identification → Analysis → Evaluation.
- ✅ **Risk Matrix 5×5** — Score = Likelihood × Severity. Max = 25. Threshold for treatment: score ≥ 10.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls** — Eliminate > Substitute > Engineering > Administrative > PPE (mnemonic: **E-S-E-A-P**).
- ✅ **ALARP** — As Low As Reasonably Practicable. The cost of further reduction must be *grossly disproportionate* to the safety benefit.
- ✅ **Tolerable Risk (HSE):** $IR \leq 10^{-6}$/yr (worker), $10^{-5}$/yr (public).
- ✅ **Residual Risk Formula:** $R_{res} = R_{inh} \times (1 - E)$ where $E$ = control effectiveness.
- ✅ **LOPA Multiplication:** Independent Protection Layers multiply their PFDs to give total PFD.
- ✅ **Bhopal 1984** is a perennial KTU favourite as a case study.
- ✅ **Indian Context:** Always link to **Factories Act 1948**, **NBC 2016**, **IS 14435**, **OSHC guidelines**, and **ISO 45001**.
- ✅ **Draw at least one diagram** in every 14-mark answer (risk matrix OR process flowchart).
- ✅ **Continuous step:** *Communication & Consultation* runs across the entire process, not just at the start.
- ✅ **Don't confuse** ISO 31000 (universal) with ISO 45001 (OHS-specific) or ISO 14001 (Env-specific).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accident Investigation - Why? When? Where? Who? and How?
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT205 - PRINCIPLES OF SAFETY MANAGEMENT (SAFETY & FIRE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Accident Investigation - Why? When? Where? Who? and How?

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Accident Investigation** is a systematic, structured, and methodical process of gathering, analyzing, and interpreting factual information about the sequence of events leading to an unplanned, undesired occurrence (accident/incident) that resulted in — or had the potential to result in — injury, illness, fatality, property damage, environmental harm, or operational disruption.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework (PCSFT205 — Module 4), accident investigation is positioned as a **proactive loss-control instrument** within the broader discipline of Safety Management. It is **not** a fault-finding exercise aimed at punishing individuals, but rather a **fact-finding** and **cause-finding** exercise aimed at preventing recurrence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *"Accident investigation is the logical, sequential, and documented analysis of an unwanted event to identify its root causes, immediate causes, contributing factors, and to recommend corrective and preventive actions (CAPA) so that the same or similar event does not recur."*

### Key Terminology (KTU Mandatory Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Incident** | Any unplanned event that did not result in injury/damage but had the potential to (near-miss). |
| **Accident** | An unplanned event that actually resulted in injury, illness, or property damage. |
| **Near-Miss** | An event that could have caused harm but did not — by sheer chance. |
| **Root Cause** | The fundamental, underlying reason why the event occurred (systemic failure). |
| **Immediate Cause** | The direct act or condition that preceded the accident. |
| **CAPA** | Corrective and Preventive Actions. |
| **PPE** | Personal Protective Equipment. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detective at a Crime Scene" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Hospital Emergency Room (ER) Analogy**
>
> Imagine a patient arrives at an emergency room after a fall. The doctor does **not** simply treat the broken bone and send the patient home. The doctor asks:
> - **WHY** did the patient fall? (Was the floor slippery? Was there poor lighting?)
> - **WHEN** did the fall occur? (Morning rush hour? After a meal?)
> - **WHERE** did it happen? (Staircase? Bathroom? Public road?)
> - **WHO** was involved? (Age, health condition, footwear?)
> - **HOW** can we prevent it next time? (Anti-skid flooring, grab bars, foot check-ups?)
>
> **Accident investigation works exactly the same way.** A safety professional acts as a "doctor" diagnosing the "patient" (the workplace) after an "illness" (the accident). Treating only the visible injury (the immediate damage) without diagnosing the root cause is like putting a bandage on a fractured bone — the problem remains and will resurface.

This analogy emphasizes that **accident investigation is diagnostic, not punitive**.

---

## 1.3 The Five W's and One H — The Investigative Framework

The KTU syllabus specifically structures accident investigation around **5 critical questions** that form the investigative spine of the topic:

$$\text{Accident Investigation} = f(\text{Why, When, Where, Who, How})$$

Each of these questions corresponds to a distinct investigative objective, and together they provide a **holistic understanding** of the event.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> Every KTU question on this topic will almost certainly test your ability to **explain the 5 W's + H framework** and apply it to a workplace scenario. Memorize the structured purpose of each question.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

- It carries **direct 14-mark questions** in the End Semester Exam (ESE) under Module 4.
- It frequently appears as a **3-mark short-answer** linking investigation to prevention philosophy.
- It is a **core competency** tested in the Course Outcome **CO4**: *"Apply accident investigation and reporting techniques to industrial scenarios."*
- It links directly to **Factories Act 1948 (Sec 88A, 88B)** and **OSH Code 2020** statutory requirements — a high-yield area for examiner focus.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix

## 2.1 The "WHY" of Accident Investigation

### 2.1.1 Why Should an Accident Be Investigated?

The **Why** question targets the **justification** for investing time, money, and manpower into an investigation. It answers: *"Is this exercise worth doing?"*

**Core Reasons (KTU Board-Preferred List):**

1. **To Prevent Recurrence (Primary Goal)**
   The single most important reason. The investigation identifies root causes and recommends controls so that the same accident does not happen again.

2. **To Fulfill Legal and Statutory Obligations**
   Under the Factories Act 1948, the OSH Code 2020, and ISO 45001:2018, certain accidents (fatal, serious injuries, dangerous occurrences) **must** be investigated and reported to authorities.

3. **To Identify Hidden Hazards and System Gaps**
   Many unsafe conditions and unsafe acts remain dormant until an accident exposes them. Investigation surfaces these "latent failures" (Reason's Swiss Cheese Model).

4. **To Estimate True Cost of Accidents**
   Direct costs (medical, compensation) and indirect costs (lost production, downtime, morale, legal) — investigation helps quantify these.

5. **To Improve Safety Management Systems (SMS)**
   Findings feed back into SOPs, training, PPE policy, and design modifications.

6. **To Reinforce Safety Culture**
   Visible, fair, and transparent investigations send a message that safety is a value, not a slogan.

7. **To Generate Reliable Data for Trend Analysis**
   Aggregated investigation data enables statistical safety analysis and predictive risk modeling.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often write *only* "to prevent recurrence." Examiners expect a **multi-point answer** covering legal, financial, cultural, and statistical dimensions. Always list at least 5 distinct reasons.

---

## 2.2 The "WHEN" of Accident Investigation

### 2.2.1 When Should an Investigation Be Conducted?

The **When** question defines the **timing and triggers** for initiating an investigation.

**Trigger Events (When to investigate):**

- **Immediately after a Fatal Accident** — within hours, before evidence is disturbed.
- **After a Serious Injury / Lost-Time Injury (LTI)** — within 24–48 hours.
- **After a Dangerous Occurrence** (e.g., near-miss, structural collapse, gas leak).
- **After Property Damage exceeding a defined threshold.**
- **After Recurring Minor Incidents** indicating a systemic issue.
- **Periodically (Proactive)** — even when no accident has occurred, to audit processes (e.g., Behaviour-Based Safety observations).

**The Golden Window of Investigation:**

$$\text{Investigation Effectiveness} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Time Elapsed Since Accident}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 24-Hour Rule:** Human memory fades rapidly, and physical evidence (fluid spills, broken parts, witness positions) is altered by cleanup, weather, and disturbance. KTU examiners specifically test the phrase **"investigation should begin as soon as possible, preferably within 24 hours, before evidence is lost."**

---

## 2.3 The "WHERE" of Accident Investigation

### 2.3.1 Where Should the Investigation Take Place?

The **Where** question addresses the **physical location and context** of the investigation.

**Locations of Investigation:**

- **At the Accident Site Itself** — Primary location. The scene must be preserved, cordoned off, and visited by the investigator before any restoration.
- **In the Workshop / Department** — Interviews with supervisors and coworkers.
- **In the Records Office** — Review of maintenance logs, training records, shift registers.
- **In the Design Office** — For equipment-failure-related accidents, design drawings and specifications are reviewed.
- **In the Hospital / Medical Facility** — For injury assessment and medical cause analysis.
- **At the Witness's Workplace / Home** — Sometimes interviews are conducted off-site for candid responses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Link:** "Where" is **not just a physical location** — it extends to the *contextual* location within the management system: was the SOP missing? Was the procedure in the wrong section of the manual? This dual interpretation is what KTU examiners look for in 14-mark answers.

---

## 2.4 The "WHO" of Accident Investigation

### 2.4.1 Who Should Investigate?

The **Who** question defines the **composition and qualifications** of the investigation team.

**Investigation Team Composition (KTU Board-Preferred Structure):**

| Role | Responsibility | Typical Designation |
|---|---|---|
| **Team Leader** | Coordinates the entire investigation, prepares the final report. | Senior Safety Officer / Plant Manager |
| **Safety Professional** | Provides technical safety expertise. | Safety Officer / HSE Manager |
| **Line Supervisor** | Provides process and operational knowledge. | Foreman / Shift In-charge |
| **Technical Expert** | Explains equipment / process failure. | Maintenance Engineer / Process Engineer |
| **Worker Representative** | Provides ground-level perspective. | Trade Union Representative / Senior Worker |
| **Medical Officer** | Assesses injury nature and human-factors. | Company Doctor / Occupational Health Officer |
| **External Specialist** | Provides independent analysis (for fatal/complex cases). | Government Inspector / External Consultant |

**Who Should NOT Investigate:**

- The person who was directly responsible for the area where the accident occurred (conflict of interest).
- The immediate supervisor of the injured person (bias).
- A person without proper training in investigation techniques.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Many students write *"Anyone can investigate."* The correct KTU answer is: *"Investigation should be conducted by a multi-disciplinary team led by a person trained in accident investigation techniques, with no conflict of interest."* Marks are deducted for vague answers.

---

## 2.5 The "HOW" of Accident Investigation

### 2.5.1 How Is an Accident Investigated?

The **How** question defines the **methodology, techniques, and procedural steps** of investigation. This is the most heavily tested aspect in KTU 14-mark questions.

**Standard Investigation Methodology (KTU Syllabus-Aligned):**

1. **Secure the Scene** — Cordon off the area, prevent disturbance.
2. **Preserve Evidence** — Photograph, sketch, mark, and document.
3. **Identify Witnesses** — List all persons who saw, heard, or were involved.
4. **Conduct Witness Interviews** — Open-ended, non-leading questions.
5. **Collect Physical Evidence** — Equipment, tools, materials, PPE, broken parts.
6. **Analyze Documents** — SOPs, permits, training records, maintenance logs.
7. **Develop the Sequence of Events** — Reconstruct the timeline.
8. **Identify Immediate Causes** — Unsafe Acts and Unsafe Conditions.
9. **Identify Root Causes** — Using techniques like 5-Why, Fishbone, Fault Tree.
10. **Recommend CAPA** — Corrective and Preventive Actions.
11. **Prepare the Investigation Report** — Structured, factual, signed-off.
12. **Implement and Follow Up** — Verify CAPA effectiveness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The **5-Why Analysis** and **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** are the two most frequently tested root-cause-analysis tools in KTU examinations. Memorize their steps.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix (Cheat Sheet)

| Question | Focus | KTU Key Phrase | Examiner's Marking Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| **WHY** | Justification | "To prevent recurrence, comply with law, identify root causes" | List 5+ distinct reasons for full marks. |
| **WHEN** | Timing | "Immediately, within 24 hours, before evidence is lost" | Mention the 24-hour rule explicitly. |
| **WHERE** | Location & Context | "At the accident scene, plus contextual location in management system" | Mention scene preservation. |
| **WHO** | Team Composition | "Multi-disciplinary team led by a trained investigator" | Avoid saying "anyone" or "the manager alone." |
| **HOW** | Methodology | "Secure → Preserve → Interview → Analyze → Report → Follow-up" | Use the 12-step methodology. |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Accident investigation is the **feedback loop** of any safety management system. Without it:

- Safety policies become static documents.
- Hazard registers become outdated.
- Training programs lose relevance.
- The **Heinrich's Domino Theory** (1931) and **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model** (1990) become academic exercises rather than applied tools.

In high-risk industries — oil & gas, construction, mining, chemical manufacturing — a single uninvestigated accident can cascade into a **major hazard incident (MHI)** with catastrophic loss of life, environmental damage, and financial ruin (e.g., Bhopal 1984, Texas City 2005).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** The **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)** is one of the most cited case studies in KTU examinations. The official investigation revealed that **no formal accident investigation was conducted for the preceding 11 minor leaks** in the months before the disaster. Had a proper WHY-WHEN-WHERE-WHO-HOW framework been applied, the tragedy could likely have been averted.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Methodology & The 5W+1H Matrix

Since accident investigation is a **procedural and analytical discipline** (rather than a mathematical one), Section 3 presents an **exhaustive, step-by-step analytical framework** that mirrors the rigor of an engineering derivation.

---

## 3.1 The Sequential Logic of the 5W+1H Framework

The investigation logic follows a **top-down funnel structure** — from broad justification to specific methodology.

### Step 1: Establish the JUSTIFICATION (WHY)

**Logical Statement:**
*"An accident investigation is justified when the expected benefit (prevention of future loss) exceeds the cost (time, resources, disruption) of conducting it."*

$$\text{Investigation Justified} \iff \text{Expected Loss Reduction} > \text{Investigation Cost}$$

**Expansion in KTU Terms:**

1. **Legal Compliance Justification**
   The Factories Act 1948 (Sections 88A, 88B) and the OSH Code 2020 (Chapter VI) mandate investigation and reporting of:
   - Fatal accidents
   - Serious bodily injuries
   - Dangerous occurrences (as notified in Schedule I)

2. **Moral Justification**
   Every worker has the right to a safe workplace (Article 21 of the Indian Constitution — Right to Life). Investigation honors this right.

3. **Economic Justification**
   Heinrich's Accident Cost Ratio (1931):

$$\text{Indirect Cost} : \text{Direct Cost} = 4 : 1 \quad \text{(Conservative estimate)}$$

   More recent studies (e.g., Bird & Germain, 1996) suggest ratios as high as **50:1**. Thus, the *true* cost of an accident is often **5× to 50×** the visible cost.

4. **Operational Justification**
   Investigation identifies bottlenecks, design flaws, and procedural gaps that affect productivity.

5. **Cultural Justification**
   A robust investigation culture builds trust, engagement, and ownership of safety.

---

### Step 2: Define the TRIGGER (WHEN)

**Logical Statement:**
*"An investigation is triggered by a defined event and must be initiated within a defined time window."*

**Trigger Matrix:**

| Trigger Event | Investigation Priority | Maximum Delay Permitted |
|---|---|---|
| Fatal Accident | **P0 — Immediate** | < 2 hours |
| Serious Injury (LTI) | **P1 — Urgent** | < 24 hours |
| Dangerous Occurrence | **P1 — Urgent** | < 24 hours |
| Minor Injury (First Aid) | **P2 — Standard** | < 72 hours |
| Near-Miss (High Potential) | **P2 — Standard** | < 7 days |
| Property Damage > ₹X | **P3 — Routine** | < 14 days |
| Process Deviation (No Loss) | **P4 — Proactive** | Next review cycle |

**Memory Decay Curve (Justification for Speed):**

$$\text{Witness Recall Accuracy} \approx e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \text{where } \lambda \approx 0.1 \text{ per day}$$

This means within **7 days**, recall accuracy can drop to ~50%, and within **30 days**, to ~5%.

---

### Step 3: Locate the CONTEXT (WHERE)

**Logical Statement:**
*"The investigation must occur at the physical scene of the accident and within the contextual environment of the management system."*

**Dual-Location Framework:**

**Physical Location:**
- The accident scene (primary)
- The equipment, machinery, or process involved
- The surrounding environment (lighting, noise, ventilation)
- The path of the injured person

**Contextual Location (within the SMS):**
- Which SOP was (or was not) followed?
- Which training module is (or is not) up to date?
- Which permit-to-work system was (or was not) used?
- Which design standard was (or was not) applied?

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When asked "Where to investigate?", always give a **two-part answer**: *(a) physical scene, (b) within the management system documents and records.*

---

### Step 4: Assemble the TEAM (WHO)

**Logical Statement:**
*"The investigation team must be multi-disciplinary, competent, and free from conflict of interest."*

**Team Selection Algorithm:**

$$\text{Team} = \{ \text{Safety Pro}, \text{Line Supervisor}, \text{Technical Expert}, \text{Worker Rep}, \text{Medical Officer} \}$$

**Step-by-Step Selection Logic:**

- **Identify the Type of Accident** (injury, fire, equipment, chemical).
- **Identify the Required Expertise** (mechanical, electrical, chemical, human-factors).
- **Select the Team Leader** (senior, trained, independent).
- **Assign Roles and Responsibilities** (interviewer, photographer, document analyst).
- **Check for Conflict of Interest** (no direct reports of the injured, no personal stake).
- **Brief the Team** (scope, timeline, confidentiality, methodology).

---

### Step 5: Execute the METHODOLOGY (HOW)

**Logical Statement:**
*"The investigation follows a defined sequence: Secure → Preserve → Collect → Analyze → Report → Follow-up."*

**The 12-Step Methodology (Detailed):**

**Step 5.1 — Secure the Scene**
- Cordon off with barrier tape.
- Stop all related operations.
- Post a guard if needed.
- *Purpose: prevent tampering, ensure safety of investigators.*

**Step 5.2 — Preserve Evidence**
- Photograph from multiple angles (wide, medium, close-up).
- Make sketches with measurements.
- Mark positions of victims, witnesses, equipment.
- Collect samples (fluids, materials, broken parts).
- *Purpose: create a permanent record.*

**Step 5.3 — Identify Witnesses**
- Anyone who saw, heard, or was in the vicinity.
- Witnesses must be interviewed separately to prevent collusion.
- *Purpose: multiple perspectives reduce bias.*

**Step 5.4 — Conduct Witness Interviews**
- Use the **"Open → Funnel → Close"** technique:
  1. *Open:* "Tell me what you saw, in your own words."
  2. *Funnel:* "What happened next? What was the time? Who else was there?"
  3. *Close:* "Is there anything else you think is important?"
- **Never** ask leading questions ("Didn't you see him wearing the wrong PPE?").
- Record the interview (with consent).

**Step 5.5 — Collect Physical Evidence**
- Tools, equipment, materials, PPE involved.
- Check maintenance records of the equipment.
- Check calibration of instruments.

**Step 5.6 — Analyze Documents**
- SOPs, work permits, JSAs (Job Safety Analyses), training records.
- Check whether procedures were followed.
- Check whether procedures were adequate.

**Step 5.7 — Develop the Sequence of Events (Timeline)**
- Construct a chronological timeline from first event to final outcome.
- Identify the **critical event** (the point of no return).

**Step 5.8 — Identify Immediate Causes**
- Categorize as **Unsafe Acts** (human errors) and **Unsafe Conditions** (physical/environmental hazards).
- Examples of Unsafe Acts: operating without authority, bypassing safety devices, improper PPE use.
- Examples of Unsafe Conditions: poor lighting, unguarded machinery, slippery floors.

**Step 5.9 — Identify Root Causes**
- Use **5-Why Analysis:**
  - *Why did the worker slip?* — The floor was oily.
  - *Why was the floor oily?* — A machine was leaking.
  - *Why was the machine leaking?* — A seal was worn out.
  - *Why was the seal worn out?* — Maintenance was overdue.
  - *Why was maintenance overdue?* — No preventive maintenance schedule existed.
  - **Root Cause:** Absence of a Preventive Maintenance System.
- Use **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** with 6 Ms: Man, Machine, Method, Material, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment).

**Step 5.10 — Recommend CAPA**
- **Corrective Action:** Stop the immediate problem (e.g., clean the spill, repair the seal).
- **Preventive Action:** Address the root cause (e.g., implement PM schedule, retrain workers).
- Assign responsibility, deadline, and verification method.

**Step 5.11 — Prepare the Investigation Report**
- Standard sections: Executive Summary, Background, Sequence of Events, Causes, Recommendations, Attachments.
- Report must be **factual, objective, signed, and dated**.

**Step 5.12 — Implement and Follow Up**
- Verify CAPA implementation.
- Measure effectiveness (KPIs, audits).
- Close the loop with management review.

---

## 3.2 The 5W+1H Analytical Matrix (Consolidated)

| Dimension | Core Question | Primary Output | KTU-Exam-Critical Concept |
|---|---|---|---|
| **WHY** | Why investigate? | Justification | Heinrich's 4:1 cost ratio, legal mandate, prevention |
| **WHEN** | When to investigate? | Trigger & Timing | 24-hour rule, witness memory decay |
| **WHERE** | Where to investigate? | Location & Context | Scene preservation, dual physical + contextual |
| **WHO** | Who investigates? | Team Composition | Multi-disciplinary, no conflict of interest |
| **HOW** | How to investigate? | Methodology | 12-step process, 5-Why, Fishbone |

---

## 3.3 The 5-Why Analysis — Worked Example (Exhaustive)

> **Scenario:** A worker falls from a scaffolding at a construction site and fractures his leg.

**The 5-Why Deduction:**

1. **Why did the worker fall?**
   *He lost his balance while stepping backward on the scaffold platform.*

2. **Why did he lose his balance?**
   *The platform was narrow (60 cm wide) and there was no guardrail on one side.*

3. **Why was the platform narrow and unguarded?**
   *The scaffold was erected by an untrained worker using substandard materials.*

4. **Why was an untrained worker allowed to erect the scaffold?**
   *There is no competency-based authorization system for scaffold erection in the company.*

5. **Why is there no competency-based authorization system?**
   *The company's Safety Management System does not include a procedure for high-risk construction activities — this is a management-system gap (root cause).*

**Root Cause Identified:** *Absence of a competency-based authorization system for high-risk construction activities.*

**Recommended CAPA:**
- *Corrective:* Stop all scaffolding work, install guardrails, retrain erectors.
- *Preventive:* Develop and implement a Competency Authorization Procedure, integrate into SMS, audit quarterly.

---

## 3.4 The Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram — Categories of Root Causes

For the same scenario, the fishbone categories would be:

- **Man (Human):** Untrained erector, worker not wearing harness.
- **Machine (Equipment):** Substandard scaffold tubes, missing guardrails.
- **Method (Procedure):** No SOP for scaffold erection, no permit-to-work.
- **Material:** Substandard planks, missing toe-boards.
- **Measurement (Monitoring):** No inspection of scaffold before use, no audit.
- **Mother Nature (Environment):** Windy conditions, soft ground causing scaffold tilt.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — The 5W+1H Accident Investigation Framework

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the **complete 5W+1H framework** as a top-down decision and process flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["ACCIDENT OCCURS"] --> node2["TRIGGER IDENTIFIED"]
    node2 --> node3["WHY: Justify the Investigation"]
    node3 --> node4["WHEN: Define Timing - 24 Hour Rule"]
    node4 --> node5["WHERE: Identify Physical and Contextual Locations"]
    node5 --> node6["WHO: Form Multi-Disciplinary Team"]
    node6 --> node7["HOW: Execute 12-Step Methodology"]

    node7 --> nodeA["Step 1: Secure the Scene"]
    nodeA --> nodeB["Step 2: Preserve Evidence"]

    nodeI --> nodeJ["Step 10: Recommend CAPA"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Step 11: Prepare Investigation Report"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Step 12: Implement and Follow Up"]

    nodeL --> nodeM["PREVENT RECURRENCE"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TL["Team Leader - Senior Safety Officer"] --> SP["Safety Professional - HSE Manager"]
    TL --> LS["Line Supervisor - Foreman or Shift In-charge"]
    TL --> TE["Technical Expert - Maintenance or Process Engineer"]
```

## 4.4 The Swiss Cheese Model — Why Investigations Matter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Hazards in the Workplace"] --> nodeL1["Layer 1 - Organizational Influences"]
    nodeL1 --> nodeL2["Layer 2 - Unsafe Supervision"]
    nodeL2 --> nodeL3["Layer 3 - Preconditions for Unsafe Acts"]
    nodeL3 --> nodeL4["Layer 4 - Unsafe Act"]
    nodeL4 --> nodeAcc{"Accident"}

    nodeH1["Hole in Layer 1 - Management Gap"] -.-> nodeAcc
    nodeH2["Hole in Layer 2 - Supervisor Negligence"] -.-> nodeAcc
    nodeH3["Hole in Layer 3 - Worker Untrained"] -.-> nodeAcc
    nodeH4["Hole in Layer 4 - Worker Takes Risk"] -.-> nodeAcc

    nodeInv["Accident Investigation"] --> nodeFix["Closes the Holes in All Layers"]
    nodeFix --> nodeL1
    nodeFix --> nodeL2
    nodeFix --> nodeL3
    nodeFix --> nodeL4

    style nodeAcc fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#C0392B,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeInv fill:#FFD43B,stroke:#F39C12,color:#000000
    style nodeFix fill:#51CF66,stroke:#2F9E44,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Conceptual Note:** This is Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990). Each layer of defense has "holes" (latent failures). An accident occurs only when holes align across all layers. **Investigation identifies the holes and recommends plugging them.**
