## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*"List any six investigation tools used in accident analysis and state the primary purpose of each."*

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

| # | Tool | Primary Purpose |
|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Camera (DSLR) | Photographic documentation of scene and physical marks |
| 2 | SCADA/DCS Historian | Retrieve time-stamped process variable data |
| 3 | GC-MS Spectrometer | Chemical identification of vapour/liquid residues |
| 4 | Total Station / LiDAR | Precise 3D spatial measurement |
| 5 | Structured Interview Forms | Standardized witness testimony collection |
| 6 | Fishbone Diagram | Multi-causal categorization of contributing factors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award **0.5 mark per tool** only if *both* name AND purpose are written. Half-answers get half-marks.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*"Explain the 'Cognitive Interview' technique used in witness handling."*

**Model Answer:**

The Cognitive Interview, developed by Fisher & Geiselman (1984), is a scientifically validated interview method designed to maximize accurate recall from witnesses while minimizing suggestibility bias. It consists of four sequential phases:

1. **Mental Re-instatement of Context:** Witness is asked to mentally recreate the original environment — sounds, lighting, weather, and their physical position.
2. **Free Recall in Narrative Form:** Witness narrates the event in their own words without interruption.
3. **Recall from Multiple Perspectives:** Witness is asked to describe events from the viewpoint of others present.
4. **Recall in Reverse Order:** Witness describes the event chronologically backwards to bypass narrative schemas.

**Advantages:** Reduces memory distortion; allows independent recall before probing; admissible in legal proceedings.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students who write only *"ask the witness what they saw"* get **0/3**. The Cognitive Interview technique must name all **four phases** to score full marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B carries **internal choice**. Both alternatives provided below are aligned with Module 4 outcomes.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Sample, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

*"Discuss in detail the step-by-step process of accident investigation with reference to a real industrial case study. Cover the following in your answer:*
*(a) The phases of investigation from immediate response to report submission. (7 marks)*
*(b) The various categories of investigation tools used, with specific examples of evidence collected in each category. (7 marks)"*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Phases of Investigation (7 marks):**

The investigation proceeds through **six sequential phases**:

**Phase 1 — Immediate Response (0–2 hrs):** Rescue injured personnel, isolate energy sources via LOTO, establish safety perimeter, appoint a Scene Custodian, prevent secondary incidents. *Key principle:* medical priority overrides evidence priority.

**Phase 2 — Notification and Team Formation (1–4 hrs):** Notify Factory Inspector, Fire Service, and senior management per the Emergency Response Plan. Constitute a multi-disciplinary team — typically a Safety Officer, Process Engineer, Maintenance Engineer, HR representative, and an external expert for major incidents.

**Phase 3 — Evidence Collection (4–48 hrs):** Systematically gather Marks (physical), Materials (chemical), Manuals (documentary), Memory (testimonial), and Machine (electronic) data — the **5M Protocol**.

**Phase 4 — Witness Interviewing (12–72 hrs):** Use Cognitive Interview or PEACE framework. Record statements in the witness's own words. Avoid leading questions. Cross-check multiple accounts for triangulation.

**Phase 5 — Causal Analysis (3–10 days):** Apply root-cause methodologies — 5-Why for simple cases, Bow-Tie / FTA / Swiss Cheese for complex cases. Differentiate **immediate (proximal) causes** from **root (distal) causes**.

**Phase 6 — Report & Follow-up (10–30 days):** Generate a structured report with SMART recommendations. Conduct verification audit at **T+90 days** to ensure closure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction examiners test:** *Proximal vs. Distal causes.* "Operator error" is proximal; "inadequate training system" is distal. KTU expects both.

**(b) Categories of Investigation Tools (7 marks):**

| Category | Tools | Evidence Example (Case Study) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Physical Evidence | Camera, measuring tape, LiDAR, borescope | Tank roof displacement 4.7 m measured |
| Chemical Evidence | Sampling kits, GC-MS, colorimetric tubes | Tank wall swab showed 87% methanol |
| Documentary Evidence | PTW register, logbooks, P&IDs, MSDS | PTW issued without gas test |
| Testimonial Evidence | Cognitive interview, audio-video | Three witnesses confirmed welding sparks |
| Electronic Evidence | SCADA historian, PLC logs, CCTV | Pressure ramp 1.2 → 8.9 kPa recorded |
| Analytical Methodology | 5-Why, Fishbone, Bow-Tie, FTA | Root cause: No PPM for N2 system |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often mention only "camera" and "interview" as tools, scoring **2/7**. KTU demands at least **three distinct categories** with **specific examples**. Generic answers without case-study application lose 3–4 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Sample, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

*"Handling witnesses is a critical skill in accident investigation.*
*(a) Explain the cognitive interview technique in detail, listing its four phases and the underlying psychology. (7 marks)*
*(b) Describe the common pitfalls in witness handling, with examples of leading questions and suggestibility bias. Propose a checklist of best practices for KTU-compliant witness interviewing. (7 marks)"*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cognitive Interview Technique (7 marks):**

The Cognitive Interview (CI) was developed by **Fisher & Geiselman (1984)** at the University of California, Los Angeles, based on experimental cognitive psychology. It addresses two competing demands: (i) retrieving **maximum accurate detail**, and (ii) avoiding **contamination** by interviewer bias.

**Underlying Psychology:**
- Memory is stored in **associative networks**; contextual cues activate related memories.
- Memory retrieval is **reconstructive**, not reproductive — each recall alters the memory trace.
- Witnesses benefit from **multiple retrieval routes** to access the same event.

**The Four Phases:**

| Phase | Psychological Mechanism | Example Question |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Mental Re-instatement of Context | Activates contextual memory engrams | *"Visualize the room — sounds, lighting, weather, your position."* |
| Free Recall (Uninterrupted Narrative) | Avoids output interference | *"Tell me everything in your own words, from start to finish."* |
| Multiple Perspective Recall | Activates alternative retrieval paths | *"What would a person at the opposite end have seen?"* |
| Reverse Order Recall | Bypasses schema-driven narrative distortion | *"Now describe the events in reverse chronological order."* |

**Variants:** The **Enhanced Cognitive Interview (ECI)** adds rapport-building and transfer-of-control techniques, used by UK police for sensitive cases.

**(b) Pitfalls & Best Practices (7 marks):**

**Common Pitfalls:**

| Pitfall | Definition | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Leading Questions | Question contains the expected answer | *"Didn't the operator bypass the interlock?"* |
| Confirmation Bias | Interviewer seeks info to confirm hypothesis | Only asking questions that fit pre-existing theory |
| Suggestibility | Interviewer's tone influences recall | *"You saw a spark, didn't you?"* (with raised eyebrow) |
| Memory Contamination | Witness absorbs info from other witnesses | Group debrief before individual interview |
| Cultural / Language Barriers | Miscommunication across linguistic groups | Using technical jargon with non-English native |
| Anxiety & Stress | High-stress environment impairs recall | Interviewing trauma victim without rapport |

**Best-Practice Checklist:**

| # | Item |
|:---|:---|
| 1 | Interview within **24 hours** when possible |
| 2 | Conduct in a **neutral, quiet, private** setting |
| 3 | Use **open-ended** questions first; closed questions only for clarification |
| 4 | Record with **written consent** (audio/video if permitted) |
| 5 | Allow the witness to say *"I don't know"* without pressure |
| 6 | **Separate witnesses** before interview to prevent cross-contamination |
| 7 | Document the **exact wording** of statements |
| 8 | Provide the witness a chance to **review and sign** the transcript |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common error is listing only pitfalls *without* the corrective practices. KTU 2024 requires both for full marks. Also, examiners **deduct 2 marks** if the psychological basis of the Cognitive Interview (associative network theory, reconstructive memory) is omitted.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Block — Read Before Exam**

- ☐ **Investigation is for PREVENTION, not PUNISHMENT.** (Single most-tested philosophical line.)
- ☐ Memorize the **6 phases in exact order**: Response → Notification → Evidence → Witness → Analysis → Report.
- ☐ Know the **4P Causal Framework**: People, Plant, Procedure, Premises.
- ☐ Memorize the **5M Evidence Protocol**: Marks, Materials, Manuals, Memory, Machine.
- ☐ Cite at least **6 tools** with specific purposes (camera, SCADA, GC-MS, LiDAR, Fishbone, Bow-Tie, FTA).
- ☐ Know **Cognitive Interview's 4 phases**: Context reinstatement, free recall, multiple perspectives, reverse order.
- ☐ Know **PEACE framework**: Preparation, Engage, Account, Closure, Evaluation.
- ☐ Differentiate **Proximal** (immediate) vs **Distal** (root) causes — examiners test this explicitly.
- ☐ Apply **Swiss Cheese Model** for any "major accident" question — it earns extra analytical marks.
- ☐ SMART recommendations: **Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound**.
- ☐ Verification audit scheduled at **T+90 days** (commonly expected figure).
- ☐ Methanol (case study): LEL 6%, UEL 36%, Flash Point 11°C, MIE 0.20 mJ — useful to mention for chemical incidents.
- ☐ Indian statutory timeline for report submission under Factories Act: **30 days**.
- ☐ Always conclude answers with **preventive** (not punitive) recommendations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Word of Advice:**
> KTU 2024 examiners reward answers that demonstrate **systems thinking** — never blame a single worker. Always trace causation upward to organizational, design, or policy levels. Quote at least one **model** (Swiss Cheese, Bow-Tie, or Reason's) and one **technique** (Cognitive Interview, 5-Why, Fishbone) per major answer for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accident Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT205 - PRINCIPLES OF SAFETY MANAGEMENT (SAFETY & FIRE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Accident Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accident (KTU 2024 Definition):** An unplanned, unexpected, and undesired event that results in or has the potential to result in personal injury, occupational illness, death, property damage, environmental harm, or a combination of these, arising out of or in connection with work activities.

**Accident Analysis** is the systematic, scientific, and structured methodology employed by safety professionals to:
1. **Identify** the root and contributing causes of an accident.
2. **Evaluate** the sequence of events leading to the incident.
3. **Determine** the deficiencies in the safety management system.
4. **Recommend** corrective and preventive actions (CAPA) to avoid recurrence.

According to the **International Labour Organization (ILO)** and **OSHA**, every accident, however minor, must be investigated because it is symptomatic of an underlying system failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology Distinction:**
> - **Incident** — A near-miss or undesirable event that did *not* result in injury or damage but had the *potential* to do so.
> - **Accident** — An unplanned event that *did* result in injury, loss, or damage.
> - **Hazard** — The inherent potential to cause harm.
> - **Risk** — The probability × consequence of that harm materialising.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor treating a patient who suddenly collapses. A good doctor does not just resuscitate the patient and walk away — they order blood tests, ECGs, and scans to find the *root cause* (e.g., a blocked artery). They then prescribe long-term treatment to ensure it does not happen again.

**Accident Analysis works the same way in a factory, construction site, or chemical plant.**
- **The accident** = the patient collapsing.
- **The first-aid treatment** = emergency response and shutdown.
- **The investigation** = the doctor's diagnostic tests.
- **The corrective action** = the long-term medication and lifestyle change.

If a factory worker slips and fractures an arm, simply bandaging the wound treats the *symptom*. Accident analysis asks: **Why did he slip?** (Cause) — Was the floor oily? (Immediate cause) — Why was oil on the floor? (Root cause: leaking valve + no preventive maintenance schedule). This layered thinking is the heart of accident analysis.

> [!TIP]
> **The Golden Rule of Safety (Frank Bird):** *“Every accident is preceded by a chain of causes, and every cause offers a chance to intervene.”* — This is why accident analysis is not about blame, but about *break*ing the causal chain.

---

## 1.3 Why Accident Analysis is Critical in the KTU 2024 Scheme

| Purpose | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|
| **Legal Compliance** | Mandatory under Factories Act 1948 (Sec. 88 & 88A), OSHA, and BIS codes. |
| **Cost Reduction** | Indirect costs (Heinrich's Iceberg Theory) are 4–10× the direct cost. |
| **System Improvement** | Reveals gaps in SOPs, training, design, and supervision. |
| **Performance Benchmarking** | Quantifies safety via LTIFR, TRIR, and Severity Rate. |
| **Cultural Change** | Moves organisation from *blame* to *learning*. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Accidents (KTU Board-Exam Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Expected Answer Style:** A clean classification table with one-line definitions of each category.

### A. Based on Severity
| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Fatal Accident** | Results in death of the worker. | Worker crushed under a fallen crane load. |
| **Serious / Disabling Injury** | Results in permanent disability or major lost time. | Amputation of a hand in a press machine. |
| **Lost-Time Injury (LTI)** | Injury that causes the worker to miss the next shift. | Sprained ankle from a fall. |
| **First-Aid Case (FAC)** | Minor injury treated on-site, no lost time. | Small cut from a sharp tool. |
| **Near-Miss (Incident)** | No injury, but had the potential. | A bolt falls from height but misses everyone. |
| **Property Damage Accident** | Equipment or material damaged, no human injury. | Forklift hits a rack and damages it. |

### B. Based on Cause
- **Personal Accidents** — caused by human factors (carelessness, fatigue).
- **Industrial Accidents** — caused by machinery, processes, or environment.
- **Traffic Accidents** — involving vehicles inside the premises.

### C. Based on Nature of Injury
- **Mechanical Injuries** — fractures, cuts, crushing.
- **Chemical Injuries** — burns, poisoning, asphyxiation.
- **Electrical Injuries** — shock, burns, cardiac arrest.
- **Radiation Injuries** — burns, long-term cellular damage.
- **Biological Injuries** — infections, toxic exposures.

---

## 2.2 Theories of Accident Causation (Theoretical Backbone)

This is a **HIGH-YIELD topic** for KTU exams. Any essay-style question (10/14 marks) frequently asks: *"Explain the theories of accident causation with neat diagrams."*

### 2.2.1 Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931) — *Single Cause*
H.W. Heinrich proposed that accidents occur due to a chain of five sequential factors, like dominos standing in a line. If one falls, the entire chain falls.

**The 5 Dominos:**
1. **Ancestry & Social Environment** — Heredity, upbringing, family background.
2. **Fault of Person** — Character defects, recklessness, ignorance.
3. **Unsafe Act / Unsafe Condition** — The immediate trigger.
4. **The Accident** — The visible event.
5. **The Injury** — The final consequence.

> Heinrich's principle: **Removing one domino** (typically Domino 3 — the unsafe act) **breaks the chain**.

> [!TIP]
> **Limitation of Domino Theory:** It oversimplifies by treating accidents as a *single linear chain*. Most modern accidents are caused by *multiple parallel* system failures.

### 2.2.2 Bird's Loss Causation Model (1966) — *Modern Domino*
Frank E. Bird updated Heinrich's model by recognising **management systems** as the root cause. He used data from over 1.7 million accidents.

**Sequence (5 phases):**
1. **Lack of Control by Management** (root).
2. **Basic Causes** — Personal factors (stress, fatigue) + Job factors (poor design, training).
3. **Immediate Causes** — Unsafe Acts + Unsafe Conditions.
4. **The Incident** — Contact with energy or substance.
5. **Loss** — To people, property, process, or environment.

> Bird's insight: **For every 1 major injury, there are 9 minor injuries, 30 property-damage accidents, and 600 near-misses (1:10:30:600 ratio).**

### 2.2.3 Adams' Overcaution/Oversimplification Theory
E. Adams argued that accidents result from a mismatch between the **operator's perception of risk** and the **actual risk**:
- **Oversimplification** — Worker underestimates risk ("This machine can't hurt me").
- **Overcaution** — Worker overestimates risk and behaves erratically.
- **Inexperience / Improper Training** — Wrong mental model of the system.

### 2.2.4 Human Factors Theory (Person-Machine-Environment)
Accidents occur when there is a mismatch between:
- **The Person** (physical, mental, psychological state).
- **The Machine** (design, ergonomics, automation).
- **The Environment** (lighting, noise, temperature, layout).

### 2.2.5 Epidemiological Theory
Borrowed from public health. Three factors:
- **Host** (the worker).
- **Agent** (the hazard — energy, substance).
- **Environment** (physical, social setting).
Accident = susceptibility of host + potency of agent + favourable environment.

### 2.2.6 Energy Release Theory (Gibson, 1961; Haddon, 1968)
Accidents occur when **uncontrolled or excessive energy** is released in an unexpected manner.
- Forms of energy: Mechanical, Electrical, Chemical, Thermal, Radiant, Acoustic.
- **Haddon's 10 Countermeasures** (widely asked in KTU):
  1. Prevent the creation of the hazard.
  2. Reduce the amount of energy.
  3. Prevent the release of energy.
  4. Slow the rate of release.
  5. Separate the energy release from the worker (in time/space).
  6. Use barriers (physical, distance).
  7. Strengthen the receiver (PPE).
  8. Evaluate damage quickly.
  9. Begin repair.
  10. Recondition the worker/process.

### 2.2.7 Systems Theory (Swiss Cheese Model — James Reason, 1990)
Each defence layer (training, SOPs, alarms, PPE) is like a slice of Swiss cheese with holes. **An accident occurs when the holes in multiple layers align**, allowing the hazard to reach the worker.

This is the **most widely accepted modern model** and is the basis of **Bow-Tie Analysis**.

---

## 2.3 Accident Investigation Process (Step-by-Step KTU Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "S-I-D-E-A-R":**
> **S**ecure the scene → **I**nitiate immediate response → **D**efine the scope → **E**vidence collection → **A**nalyse the chain → **R**ecommend & report.

| Step | Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Secure the Scene** | Isolate, prevent further harm, preserve evidence. | Safe zone established. |
| 2. **Notify & Mobilise** | Inform management, regulators, emergency services. | Communication log. |
| 3. **Form Investigation Team** | Multi-disciplinary: Safety officer, engineer, supervisor, workers' rep. | Team charter. |
| 4. **Gather Information** | Interviews, document review, photographs, video, data logs. | Evidence register. |
| 5. **Establish Sequence of Events** | Reconstruct the timeline using data (DCS, CCTV, witness). | Event sequence map. |
| 6. **Determine Causes** | Apply 5-Whys, Fishbone, Fault Tree. | Causal factor list. |
| 7. **Recommend CAPA** | Corrective (fix) + Preventive (avoid recurrence). | Action plan. |
| 8. **Report & Disseminate** | Formal report, share learnings. | Final investigation report. |

---

## 2.4 Tools of Accident Analysis (KTU Board-Exam Must-Know)

### 2.4.1 The 5-Whys Analysis
A simple but powerful technique where the analyst repeatedly asks **"Why?"** at least 5 times to drill down from the *symptom* to the *root cause*.
- Origin: Toyota Production System (Sakichi Toyoda).
- Example:
  - *Why did the worker fall?* The floor was wet.
  - *Why was the floor wet?* A pipe was leaking.
  - *Why was the pipe leaking?* It had corroded.
  - *Why had it corroded?* No periodic inspection.
  - *Why no inspection?* No scheduled maintenance plan in the SOP.

### 2.4.2 Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram
A cause-and-effect diagram shaped like a fish skeleton, categorising causes into standard **6 M's**: **Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment)**.
- Each "rib" of the fish represents a category.
- The "head" is the problem (the accident).
- Used to systematically brainstorm *all possible* causes.

### 2.4.3 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)
A **top-down, deductive** logic diagram.
- **Top event** = the accident.
- Branches down through AND/OR gates to **basic events** (root causes).
- Quantitative FTA can calculate the **probability of the top event**.

### 2.4.4 Event Tree Analysis (ETA)
A **bottom-up, inductive** logic diagram.
- Starts with an **initiating event** and traces all possible outcomes (success/failure of safety barriers).
- Used to calculate accident *consequences* and *frequencies*.

### 2.4.5 Bow-Tie Analysis
Combines FTA (causes on the left) + ETA (consequences on the right), with **proactive barriers** (left side) and **reactive barriers** (right side) separating the hazard from the outcome.
- Industry standard in oil & gas, petrochemical, mining (mandated by COMAH regulations).

### 2.4.6 Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)
A **proactive, systematic** method to evaluate potential failure modes of a process, equipment, or system, their effects, and criticality.
- **RPN** (Risk Priority Number) = **S**everity × **O**ccurrence × **D**etection.
- Higher RPN = higher priority for corrective action.

### 2.4.7 Pareto Analysis (80/20 Rule)
Used to identify the *vital few* causes responsible for 80% of accidents. Bar chart of cause-frequency, with a cumulative line.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Accident Statistics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are heavily tested in the numerical part of KTU questions. Memorise the numerator/denominator positions and the multiplier.**

| Formula | Equation | Variables | Unit | Standard Multiplier |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Rate (FR)** | $FR = \dfrac{\text{Number of Injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Lost-time injuries; Man-hours | Per million man-hours | $1{,}000{,}000$ |
| **Severity Rate (SR)** | $SR = \dfrac{\text{Lost Work-Days} \times 10^3}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Days lost; Man-hours | Per thousand man-hours | $1{,}000$ |
| **Incidence Rate (IR)** | $IR = \dfrac{\text{Number of Injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Avg. Employees} \times \text{Work-Days/Year} \times 8 \text{ hrs}}$ | Injuries; Employees; Days | Per million man-hours | $1{,}000{,}000$ |
| **LTIFR** (Lost-Time Injury Frequency Rate) | $LTIFR = \dfrac{\text{Lost-Time Injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Same as FR | Per million man-hours | $1{,}000{,}000$ |
| **LTISR** (Lost-Time Injury Severity Rate) | $LTISR = \dfrac{\text{Lost Work-Days} \times 10^6}{\text{Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Days lost; Man-hours | Per million man-hours | $1{,}000{,}000$ |
| **TRIR** (Total Recordable Incident Rate, OSHA) | $TRIR = \dfrac{\text{Recordable Injuries} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Recordables; Man-hours | Per 100 FTE/year | $200{,}000$ |
| **DART Rate** | $DART = \dfrac{(\text{Days Away + Restricted}) \times 200{,}000}{\text{Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Days away/restricted | Per 100 FTE/year | $200{,}000$ |
| **Accident Index (Heinrich)** | $AI = FR \times SR$ | FR; SR | Composite | — |
| **FMEA — RPN** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Severity (1–10); Occurrence (1–10); Detection (1–10) | Unitless score | — |
| **FTA — Top Event Probability (OR gate)** | $P_{top} = 1 - \prod_{i}(1 - p_i)$ | Individual basic-event probabilities | Probability (0–1) | — |
| **ETA — Consequence Probability** | $P_{consequence} = P_{initiator} \times \prod P_{barrier}$ | Initiator; barrier probabilities | Probability (0–1) | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Why 200,000 in OSHA formulas?**
> Because one Full-Time Employee (FTE) works 50 weeks × 40 hours = **2,000 hrs/year**. To express the rate *per 100 FTE/year*, you multiply by $100 \times 2{,}000 = 200{,}000$.

---

## 2.6 Accident Cost Analysis — Heinrich's Iceberg Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heinrich's Iceberg Model:** Visible *direct* costs (medical, insurance) are only the *tip of the iceberg* above water. The massive *hidden* indirect costs (production loss, legal, morale, replacement) lie below.

| Visible (Direct) Costs — *10%* | Hidden (Indirect) Costs — *90%* |
|---|---|
| Medical expenses | Wages paid to injured worker on idle time |
| Insurance premiums | Damaged equipment / material |
| Compensation claims | Production delays / lost output |
| Ambulance / hospital | Cost of training replacement worker |
| Legal fines | Investigation time |
| | Loss of customer goodwill |
| | Damage to company reputation |
| | Lower morale → more accidents (cycle) |

**Total Cost of an Accident (TCOA)** is typically estimated as:
$$TCOA = \text{Direct Cost} + k \times \text{Direct Cost}, \quad k \in [4, 10]$$

where $k$ is the **indirect-to-direct cost ratio** (industry empirical value).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Problem Demonstration (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example — Fully solved in KTU valuation style. This is the exact format expected in the 14-mark numerical question.**

### **Problem (Model Question):**
> A manufacturing plant recorded the following safety data for the year 2023:
> - Total number of employees: **1,200**
> - Average working days/year: **300**
> - Average working hours/day: **8**
> - Number of reportable lost-time injuries: **24**
> - Total man-days lost due to these injuries: **480**
>
> Calculate:
> **(a)** Frequency Rate (FR)
> **(b)** Severity Rate (SR)
> **(c)** Incidence Rate (IR)
> **(d)** Accident Index (AI)
> **(e)** Comment on the safety performance if the industry standard is FR ≤ 5.0 and SR ≤ 0.4.

---

### **Solution (Step-by-Step):**

#### Step 1: Compute Total Man-Hours Worked
$$N_{\text{employees}} = 1{,}200$$
$$D_{\text{days/year}} = 300$$
$$H_{\text{hrs/day}} = 8$$

The total man-hours worked is the product of the three quantities.

$$MH = N_{\text{employees}} \times D_{\text{days/year}} \times H_{\text{hrs/day}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$MH = 1{,}200 \times 300 \times 8$$

Multiplying sequentially:

$$MH = 1{,}200 \times 2{,}400$$

$$MH = 2{,}880{,}000 \text{ man-hours/year}$$

---

#### Step 2: Compute Frequency Rate (FR)
The Frequency Rate normalises the number of injuries per million man-hours worked. This is the KTU standard.

$$FR = \dfrac{N_{\text{injuries}} \times 10^{6}}{MH}$$

Substituting the known values:

$$FR = \dfrac{24 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{2{,}880{,}000}$$

$$FR = \dfrac{24{,}000{,}000}{2{,}880{,}000}$$

$$FR = 8.33 \text{ injuries per million man-hours}$$

---

#### Step 3: Compute Severity Rate (SR)
The Severity Rate is computed per thousand man-hours (KTU convention) using the days lost.

$$SR = \dfrac{D_{\text{lost}} \times 10^{3}}{MH}$$

Substituting the known values:

$$SR = \dfrac{480 \times 1{,}000}{2{,}880{,}000}$$

$$SR = \dfrac{480{,}000}{2{,}880{,}000}$$

$$SR = 0.167 \text{ days lost per thousand man-hours}$$

---

#### Step 4: Compute Incidence Rate (IR)
The Incidence Rate uses the average number of employees and the work-year conversion factor.

$$IR = \dfrac{N_{\text{injuries}} \times 10^{6}}{N_{\text{employees}} \times D_{\text{days/year}} \times 8 \text{ hrs/day}}$$

The denominator is identical to $MH$, so we can reuse the calculated value.

$$IR = \dfrac{24 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}200 \times 300 \times 8}$$

$$IR = \dfrac{24{,}000{,}000}{2{,}880{,}000}$$

$$IR = 8.33 \text{ per million man-hours}$$

> *Note:* Frequency Rate and Incidence Rate are numerically equal in this case because the multiplier and the man-hour base coincide. In general, they are *different* metrics that depend on how the base is defined.

---

#### Step 5: Compute Accident Index (AI)
The Accident Index is the product of the Frequency Rate and the Severity Rate, as defined by Heinrich.

$$AI = FR \times SR$$

Substituting the calculated values:

$$AI = 8.33 \times 0.167$$

$$AI = 1.39 \text{ (composite unitless index)}$$

---

#### Step 6: Comparative Comment on Safety Performance
- Industry standard for Frequency Rate: $FR \leq 5.0$. Calculated $FR = 8.33$ → **Higher than the standard → Unfavourable.**
- Industry standard for Severity Rate: $SR \leq 0.4$. Calculated $SR = 0.167$ → **Lower than the standard → Favourable.**

**Interpretation:** The plant has a *higher frequency* of injuries (more incidents) than the benchmark, but each injury is *less severe* on average. The high FR suggests issues with **frequency control** (training, supervision, SOPs), while the low SR indicates that **injury management and first-aid response** are good.

> **Recommendation:** Focus on **preventive controls** — root-cause analysis of recurring minor injuries, enhanced safety training, machine guarding, and behavioural-based safety (BBS) programmes to bring FR below 5.0.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Tool Selection for Different Accident Types

| Tool | Type | When to Use | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **5-Whys** | Reactive, Qualitative | Single-incident, quick root-cause | Floor-level shop-floor inquiry |
| **Fishbone** | Reactive, Qualitative | Multi-factor brainstorming | Group investigation workshops |
| **FTA** | Proactive/Reactive, Quantitative | Complex systems with logic dependencies | Process plant, nuclear, aviation |
| **ETA** | Proactive/Reactive, Quantitative | Consequence modelling | Off-shore, chemical storage |
| **Bow-Tie** | Proactive/Reactive, Visual | High-consequence industry compliance | Oil & gas, COMAH sites |
| **FMEA** | Proactive, Quantitative | Design & reliability engineering | Equipment procurement, new line |
| **Pareto** | Reactive, Quantitative | Identifying the vital-few causes | Annual safety review |
| **Swiss Cheese (Reason)** | Conceptual, Qualitative | Organisational culture analysis | Aviation, healthcare, mining |

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analysis: Heinrich vs. Bird vs. Reason

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequently asked 10-mark question:** "Compare and contrast the accident causation theories of Heinrich, Bird, and Reason."

| Parameter | **Heinrich (1931)** | **Bird (1966)** | **Reason (1990)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Era** | Pre-WWII industrial | Post-war industrial | Modern, high-tech |
| **Root Cause** | Ancestry & character | Lack of management control | Organisational defences (latent failures) |
| **Model Type** | Linear, single cause | Linear, multi-cause | Multi-layer, parallel |
| **Dominoes** | 5 dominos | 5 dominos (revised) | Swiss cheese slices |
| **Focus** | Worker behaviour | Management system | Organisational culture |
| **Data Base** | ~5,000 accidents | ~1.7 million accidents | Aviation & nuclear industries |
| **Key Insight** | 88:10:1 ratio (1 fatal : 10 serious : 600 no-injury) | 1 : 10 : 30 : 600 ratio | Latent vs. active failures |
| **Limitation** | Blames worker; oversimplified | Still linear; ignores complexity | Hard to quantify layers |

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["ORGANISATIONAL INFLUENCES"] --> S2["SUPERVISORY FACTORS"]
    S2 --> S3["PRECURSORS Unsafe Acts and Conditions"]
    S3 --> S4["THE ACCIDENT"]

    S7 --> S8["WORKER AND PUBLIC"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each rectangle represents a "slice" of Swiss cheese. In reality, each has *holes* (latent failures). An accident occurs when the *holes align* across all layers. Removing one layer (or one hole) breaks the trajectory.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*Define the term "accident" as per the Factories Act, 1948. Differentiate between an "accident" and a "near-miss" with one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **accident**, as per **Section 88** of the Factories Act, 1948, is an occurrence in a factory that causes, or has the potential to cause, bodily injury to any person inside the factory premises, during the course of work. Reporting within **24 hours** is mandatory for certain categories.
>
> **Difference Table:**
>
> | Parameter | Accident | Near-Miss |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Outcome** | Actual injury or damage occurs | *Could* have caused harm, but did not |
> | **Reporting** | Mandatory under Sec. 88 | Not legally mandatory, but best practice |
> | **Example** | A worker cuts his finger on a blade | A blade falls from a workbench but misses the worker |
> | **Learning value** | Lower (costly lesson) | High (cheap warning sign) |

*(1 mark for Factories Act definition + 1 mark for differentiation table + 1 mark for examples.)*

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
*List any **six (6)** accident causation theories. Name the proponent of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Domino Theory** — H.W. **Heinrich** (1931).
2. **Loss Causation Model (Modern Domino)** — Frank E. **Bird** (1966).
3. **Energy Release Theory** — **William Gibson** (1961); expanded by **William Haddon Jr.** (1968).
4. **Epidemiological Theory** — Adapted from public health by **Gordon** (1949).
5. **Swiss Cheese Model** — **James Reason** (1990).
6. **Adams' Overcaution/Oversimplification Theory** — **E. Adams** (1976).
7. **Human Factors Theory** — Multiple contributors (Fitts, Jones, 1947).

*(½ mark each for the theory name + ½ mark for the proponent. Six correct pairs = 3 marks.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** Two 14-mark questions are provided. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks.

---

### **Question A. [14 Marks] — Accident Causation Theories + Numerical Statistics** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

#### (a) Explain in detail **Heinrich's Domino Theory** of accident causation with a neat diagram. List its **five (5) limitations**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931) — H.W. Heinrich:**
H.W. Heinrich, an engineer at the Travellers Insurance Company, studied approximately 5,000 workplace accidents and proposed that accidents result from a *chain of sequential factors*, visually represented as five dominos standing in a line. The fall of the first domino causes the fall of the next, eventually leading to injury.

**The Five Dominos:**

1. **Domino 1 — Ancestry and Social Environment:**
   Hereditary traits, family upbringing, socioeconomic status, and social influences that shape a person's character, intelligence, and disposition toward risk. Heinrich believed that a worker's *innate personality* is the *first* factor in the accident chain.

2. **Domino 2 — Fault of Person:**
   Personal shortcomings such as recklessness, stubbornness, nervousness, excitability, or ignorance. These traits lead to dangerous mental states that make the person *prone* to unsafe behaviour.

3. **Domino 3 — Unsafe Act and Unsafe Condition:**
   The *immediate trigger* of the accident. Examples of unsafe acts: operating machinery without authority, removing safety devices, working at unsafe speeds. Examples of unsafe conditions: unguarded machinery, poor lighting, defective tools.

4. **Domino 4 — The Accident:**
   The visible, unwanted event — the actual moment of contact with the energy source (fall, strike, electric shock, chemical exposure, etc.).

5. **Domino 5 — The Injury or Loss:**
   The final outcome — bodily harm (cut, fracture, burn, death) or property/equipment damage.

**Heinrich's Key Principle:** *If any one of the five dominos is removed*, the entire chain is broken and the injury is prevented. Heinrich emphasised that the **third domino (unsafe act/condition)** is the most practical and impactful point of intervention, since the first two are hard to control in adult life.

**Heinrich's Famous Ratios (also 2 marks expected):**
For every **1 major/fatal injury**, there are:
- **29** minor injuries
- **300** no-injury accidents (near-misses)
i.e., the **1 : 29 : 300** ratio (later modified to **1 : 10 : 30 : 600** by Bird).

**Five Limitations of the Theory:**

1. **Blaming the Worker:** Heinrich places the root cause on the worker's ancestry and personality, which encourages a "blame culture" rather than systemic improvement.
2. **Linear, Single-Cause Model:** It assumes a *one-direction* chain, whereas real accidents have *parallel* and *interacting* causes.
3. **Ignores Management Responsibility:** The model does not acknowledge that management decisions (design, training, supervision) are often the true root cause.
4. **Based on Limited Data:** The 5,000-accident sample was not globally representative, reducing generalisability.
5. **No Differentiation of Severities:** The model treats all accidents equally, ignoring the vast difference between a near-miss and a fatality.

*(3 marks for the five dominos with explanations, 2 marks for the diagram, 1 mark for Heinrich's ratios, 1 mark for the key principle.)*

#### (b) A factory employs **800 workers**. The plant works **300 days/year** at **8 hours/day**. In 2023, there were **15 lost-time injuries** and **450 man-days lost**. Calculate the **Frequency Rate, Severity Rate, and Accident Index**. Comment on the safety performance with reference to industry standards (FR ≤ 4.0, SR ≤ 0.5). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Total Man-Hours Worked.**

The total man-hours is the product of employees, working days, and hours per day.

$$MH = 800 \times 300 \times 8$$

Sequential multiplication:

$$MH = 800 \times 2{,}400$$

$$MH = 1{,}920{,}000 \text{ man-hours}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Frequency Rate (FR).**

The FR is injuries per million man-hours.

$$FR = \dfrac{15 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}920{,}000}$$

$$FR = \dfrac{15{,}000{,}000}{1{,}920{,}000}$$

$$FR = 7.81 \text{ per million man-hours}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Severity Rate (SR).**

The SR is days lost per thousand man-hours.

$$SR = \dfrac{450 \times 1{,}000}{1{,}920{,}000}$$

$$SR = \dfrac{450{,}000}{1{,}920{,}000}$$

$$SR = 0.234 \text{ days lost per thousand man-hours}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Accident Index (AI).**

The AI is the product of FR and SR.

$$AI = 7.81 \times 0.234$$

$$AI = 1.83 \text{ (unitless composite index)}$$

**Step 5: Comparative Analysis.**

| Metric | Calculated | Industry Standard | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency Rate | **7.81** | ≤ 4.0 | **Unfavourable** (1.95× the limit) |
| Severity Rate | **0.234** | ≤ 0.5 | **Favourable** (well within limit) |
| Accident Index | **1.83** | Lower is better | Moderate — driven by high FR |

**Interpretation & Recommendation:**
The plant's high Frequency Rate suggests a **frequency control problem** — too many injuries are occurring. The low Severity Rate, however, indicates that **injury management, first aid, and emergency response are excellent**, preventing minor injuries from becoming serious. Recommended actions: conduct **root-cause analysis** of recurring incidents, strengthen **behavioural-based safety (BBS) programmes**, intensify **supervisory training**, install **machine guards**, and review **SOPs** for high-risk tasks.

*(1 mark for man-hour calculation, 1.5 marks for FR, 1.5 marks for SR, 1 mark for AI, 2 marks for analysis & recommendation.)*

---

### **Question B. [14 Marks] — Bow-Tie + Accident Cost Analysis** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### (a) With a neat **Bow-Tie diagram**, explain the **Bow-Tie method of accident analysis**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Bow-Tie Analysis — Definition:**
The Bow-Tie method is a **risk-evaluation** technique that visually combines **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** on the *left side* (showing causes) and **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** on the *right side* (showing consequences), connected through a **central knot** representing the **top event** (the accident or undesired event). The method is widely used in the oil, gas, petrochemical, mining, and aviation industries, and is mandated under the **COMAH (Control of Major Accident Hazards) Regulations** in the UK and EU.

**Structure of a Bow-Tie Diagram:**

The diagram takes the shape of a bow-tie, hence the name.

1. **Centre — The Hazard:**
   The source of potential harm. Example: pressurised LPG storage tank, reactive chemical, or a high-voltage busbar.

2. **Left Side — Threats (Causes):**
   The events or conditions that can *release* the hazard and lead to the top event. Example: overfilling, valve failure, external impact, corrosion, sensor malfunction.

3. **Left-Hand Barriers — Proactive Controls:**
   Preventive measures *between the threats and the top event*. These reduce the *probability* of the top event occurring. Example: level sensors, maintenance schedules, training, SOPs, alarms.

4. **Centre Knot — The Top Event (Loss of Control):**
   The moment the hazard is released — e.g., a tank overflow, an uncontrolled chemical reaction, an electrical arc. This is the *point of no return*.

5. **Right-Hand Barriers — Reactive Controls (Mitigation):**
   Measures that *limit the consequences* if the top event occurs. Example: bund walls, fire suppression, evacuation procedures, PPE, emergency shutdown systems (ESD).

6. **Right Side — Consequences (Outcomes):**
   The ultimate losses if all barriers fail — e.g., fire, explosion, fatality, environmental pollution, equipment loss.

**Escalation Factors and Degradation Mechanisms:**
Each barrier has associated *escalation factors* (factors that degrade the barrier's effectiveness) such as: human error, ageing equipment, weather, lack of training, and alarm fatigue.

**Advantages of Bow-Tie Analysis (5 points):**

1. **Visual Clarity:** The single diagram consolidates causes, controls, and consequences — easy to communicate to all stakeholders, including non-technical workers and management.
2. **Comprehensive:** Combines the best of FTA (causal analysis) and ETA (consequence analysis) in one integrated picture.
3. **Barrier Management:** Highlights the *role of every safeguard* (proactive and reactive), making it easy to identify weak or missing barriers.
4. **Quantitative Extension:** Barrier effectiveness can be quantified (probability of failure on demand) for use in **LOPA** (Layer of Protection Analysis).
5. **Regulatory Acceptance:** Widely recognised by regulators (HSE, OSHA, COMAH) and used as the basis for **Safety Case** submissions.
6. **Easy to Update:** When a barrier fails, it is easy to update the diagram with lessons learned.

**Limitations (3 points):**

1. **Static Snapshot:** The Bow-Tie is a *snapshot* in time. It does not capture dynamic, time-dependent failure modes.
2. **No Human Factors Detail:** The diagram does not drill down into the *psychological and ergonomic* reasons behind unsafe acts.
3. **Requires Expert Facilitation:** Building a meaningful Bow-Tie requires cross-functional expertise; otherwise, key threats or barriers may be missed.
4. **Subjective Quantification:** Assigning probabilities to barriers can be subjective without a strong incident database.

*(3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for structure explanation, 1 mark for advantages, 1 mark for limitations.)*

#### (b) Explain **Heinrich's Iceberg Theory** of accident costs. A factory incurred a direct cost of **₹ 5,00,000** in a single accident. Estimate the total cost of the accident if the indirect-to-direct cost ratio is **8:1**. List **five (5) categories of indirect costs**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Heinrich's Iceberg Theory:**
H.W. Heinrich, in his book *"Industrial Accident Prevention: A Scientific Approach"* (1931), proposed that the **costs of an industrial accident** are analogous to an **iceberg floating in the ocean**. The visible tip represents only a small fraction (about 10%) of the total cost — the *direct* costs that are easily identifiable and recorded. The massive submerged portion, hidden below the waterline, represents the *indirect* (hidden) costs that often go unrecorded but constitute the bulk (about 90%) of the total cost burden.

**Key Insight:** *Focusing only on direct costs grossly underestimates the true financial impact of accidents on the organisation.*

**Direct Costs (Visible — Above Waterline, ~10%):**
These are easily measurable expenses directly tied to the accident:
- Medical expenses and hospitalisation
- Insurance premiums and compensation
- Repair or replacement of damaged equipment
- Legal fines and settlements
- Ambulance and emergency services

**Indirect Costs (Hidden — Below Waterline, ~90%):**
These are the subtle, often-overlooked costs that have a cumulative effect:
1. **Wages Paid to Injured Worker During Idle Time** — The worker is paid for the day even if unable to work.
2. **Cost of Lost Production / Output** — Line stoppage, machine downtime, missed delivery deadlines.
3. **Cost of Training the Replacement Worker** — Hiring or training a substitute reduces productivity of supervisors and trainers.
4. **Damage to Equipment and Material Beyond the Initial** — Hidden secondary damage, electrical faults caused by the accident.
5. **Loss of Morale and Decreased Productivity of Other Workers** — Witnessing an accident creates fear and reduces output.
6. **Cost of Investigation, Reports, and Administrative Time** — Management and safety officer time spent on paperwork.
7. **Damage to Company Reputation and Loss of Customer Goodwill** — Negative publicity, loss of contracts, tarnished brand.
8. **Increased Insurance Premiums in the Long Term** — Insurers may revise premiums based on the loss record.
9. **Legal Costs and Litigation** — Even when not immediately direct, lawsuits can be indirect long-term costs.

**Numerical Computation:**

Given:
- Direct cost: $C_{direct} = \text{₹ } 5{,}00{,}000$
- Indirect-to-direct cost ratio: $k = 8$

The total cost is the sum of direct and indirect costs:

$$C_{total} = C_{direct} + C_{indirect}$$

The indirect cost is the ratio multiplied by the direct cost:

$$C_{indirect} = k \times C_{direct} = 8 \times 5{,}00{,}000$$

$$C_{indirect} = \text{₹ } 40{,}00{,}000$$

Substituting into the total cost equation:

$$C_{total} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}00{,}000$$

$$C_{total} = \text{₹ } 45{,}00{,}000$$

**Alternative Compact Formula:**

$$C_{total} = C_{direct} \times (1 + k) = 5{,}00{,}000 \times (1 + 8) = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 9 = \text{₹ } 45{,}00{,}000$$

**Interpretation:**
The direct cost of ₹ 5,00,000 looks manageable, but the *true* financial impact of the accident is **9 times higher** at ₹ 45,00,000. This demonstrates that *every rupee* invested in safety has the potential to save *nine rupees* in accident-related losses. This is the financial basis for arguing safety budgets to top management.

*(2 marks for the iceberg concept, 3 marks for five indirect cost categories, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the final answer and interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Wrong Multiplier in Formulas:** Mixing up the $10^{6}$ and $10^{3}$ multipliers in FR vs. SR. **Memory aid:** FR uses $10^{6}$ (larger multiplier, larger base), SR uses $10^{3}$ (smaller multiplier, smaller base).
> 2. **Forgetting the Diagram:** In a 7-mark theory question, *always* draw a labelled diagram. A 7-mark question without a diagram typically caps at 4–5 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Heinrich's Ratios:** Heinrich gave **1 : 29 : 300**, Bird refined it to **1 : 10 : 30 : 600**. Examiners *do* check the difference.
> 4. **Mistaking LTIFR for TRIR:** TRIR uses the **200,000** multiplier (per 100 FTE/year), LTIFR uses **1,000,000** (per million man-hours).
> 5. **Not Concluding the Numerical:** A numerical answer without a comparison to the industry standard and a recommendation loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Domino Diagram Without Arrows:** Always show directional arrows. A domino without an arrow does not communicate the causal chain.
> 7. **Skipping Units:** Always write the unit (per million man-hours, per 100 FTE/year) in the final answer line.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU Board Revision Sheet — Memorise the bullets below.**

### 🔑 Definitions
- **Accident** = Unplanned event causing injury, damage, or loss.
- **Near-Miss** = Unplanned event *with potential* to cause harm.
- **Hazard** = Source of potential harm. **Risk** = Probability × Consequence.
- **Direct Cost** = Visible, easily measurable. **Indirect Cost** = Hidden, 4–10× the direct cost.
- **Frequency Rate** = Injuries per **million** man-hours. **Severity Rate** = Days lost per **thousand** man-hours.

### 🔑 Causation Theories — One-Line Recall
- **Heinrich (1931):** 5-domino chain, blames ancestry. Ratio 1:29:300.
- **Bird (1966):** Revised dominoes, blames management. Ratio 1:10:30:600.
- **Adams (1976):** Overcaution vs. Oversimplification mismatch.
- **Haddon (1968):** Energy release + 10 countermeasures.
- **Reason (1990):** Swiss cheese model — latent vs. active failures.
- **Epidemiological:** Host + Agent + Environment (like disease).

### 🔑 Haddon's 10 Countermeasures (Memorise the Sequence)
Prevent creation → Reduce amount → Prevent release → Slow release → Separate in time/space → Use barriers → Strengthen receiver (PPE) → Damage evaluation → Begin repair → Recondition.

### 🔑 Analysis Tools — Recall Map
- **5-Whys** → Quick, single-incident.
- **Fishbone** → Multi-cause brainstorming (6M's).
- **FTA** → Top-down, deductive.
- **ETA** → Bottom-up, inductive.
- **Bow-Tie** → Hazard-centric, barrier-based.
- **FMEA** → RPN = S × O × D.
- **Pareto** → Vital few vs. trivial many (80/20).

### 🔑 Numerical Formulas (Quick Reference Card)
- $FR = \dfrac{N \times 10^{6}}{MH}$
- $SR = \dfrac{D \times 10^{3}}{MH}$
- $IR = \dfrac{N \times 10^{6}}{N_{emp} \times D \times 8}$
- $TRIR = \dfrac{N_{rec} \times 200{,}000}{MH}$
- $AI = FR \times SR$
- $RPN = S \times O \times D$
- $C_{total} = C_{direct} \times (1 + k)$

### 🔑 KTU High-Frequency Keywords (Use in Answers for Higher Marks)
- "**Root cause**" instead of "cause".
- "**Proactive barrier**" and "**reactive barrier**" (Bow-Tie terminology).
- "**Latent failure**" and "**active failure**" (Reason's terminology).
- "**Energy transfer**" (Haddon's terminology).
- "**CAPA**" (Corrective and Preventive Action).

### 🔑 Heinrich's Iceberg Rule of Thumb
For every ₹ 1 of direct cost, expect **₹ 4 to ₹ 10** of indirect cost. Always use a range, not a single number, unless the data specifies the exact ratio.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analytical Techniques - System Safety - Change Analysis - MORT - Multi Events Sequencing - TOR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT205 - PRINCIPLES OF SAFETY MANAGEMENT (SAFETY & FIRE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analytical Techniques - System Safety - Change Analysis - MORT - Multi Events Sequencing - TOR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 System Safety — The Foundational Concept

**System Safety** is an engineering and management discipline that systematically identifies, evaluates, and eliminates or controls hazards throughout the entire life cycle of a system — from concept and design, through manufacture, operation, and finally to decommissioning. Unlike traditional safety approaches that react *after* accidents occur, system safety is a **proactive, anticipatory, and integrated** methodology embedded into the very fabric of system design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** System Safety is the systematic application of engineering and management principles, criteria, and techniques to optimize all aspects of safety within the constraints of operational effectiveness, time, and cost throughout all phases of the system life cycle.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "House Building" Intuition

Imagine you are constructing a house. A **reactive safety approach** is like testing whether the staircase holds weight *after* building it — if it cracks, you rebuild. A **system safety approach** is like an architect calculating structural loads, choosing fire-resistant materials, planning escape routes, and reinforcing the foundation **on the blueprint** before a single brick is laid. System Safety is the "architect of accident prevention" — it builds safety *into* the design, not *onto* it.

### Core Metrics in System Safety

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value/Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Probability of Failure** | $P_f$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-9}$/hour | Critical system reliability |
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | MTBF | Hours/Cycles | Equipment maintenance |
| **Fatal Accident Rate** | FAR | **1–10 per $10^8$ man-hours** | Industry risk benchmarking |
| **Risk Tolerance Threshold** | $R_T$ | $10^{-3}$/year (individual) | Public risk acceptance |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** The FAR (Fatal Accident Rate) of **1–10 per 100 million man-hours** is a globally accepted industry safety benchmark. Industries exceeding this are considered "high-risk" by international standards.

---

## 1.2 Analytical Techniques in Safety — The Toolbox

**Analytical Techniques** in safety engineering are structured, systematic methods used to dissect complex systems and uncover hidden hazards, failure pathways, and accident precursors. They transform qualitative intuition into quantitative risk assessment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** Analytical techniques are formalized logical and mathematical procedures applied to system safety analysis to identify, classify, and evaluate hazards and their interrelationships within a system.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Medical Diagnosis" View

Think of a complex industrial plant as a human body. Analytical techniques are the **diagnostic tools** a doctor uses:
- **FMEA** is like a blood test — checking individual component health.
- **Fault Tree Analysis** is like a CT scan — tracing root causes from symptoms.
- **HAZOP** is like a neurological exam — checking deviations from normal brain function.
- **MORT** is like a full-body health audit — looking at management, environment, and behavior together.

### Major Analytical Techniques Covered in KTU Module 4

1. **Change Analysis (Management of Change — MOC)**
2. **MORT (Management Oversight and Risk Tree)**
3. **Multi-Events Sequencing**
4. **TOR (Theory of Occurrence Regression / Task-Oriented Review)**

Each technique is examined in detail in the sections that follow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Change Analysis — Managing the Dynamics of Risk

**Change Analysis** (often called **Management of Change — MOC**) is the systematic process of evaluating the safety, health, and environmental implications of any modification — whether to equipment, procedures, materials, personnel, or organizational structure — **before** the change is implemented.

### Why Change Analysis is Critical

In the life of any industrial system, change is inevitable. However, **most major accidents have a "change trigger"** — a modification that was not properly analyzed:

- Bhopal (1984) — Storage of MIC in tanks instead of immediate use (organizational change).
- Texas City Refinery (2005) — Raffinate splitter tower startup procedure alteration.
- Fukushima Daiichi (2011) — Backup power design change assumptions (flood protection change).

### The Change Analysis Logic Flow

1. **Trigger Identification** — What is changing? (Equipment, Process, Procedure, People, Materials)
2. **Hazard Screening** — What new hazards does the change introduce?
3. **Risk Evaluation** — What is the new risk profile?
4. **Control Specification** — What safeguards are required?
5. **Approval Authorization** — Who signs off?
6. **Implementation Monitoring** — Verify the change is executed as designed.
7. **Documentation & Closure** — Update drawings, procedures, training.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Concept — MOC Hierarchy:** A change analysis is **mandatory** for *any* modification affecting process safety information, operating procedures, mechanical integrity, training, emergency response, or management of change procedures themselves.

### Change Analysis Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Variables | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk Score | $R = L \times S \times C$ | $L$=Likelihood, $S$=Severity, $C$=Consequence weight | Initial screening |
| New Risk vs Old Risk | $\Delta R = R_{new} - R_{old}$ | Difference evaluation | Decision go/no-go |
| Change Frequency Factor | $CF = \dfrac{N_{changes}}{T}$ | $N$ = number of changes, $T$ = time | MOC audit metric |
| MOC Compliance Index | $MCI = \dfrac{N_{documented}}{N_{total}}$ | Documented changes / Total changes | Performance KPI |

---

## 2.2 MORT — Management Oversight and Risk Tree

**MORT** is a comprehensive, deductive analytical technique developed by **William Johnson (1973)** of the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission. It is a **hybrid analytical framework** that combines elements of Fault Tree Analysis, Event Tree Analysis, and management system auditing.

### The Philosophical Foundation of MORT

MORT recognizes that **95% of all accidents have their root cause in management system failures**, not in direct technical failures. Therefore, MORT places the **management hierarchy at the top** of its analytical tree, forcing analysts to ask: *"What did management do or fail to do that allowed this accident pathway to develop?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** MORT is a total system safety analysis technique that uses a pre-defined logic tree with approximately 1500 elements arranged in a deductive hierarchy from "undesired outcomes" (the top event) through "system inadequacies" to "specific events," designed to evaluate the adequacy of management controls in accident causation.

### The MORT Three-Layer Architecture

| Layer | Level | Focus | Example Elements |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Top Layer** | Management & Oversight | "What should be done?" | Policies, Standards, Audits, Reviews |
| **Middle Layer** | System Factors | "What was done?" | Design, Procedures, Training, Environment |
| **Bottom Layer** | Specific Events & Lapses | "What actually happened?" | Direct causes, Failures, Violations |

### MORT Key Mathematical Formulas

| Concept | Equation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Total Risk Reduction | $R_{reduced} = R_{initial} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_{control,i})$ | Effectiveness of all controls |
| Barrier Effectiveness | $E_{barrier} = 1 - P_{breach}$ | Probability a control barrier holds |
| MORT Coverage Score | $MCS = \dfrac{\sum_{i} w_i \cdot c_i}{N_{max}}$ | $w_i$=weight, $c_i$=control present |
| Performance Gap | $\Delta P = P_{expected} - P_{actual}$ | Deviation from management standards |

Where $P_{control,i}$ is the probability that the $i$-th control fails to function as designed.

### The 3 Pillars of MORT — SBF, RBF, and OLS

1. **SBF — Special Barrier Functions** (Lapses that allow direct causes to reach the target)
2. **RBF — Reduced Barrier Functions** (Inadequate control allowing accident progression)
3. **OLS — Oversight, Lapses, and System** (Top-level management failures)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** MORT is unique because it is the **only analytical technique** that explicitly integrates human factors, management systems, and engineering barriers into a single deductive tree. Examiners frequently test your understanding of the **SBF-RBF-OLS triad**.

### Real-World Utility of MORT

MORT has been used for:
- **Nuclear Power Plant Safety Reviews** (Original NRC application)
- **Chemical Process Industry Audits** (After Bhopal, 1984)
- **Aviation Safety Investigations** (NASA and FAA)
- **Offshore Oil & Gas Safety Audits** (Post-Piper Alpha, 1988)

---

## 2.3 Multi-Events Sequencing (MES)

**Multi-Events Sequencing** is an analytical technique that focuses on the **temporal and causal chain** of multiple events leading to an accident. It is particularly powerful when an incident is not the result of a single failure but a **sequence of small events** that, when combined, produce a major outcome.

### Core Principle

In complex systems, accidents rarely occur from a single cause. They emerge from a **chain of contributing events** that interact across time. Multi-Events Sequencing provides a formal framework to:

1. Identify each event in the sequence
2. Determine the causal relationships between events
3. Identify which event(s), if blocked, would have prevented the outcome
4. Establish the **critical path** of failure

### The 4-Layer Event Hierarchy

| Layer | Event Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Triggering Event** | Initial deviation from normal | Pressure spike |
| 2 | **Contributing Events** | Conditions that enabled escalation | Sensor calibration overdue |
| 3 | **Aggravating Events** | Worsened the situation | Operator misread gauge |
| 4 | **Terminal Event** | Final undesired outcome | Tank rupture |

### Key Sequencing Formulas

The **probability of the entire sequence** is calculated using the product rule (assuming independence):

$$
P_{sequence} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(E_i)
$$

Where each $P(E_i)$ is the probability of event $i$ occurring.

The **critical path** is identified by finding the sequence with the **highest contribution to risk**:

$$
R_{critical} = \max_{j}\left[\prod_{i \in path_j} P(E_i) \times C_j\right]
$$

Where $C_j$ is the consequence severity of path $j$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked about Multi-Events Sequencing in exams, emphasize the **temporal/causal ordering** and the concept of the **"Swiss Cheese Model"** where multiple barrier failures must align for an accident to occur.

---

## 2.4 TOR — Theory of Occurrence Regression (Task-Oriented Review)

**TOR (Theory of Occurrence Regression)** is an analytical technique that traces the **regression of contributing factors** backward from an accident to identify the **deepest system-level and management-level root causes**. The term is also used in some KTU syllabi to mean **Task-Oriented Review**, which is a systematic approach to evaluate tasks for hazard potential.

### Dual Interpretation in KTU Context

| Interpretation | Focus | Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Theory of Occurrence Regression** | Retrospective accident analysis | Trace back from outcome to root cause |
| **Task-Oriented Review** | Prospective task analysis | Break task into steps, identify hazards |

### The 6-Step TOR Process

1. **Define the Task/Incident** — Clearly state what occurred.
2. **Decompose into Sub-Tasks/Events** — Break into manageable units.
3. **Identify Hazards at Each Step** — Apply what-if analysis per step.
4. **Determine Control Failures** — What was supposed to prevent this?
5. **Trace Regression to Management** — Push the analysis up to policy level.
6. **Recommend Corrective Actions** — At task, engineering, and management levels.

### TOR Risk Score Formula

$$
R_{TOR} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (L_i \times S_i \times D_i)
$$

Where:
- $L_i$ = Likelihood of hazard $i$ (1–5 scale)
- $S_i$ = Severity of consequence $i$ (1–5 scale)
- $D_i$ = Detection difficulty of hazard $i$ (1–5 scale)
- $n$ = Total number of hazards identified

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The TOR risk score above $R_{TOR} > 50$ is typically classified as **"Unacceptable Risk"** and requires immediate corrective action. This is a frequently tested threshold in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: MORT Risk Reduction Formula

We start from the fundamental principle that risk is the product of **probability** and **consequence**:

$$
R_{initial} = P_{accident} \times C
$$

When a single control barrier is introduced, the new probability becomes:

$$
P_{new} = P_{accident} \times P_{control,fail}
$$

Where $P_{control,fail}$ is the probability the control fails.

For $n$ independent control barriers in series (each must function for the system to be safe):

$$
P_{system\ fail} = P_{accident} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{control,i,fail}
$$

Therefore, the **residual risk** is:

$$
R_{residual} = C \times P_{accident} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{control,i,fail}
$$

Dividing by initial risk to get the **risk reduction factor (RRF)**:

$$
\text{Step 1: } RRF = \dfrac{R_{residual}}{R_{initial}}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: } RRF = \dfrac{C \times P_{accident} \times \prod P_{control,i,fail}}{P_{accident} \times C}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: } RRF = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{control,i,fail}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{residual} = R_{initial} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{control,i,fail}}
$$

**Engineering Utility:** This formula allows safety engineers to quantify the cumulative risk reduction achieved by stacking multiple safety controls — a principle known as the **"Defense-in-Depth"** methodology used in nuclear, chemical, and aerospace engineering.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Multi-Events Sequence Probability

Consider an industrial accident with **3 sequential events**: $E_1$, $E_2$, $E_3$.

**Step 1: Define individual event probabilities.**

From historical data:
- $P(E_1) = 0.1$ (Equipment failure probability)
- $P(E_2 \mid E_1) = 0.3$ (Given $E_1$, the conditional probability of $E_2$)
- $P(E_3 \mid E_1 \cap E_2) = 0.5$ (Final cascade probability)

**Step 2: Apply the multiplication rule for joint probability.**

$$
P(E_1 \cap E_2 \cap E_3) = P(E_1) \times P(E_2 \mid E_1) \times P(E_3 \mid E_1 \cap E_2)
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
P(\text{sequence}) = 0.1 \times 0.3 \times 0.5
$$

**Step 4: Compute the result.**

$$
P(\text{sequence}) = 0.015
$$

**Step 5: Express in scientific notation for industrial context.**

$$
\boxed{P(\text{sequence}) = 1.5 \times 10^{-2}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 1.5% probability of the full sequence occurring per operational cycle. To bring this below the $10^{-3}$ safety threshold, control barriers must reduce each event's conditional probability.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: TOR Risk Score with Worked Example

**Scenario:** A chemical transfer task has 4 identified hazards.

| Hazard | $L_i$ | $S_i$ | $D_i$ | $L_i \times S_i \times D_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Static electricity buildup | 4 | 5 | 4 | 80 |
| Hose wear | 2 | 4 | 3 | 24 |
| Operator fatigue | 3 | 3 | 2 | 18 |
| Inadequate bonding | 5 | 5 | 5 | 125 |

**Step 1: Apply the TOR scoring formula to each hazard.**

$$
R_1 = 4 \times 5 \times 4 = 80
$$
$$
R_2 = 2 \times 4 \times 3 = 24
$$
$$
R_3 = 3 \times 3 \times 2 = 18
$$
$$
R_4 = 5 \times 5 \times 5 = 125
$$

**Step 2: Sum all hazard risk scores.**

$$
R_{TOR} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4
$$

$$
R_{TOR} = 80 + 24 + 18 + 125
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{TOR} = 247}
$$

**Step 3: Compare against the KTU threshold.**

Since $R_{TOR} = 247 \gg 50$, the task is classified as **UNACCEPTABLE RISK** and requires immediate engineering controls (e.g., improved bonding, anti-static hoses, fatigue management protocols) before resumption.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Change Analysis | MORT | Multi-Events Sequencing | TOR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Prospective | Retrospective/Mixed | Retrospective | Both |
| **Primary Focus** | Modification control | Management oversight | Temporal event chains | Task hazard regression |
| **Logic** | Inductive screening | Deductive tree | Sequential causal | Regression analysis |
| **Output** | Approval/rejection | Risk control map | Critical event path | Risk-scored task list |
| **Best Used When** | New/modified system | Major accident review | Multi-failure incident | Routine task audit |
| **Data Requirement** | Low–Medium | Medium–High | Medium | Low–Medium |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | Very High | High | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 MORT — Conceptual Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the MORT analytical tree's three-layer architecture, mapping management oversights through system factors to specific events.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[UNDESIRED OUTCOME LOSS EVENT] --> B[OVERSIGHT LAPSES AND SYSTEM OLS]
    A --> C[REDUCED BARRIER FUNCTIONS RBF]
    A --> D[SPECIAL BARRIER FUNCTIONS SBF]
    
    B --> B1[POLICY AND STANDARDS FAILURE]
    B --> B2[AUDIT AND REVIEW FAILURE]
    B --> B3[ACCOUNTABILITY GAPS]
    B --> B4[RESOURCE ALLOCATION FAILURE]
    
    C --> C1[INADEQUATE DESIGN CONTROLS]
    C --> C2[INADEQUATE PROCEDURAL CONTROLS]
    C --> C3[INADEQUATE TRAINING CONTROLS]
    C --> C4[INADEQUATE ENVIRONMENT CONTROLS]
    
    D --> D1[DIRECT CAUSE LAPSE]
    D --> D2[IMMEDIATE TRIGGER EVENT]
    D --> D3[BARRIER PENETRATION POINT]
    
    B1 --> E1[NO WRITTEN SAFETY POLICY]
    B2 --> E2[AUDITS NOT CONDUCTED]
    C1 --> E3[DESIGN DEFICIENCY]
    C3 --> E4[OPERATOR NOT TRAINED]
    D1 --> E5[SPECIFIC HUMAN ERROR]
    D2 --> E6[SPECIFIC EQUIPMENT FAILURE]
    
    style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#f08c00,color:#000000
    style C fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#f08c00,color:#000000
    style D fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#f08c00,color:#000000
    style B1 fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000000
```
